title: InfoChess: A Game of Adversarial Inference and a Laboratory for Quantifiable Information Control
comment: Accepted at Adaptive and Learning Agents Workshop, AAMAS 2026. Project page: https://github.com/murphyka/infochess
summary: We propose InfoChess, a symmetric adversarial game that elevates competitive information acquisition to the primary objective. There is no piece capture, removing material incentives that would otherwise confound the role of information. Instead, pieces are used to alter visibility. Players are scored on their probabilistic inference of the opponent's king location over the duration of the game. To explore the space of strategies for playing InfoChess, we introduce a hierarchy of heuristic agents defined by increasing levels of opponent modeling, and train a reinforcement learning agent that outperforms these baselines. Leveraging the discrete structure of the game, we analyze gameplay through natural information-theoretic characterizations that include belief entropy, oracle cross entropy, and predictive log score under the action-induced observation channel. These measures disentangle epistemic uncertainty, calibration mismatch, and uncertainty induced by adversarial movement. The design of InfoChess renders it a testbed for studying multi-agent inference under partial observability. We release code for the environment and agents, and a public interface to encourage further study.

AI.tldr: InfoChess is a novel symmetric adversarial game where information acquisition is the primary objective, with no piece capture, and players score based on inferring opponent's king location through visibility manipulation.
AI.motivation: To create a testbed for studying multi-agent inference under partial observability by designing a game where competitive information acquisition is the core objective, removing confounding factors like material capture that exist in traditional games.
AI.method: Designed InfoChess with no piece capture, using pieces only to alter visibility. Created heuristic agents with increasing opponent modeling levels, trained a reinforcement learning agent, and analyzed gameplay using information-theoretic measures like belief entropy, oracle cross entropy, and predictive log score.
AI.result: The reinforcement learning agent outperformed heuristic baselines. Gameplay analysis using information-theoretic measures successfully disentangled epistemic uncertainty, calibration mismatch, and uncertainty induced by adversarial movement.
AI.conclusion: InfoChess serves as an effective testbed for studying multi-agent inference under partial observability, with its design enabling clear analysis of information acquisition strategies through natural information-theoretic characterizations.

title: Scalable Algorithms with Provable Optimality Bounds for the Multiple Watchman Route Problem
summary: In this paper, we tackle the Multiple Watchman Route Problem (MWRP), which aims to find a set of paths that M watchmen can follow such that every location on the map can be seen by at least one watchman. First, we propose multiple methods to reduce the state space over which a search needs to be conducted by pruning map areas that are guaranteed to be seen en route to other areas. Next, we introduce MWRP-CP3, an efficient optimal planner that combines these methods with techniques that improve the quality and calculation time of existing heuristics. We present several suboptimal algorithms with bounds on solution quality, including MxWA*, a general variant of weighted A* for makespan problems. We also present anytime variations of our suboptimal algorithms, as well as techniques to improve an existing suboptimal solution by solving multiple decomposed sub-problems. We show that MWRP-CP3 can reduce the search space by more than 95% and runs more than 200x faster than existing optimal algorithms on 2D grid maps. We also show that our suboptimal algorithms solve maps 3x larger than those solvable by MWRP-CP3. See mwrp-cp3.github.io for the open source codebase and video demonstrations.

AI.tldr: MWRP-CP3: An efficient optimal planner for the Multiple Watchman Route Problem that reduces search space by 95% and runs 200x faster than existing optimal algorithms, plus suboptimal algorithms that solve 3x larger maps.
AI.motivation: The Multiple Watchman Route Problem (MWRP) aims to find optimal paths for M watchmen to ensure every location on a map can be seen by at least one watchman, which has practical applications in surveillance, security, and coverage planning.
AI.method: Proposes multiple methods to reduce state space by pruning map areas guaranteed to be seen en route to other areas. Combines these with improved heuristics in MWRP-CP3 optimal planner. Also presents suboptimal algorithms including MxWA* (weighted A* variant for makespan problems), anytime variations, and techniques to improve existing solutions via decomposed sub-problems.
AI.result: MWRP-CP3 reduces search space by more than 95% and runs over 200x faster than existing optimal algorithms on 2D grid maps. Suboptimal algorithms solve maps 3x larger than those solvable by MWRP-CP3.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods significantly advance MWRP solving capabilities, offering both efficient optimal solutions via MWRP-CP3 and scalable suboptimal solutions for larger problems, with open-source implementation available.

title: AstroVLM: Expert Multi-agent Collaborative Reasoning for Astronomical Imaging Quality Diagnosis
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) have been applied to several specific domains and have shown strong problem-solving capabilities. However, astronomical imaging, a quite complex problem involving multidisciplinary knowledge and several subtasks, has not been adequately studied. Due to the complexity of the astronomical imaging process, both world-class astronomical organizations, such as NASA, and expert enthusiasts devote a great deal of time and effort. This is because the processes in astronomical imaging have complex underlying correlations that significantly influence one another, making the quality diagnosis and error localization of astronomical images challenging. To address this problem, we propose AstroVLM, a collaborative multi-agent system for diagnosing the quality of astronomical images. Experiment results show that AstroVLM outperforms all baselines on real-world astronomical imaging quality diagnosis tasks, providing a reference for language models to handle complicated multi-process tasks.

AI.tldr: AstroVLM is a collaborative multi-agent vision-language model system designed for diagnosing astronomical image quality, outperforming existing baselines on real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Astronomical imaging is a complex multidisciplinary problem with many subtasks, involving intricate correlations between processes that make quality diagnosis and error localization challenging. Current vision-language models haven't been adequately applied to this domain despite their strong problem-solving capabilities in other areas.
AI.method: The authors propose AstroVLM, a collaborative multi-agent system specifically designed for diagnosing astronomical image quality. The system leverages multi-agent collaboration to handle the complexity of astronomical imaging processes.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that AstroVLM outperforms all baseline methods on real-world astronomical imaging quality diagnosis tasks.
AI.conclusion: AstroVLM provides an effective approach for applying vision-language models to complex multi-process tasks in astronomical imaging, offering a reference framework for handling similar complicated multidisciplinary problems.

title: Veritas-RPM: Provenance-Guided Multi-Agent False Positive Suppression for Remote Patient Monitoring
summary: We present Veritas-RPM, a provenance-guided multi-agent architecture comprising five processing layers: VeritasAgent (ground-truth assembly), SentinelLayer (anomaly detection), DirectorAgent (specialist routing), six domain Specialist Agents, and MetaSentinelAgent (conflict resolution and final decision). We construct a 98-case synthetic taxonomy of false-positive scenarios derived from documented RPM patterns. Synthetic patient epochs (n = 530) were generated directly from taxonomy parameters and processed through the pipeline. Ground-truth labels are known for all cases. Performance is reported as True Suppression Rate (TSR), False Escalation Rate (FER), and Indeterminate Rate (INDR).

AI.tldr: Veritas-RPM is a multi-agent architecture for remote patient monitoring that uses five processing layers and six specialist agents to detect and suppress false-positive alarms while avoiding false escalations, validated on synthetic patient data.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of false-positive alarms in remote patient monitoring (RPM) systems, which can lead to alert fatigue and compromise patient safety. Current RPM systems often generate excessive false positives that burden healthcare providers.
AI.method: The authors developed Veritas-RPM, a provenance-guided multi-agent architecture with five layers: VeritasAgent (ground-truth assembly), SentinelLayer (anomaly detection), DirectorAgent (specialist routing), six domain Specialist Agents, and MetaSentinelAgent (conflict resolution). They created a 98-case taxonomy of false-positive scenarios from documented RPM patterns and generated 530 synthetic patient epochs for testing.
AI.result: Performance was evaluated using True Suppression Rate (TSR), False Escalation Rate (FER), and Indeterminate Rate (INDR). The system was tested on synthetic patient data where ground-truth labels were known, demonstrating its ability to process complex RPM scenarios through the multi-agent pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Veritas-RPM represents a novel multi-agent approach to addressing false positives in remote patient monitoring, with a structured taxonomy of failure modes and a comprehensive evaluation framework that could improve clinical decision support and reduce alert fatigue in healthcare settings.

title: The Spectral Geometry of Thought: Phase Transitions, Instruction Reversal, Token-Level Dynamics, and Perfect Correctness Prediction in How Transformers Reason
summary: We discover that large language models exhibit \emph{spectral phase transitions} in their hidden activation spaces when engaging in reasoning versus factual recall. Through systematic spectral analysis across \textbf{11 models} spanning \textbf{5 architecture families} (Qwen, Pythia, Phi, Llama, DeepSeek-R1), we identify \textbf{seven} core phenomena: (1)~\textbf{Reasoning Spectral Compression} -- 9/11 models show significantly lower $α$ for reasoning ($p < 0.05$), with larger effects in stronger models; (2)~\textbf{Instruction Tuning Spectral Reversal} -- base models show reasoning $α< $ factual $α$, while instruction-tuned models reverse this relationship; (3)~\textbf{Architecture-Dependent Generation Taxonomy} -- prompt-to-response shifts partition into expansion, compression, and equilibrium regimes; (4)~\textbf{Spectral Scaling Law} -- $α_\text{reasoning} \propto -0.074 \ln N$ across 4 Qwen base models ($R^2 = 0.46$); (5)~\textbf{Token-Level Spectral Cascade} -- per-token alpha tracking reveals local synchronization that decays exponentially with layer distance, and is weaker for reasoning than factual tasks; (6)~\textbf{Reasoning Step Spectral Punctuation} -- phase-transition signatures align with reasoning step boundaries; and (7)~\textbf{Spectral Correctness Prediction} -- spectral $α$ alone achieves AUC $= 1.000$ (Qwen2.5-7B, late layers) and mean AUC $= 0.893$ across 6 models in predicting correctness \emph{before} the final answer is generated. Together, these findings establish a comprehensive \emph{spectral theory of reasoning} in transformers, revealing that the geometry of thought is universal in direction, architecture-specific in dynamics, and predictive of outcome.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit spectral phase transitions in hidden activations during reasoning vs. factual recall, with seven core phenomena revealing universal patterns across architectures that can predict correctness before answers are generated.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental differences in how LLMs process reasoning versus factual recall tasks at the neural activation level, and to establish whether there are universal spectral patterns across different model architectures.
AI.method: Systematic spectral analysis across 11 models spanning 5 architecture families (Qwen, Pythia, Phi, Llama, DeepSeek-R1), examining hidden activation spaces to identify spectral patterns and transitions during different cognitive tasks.
AI.result: Identified seven core spectral phenomena including reasoning spectral compression (9/11 models show lower α for reasoning), instruction tuning spectral reversal, architecture-dependent generation taxonomy, spectral scaling law, token-level spectral cascade, reasoning step spectral punctuation, and spectral correctness prediction with AUC = 1.000 for some models.
AI.conclusion: These findings establish a comprehensive spectral theory of reasoning in transformers, showing that the geometry of thought is universal in direction but architecture-specific in dynamics, and spectral features can predict outcome correctness before final answer generation.

title: DeepER-Med: Advancing Deep Evidence-Based Research in Medicine Through Agentic AI
summary: Trustworthiness and transparency are essential for the clinical adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) in healthcare and biomedical research. Recent deep research systems aim to accelerate evidence-grounded scientific discovery by integrating AI agents with multi-hop information retrieval, reasoning, and synthesis. However, most existing systems lack explicit and inspectable criteria for evidence appraisal, creating a risk of compounding errors and making it difficult for researchers and clinicians to assess the reliability of their outputs. In parallel, current benchmarking approaches rarely evaluate performance on complex, real-world medical questions. Here, we introduce DeepER-Med, a Deep Evidence-based Research framework for Medicine with an agentic AI system. DeepER-Med frames deep medical research as an explicit and inspectable workflow of evidence-based generation, consisting of three modules: research planning, agentic collaboration, and evidence synthesis. To support realistic evaluation, we also present DeepER-MedQA, an evidence-grounded dataset comprising 100 expert-level research questions derived from authentic medical research scenarios and curated by a multidisciplinary panel of 11 biomedical experts. Expert manual evaluation demonstrates that DeepER-Med consistently outperforms widely used production-grade platforms across multiple criteria, including the generation of novel scientific insights. We further demonstrate the practical utility of DeepER-Med through eight real-world clinical cases. Human clinician assessment indicates that DeepER-Med's conclusions align with clinical recommendations in seven cases, highlighting its potential for medical research and decision support.

AI.tldr: DeepER-Med is an agentic AI system for evidence-based medical research that introduces explicit, inspectable workflows and outperforms existing platforms on expert-level medical questions.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems for scientific discovery lack explicit evidence appraisal criteria, risking compounding errors, and existing benchmarks rarely evaluate complex real-world medical questions. There's a need for trustworthy, transparent AI systems in healthcare and biomedical research.
AI.method: DeepER-Med frames medical research as an explicit workflow with three modules: research planning, agentic collaboration, and evidence synthesis. It's evaluated using DeepER-MedQA, a dataset of 100 expert-level questions curated by biomedical experts.
AI.result: DeepER-Med consistently outperforms production-grade platforms across multiple criteria, including generating novel scientific insights. In eight real-world clinical cases, clinician assessment shows its conclusions align with clinical recommendations in seven cases.
AI.conclusion: DeepER-Med demonstrates potential for trustworthy medical research and decision support by providing explicit, inspectable evidence-based workflows that address current limitations in AI systems for healthcare.

title: Preregistered Belief Revision Contracts
summary: Deliberative multi-agent systems allow agents to exchange messages and revise beliefs over time. While this interaction is meant to improve performance, it can also create dangerous conformity effects: agreement, confidence, prestige, or majority size may be treated as if they were evidence, producing high-confidence convergence to false conclusions. To address this, we introduce PBRC (Preregistered Belief Revision Contracts), a protocol-level mechanism that strictly separates open communication from admissible epistemic change. A PBRC contract publicly fixes first-order evidence triggers, admissible revision operators, a priority rule, and a fallback policy. A non-fallback step is accepted only when it cites a preregistered trigger and provides a nonempty witness set of externally validated evidence tokens. This ensures that every substantive belief change is both enforceable by a router and auditable after the fact. In this paper, (a) we prove that under evidential contracts with conservative fallback, social-only rounds cannot increase confidence and cannot generate purely conformity-driven wrong-but-sure cascades. (b) We show that auditable trigger protocols admit evidential PBRC normal forms that preserve belief trajectories and canonicalized audit traces. (c) We demonstrate that sound enforcement yields epistemic accountability: any change of top hypothesis is attributable to a concrete validated witness set. For token-invariant contracts, (d) we prove that enforced trajectories depend only on token-exposure traces; under flooding dissemination, these traces are characterized exactly by truncated reachability, giving tight diameter bounds for universal evidence closure. Finally, we introduce a companion contractual dynamic doxastic logic to specify trace invariants, and provide simulations illustrating cascade suppression, auditability, and robustness-liveness trade-offs.

AI.tldr: PBRC protocol prevents dangerous conformity effects in multi-agent systems by enforcing evidence-based belief changes through preregistered contracts, ensuring auditability and accountability.
AI.motivation: Deliberative multi-agent systems can create dangerous conformity effects where agents treat social factors (agreement, confidence, prestige, majority) as evidence, leading to high-confidence convergence to false conclusions.
AI.method: Introduces PBRC (Preregistered Belief Revision Contracts) - a protocol-level mechanism separating open communication from admissible epistemic change. Contracts fix evidence triggers, revision operators, priority rules, fallback policies, requiring non-fallback steps to cite preregistered triggers with validated evidence tokens.
AI.result: Proves that under evidential contracts with conservative fallback, social-only rounds cannot increase confidence or generate conformity-driven wrong cascades. Shows auditable trigger protocols admit evidential PBRC normal forms preserving belief trajectories and audit traces. Demonstrates sound enforcement yields epistemic accountability. For token-invariant contracts, proves enforced trajectories depend only on token-exposure traces.
AI.conclusion: PBRC provides a formal framework for evidence-based belief revision in multi-agent systems that prevents conformity-driven errors while maintaining auditability and accountability, with accompanying dynamic doxastic logic for trace invariants.

title: Aletheia: Gradient-Guided Layer Selection for Efficient LoRA Fine-Tuning Across Architectures
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, 2 frozen evidence campaigns, 81 experiment rows across 14 successful models and 8 architecture families, plus one documented failed Pythia/GPT-NeoX attempt
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become the dominant parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for large language models, yet standard practice applies LoRA adapters uniformly to all transformer layers regardless of their relevance to the downstream task. We introduce Aletheia, a gradient-guided layer selection method that identifies the most task-relevant layers via a lightweight gradient probe and applies LoRA adapters only to those layers with asymmetric rank allocation. Across 81 experiment rows covering 14 successful models from 8 architecture families (0.5B-72B parameters, including dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures), with one additional documented failed Pythia/GPT-NeoX attempt in Campaign 2, Aletheia achieves a 15-28% training speedup (mean 23.1%, p < 0.001) with bounded extra forgetting and broadly matched downstream behavior on the evaluated MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval benchmark pack. Across the tested families and scales, Campaign 1 shows a 100% per-model speed win rate and Campaign 2 shows broadly preserved downstream behavior within a bounded-degradation framing. Together these results support a practical model-economics claim: intelligent layer selection can make LoRA fine-tuning materially more efficient without introducing major downstream damage on the evaluated set.

AI.tldr: Aletheia is a gradient-guided layer selection method for LoRA fine-tuning that identifies task-relevant layers and applies LoRA adapters only to those layers with asymmetric rank allocation, achieving 15-28% training speedup without major downstream performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA practice applies adapters uniformly to all transformer layers regardless of their relevance to the downstream task, which may be inefficient since not all layers contribute equally to task learning.
AI.method: Aletheia uses a lightweight gradient probe to identify the most task-relevant layers, then applies LoRA adapters only to those selected layers with asymmetric rank allocation (different rank allocations per layer).
AI.result: Across 81 experiment rows covering 14 models from 8 architecture families (0.5B-72B parameters), Aletheia achieves 15-28% training speedup (mean 23.1%, p<0.001) with bounded extra forgetting and broadly matched downstream performance on MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent layer selection can make LoRA fine-tuning materially more efficient without introducing major downstream damage, supporting a practical model-economics claim for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

title: GIST: Multimodal Knowledge Extraction and Spatial Grounding via Intelligent Semantic Topology
summary: Navigating complex, densely packed environments like retail stores, warehouses, and hospitals poses a significant spatial grounding challenge for humans and embodied AI. In these spaces, dense visual features quickly become stale given the quasi-static nature of items, and long-tail semantic distributions challenge traditional computer vision. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) help assistive systems navigate semantically-rich spaces, they still struggle with spatial grounding in cluttered environments. We present GIST (Grounded Intelligent Semantic Topology), a multimodal knowledge extraction pipeline that transforms a consumer-grade mobile point cloud into a semantically annotated navigation topology. Our architecture distills the scene into a 2D occupancy map, extracts its topological layout, and overlays a lightweight semantic layer via intelligent keyframe and semantic selection. We demonstrate the versatility of this structured spatial knowledge through critical downstream Human-AI interaction tasks: (1) an intent-driven Semantic Search engine that actively infers categorical alternatives and zones when exact matches fail; (2) a one-shot Semantic Localizer achieving a 1.04 m top-5 mean translation error; (3) a Zone Classification module that segments the walkable floor plan into high-level semantic regions; and (4) a Visually-Grounded Instruction Generator that synthesizes optimal paths into egocentric, landmark-rich natural language routing. In multi-criteria LLM evaluations, GIST outperforms sequence-based instruction generation baselines. Finally, an in-situ formative evaluation (N=5) yields an 80% navigation success rate relying solely on verbal cues, validating the system's capacity for universal design.

AI.tldr: GIST transforms mobile point clouds into semantically annotated navigation topologies for improved spatial grounding in cluttered environments, enabling downstream navigation assistance tasks.
AI.motivation: Dense, cluttered environments like retail stores and warehouses pose spatial grounding challenges for humans and AI due to stale visual features and long-tail semantic distributions, requiring better semantic-spatial representations.
AI.method: A multimodal pipeline that converts mobile point clouds into 2D occupancy maps, extracts topological layouts, and overlays lightweight semantic layers via intelligent keyframe and semantic selection.
AI.result: Developed four downstream applications: semantic search engine (with categorical inference), semantic localizer (1.04m top-5 mean error), zone classification, and instruction generator (outperforms baselines in LLM evaluations). Formative evaluation shows 80% navigation success rate.
AI.conclusion: GIST provides structured spatial knowledge that enables effective human-AI interaction in complex environments, with potential for universal design applications requiring minimal visual cues.

title: Experience Compression Spectrum: Unifying Memory, Skills, and Rules in LLM Agents
summary: As LLM agents scale to long-horizon, multi-session deployments, efficiently managing accumulated experience becomes a critical bottleneck. Agent memory systems and agent skill discovery both address this challenge -- extracting reusable knowledge from interaction traces -- yet a citation analysis of 1,136 references across 22 primary papers reveals a cross-community citation rate below 1%. We propose the \emph{Experience Compression Spectrum}, a unifying framework that positions memory, skills, and rules as points along a single axis of increasing compression (5--20$\times$ for episodic memory, 50--500$\times$ for procedural skills, 1,000$\times$+ for declarative rules), directly reducing context consumption, retrieval latency, and compute overhead. Mapping 20+ systems onto this spectrum reveals that every system operates at a fixed, predetermined compression level -- none supports adaptive cross-level compression, a gap we term the \emph{missing diagonal}. We further show that specialization alone is insufficient -- both communities independently solve shared sub-problems without exchanging solutions -- that evaluation methods are tightly coupled to compression levels, that transferability increases with compression at the cost of specificity, and that knowledge lifecycle management remains largely neglected. We articulate open problems and design principles for scalable, full-spectrum agent learning systems.

AI.tldr: LLM agents need to manage accumulated experience efficiently; the paper introduces the Experience Compression Spectrum framework unifying memory, skills, and rules along a compression axis, revealing a 'missing diagonal' of adaptive compression and gaps in cross-community knowledge sharing.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents scale to long-horizon, multi-session deployments, efficiently managing accumulated experience becomes a critical bottleneck. There's a significant disconnect between agent memory systems and agent skill discovery communities (cross-community citation rate below 1%), despite both addressing the same core challenge of extracting reusable knowledge from interaction traces.
AI.method: The paper conducts a citation analysis of 1,136 references across 22 primary papers to reveal the community disconnect. It introduces the Experience Compression Spectrum framework that positions memory, skills, and rules as points along a single axis of increasing compression (5-20× for episodic memory, 50-500× for procedural skills, 1,000×+ for declarative rules). The authors map 20+ existing systems onto this spectrum and analyze the gaps.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that every existing system operates at a fixed, predetermined compression level with none supporting adaptive cross-level compression (the 'missing diagonal'). It shows both communities independently solve shared sub-problems without exchanging solutions, evaluation methods are tightly coupled to compression levels, transferability increases with compression at the cost of specificity, and knowledge lifecycle management remains largely neglected.
AI.conclusion: The paper articulates open problems and design principles for scalable, full-spectrum agent learning systems, advocating for adaptive compression approaches that can move knowledge across the compression spectrum and better integration between memory, skills, and rule systems.

title: Sequential KV Cache Compression via Probabilistic Language Tries: Beyond the Per-Vector Shannon Limit
summary: Recent work on KV cache quantization, culminating in TurboQuant, has approached the Shannon entropy limit for per-vector compression of transformer key-value caches. We observe that this limit applies to a strictly weaker problem than the one that actually matters: compressing the KV cache as a sequence. The tokens stored in a KV cache are not arbitrary floating-point data -- they are samples from the exact formal language the model was trained on, and the model is by construction a near-optimal predictor of that language. We introduce sequential KV compression, a two-layer architecture that exploits this structure. The first layer, probabilistic prefix deduplication, identifies semantically equivalent shared prefixes across sessions using the trie metric d_T(s, s') = -log_2 P_M(s ^ s') from Probabilistic Language Tries (PLTs). The second layer, predictive delta coding, stores only the residual of each new KV vector from the model's own prediction of it, achieving a per-token entropy bound of H(KV_{i+1} | KV_{<=i}) <= H(token_{i+1} | token_{<=i}). We prove that at typical language model perplexity -- approximately 10-20 for fluent English text -- this bound is 3.3-4.3 bits on average per token position, compared to TurboQuant's 3 bits per vector component (with typical attention heads having 64-128 components). The theoretical compression ratio over TurboQuant is approximately 914,000x at the Shannon limit. Even at 1000x above the entropy floor -- a deliberately pessimistic worst-case overhead, two orders of magnitude above the 2-5x typical of practical source coders -- the ratio remains approximately 914x over TurboQuant, with compression improving rather than degrading as context length grows. The two layers are orthogonal and compose with existing per-vector quantization methods including TurboQuant.

AI.tldr: A new sequential KV compression method that achieves ~914,000x better compression than state-of-the-art TurboQuant by treating KV cache as language samples rather than arbitrary data.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache quantization methods like TurboQuant approach the Shannon entropy limit for per-vector compression, but this is a weaker problem than actually needed. The tokens in KV cache are samples from a formal language the model was trained on, and the model itself is a near-optimal predictor of that language. Current approaches don't exploit this structure.
AI.method: Two-layer architecture: 1) Probabilistic prefix deduplication using trie metric d_T(s, s') = -log_2 P_M(s ^ s') from Probabilistic Language Tries to identify semantically equivalent shared prefixes across sessions; 2) Predictive delta coding that stores only the residual of each new KV vector from the model's own prediction of it, achieving a per-token entropy bound of H(KV_{i+1} | KV_{<=i}) <= H(token_{i+1} | token_{<=i}).
AI.result: At typical language model perplexity (10-20 for fluent English), the bound is 3.3-4.3 bits per token position vs TurboQuant's 3 bits per vector component. Theoretical compression ratio over TurboQuant is ~914,000x at Shannon limit. Even at 1000x above entropy floor (pessimistic worst-case), ratio remains ~914x over TurboQuant. Compression improves as context length grows, and the two layers are orthogonal and compose with existing per-vector quantization.
AI.conclusion: Sequential KV compression represents a fundamental advance by exploiting the fact that KV cache contains language samples, achieving dramatically better compression than previous approaches that treated them as arbitrary floating-point data. The method theoretically outperforms TurboQuant by several orders of magnitude while maintaining composability with existing techniques.

title: Bureaucratic Silences: What the Canadian AI Register Reveals, Omits, and Obscures
summary: In November 2025, the Government of Canada operationalized its commitment to transparency by releasing its first Federal AI Register. In this paper, we argue that such registers are not neutral mirrors of government activity, but active instruments of ontological design that configure the boundaries of accountability. We analyzed the Register's complete dataset of 409 systems using the Algorithmic Decision-Making Adapted for the Public Sector (ADMAPS) framework, combining quantitative mapping with deductive qualitative coding. Our findings reveal a sharp divergence between the rhetoric of "sovereign AI" and the reality of bureaucratic practice: while 86\% of systems are deployed internally for efficiency, the Register systematically obscures the human discretion, training, and uncertainty management required to operate them. By privileging technical descriptions over sociotechnical context, the Register constructs an ontology of AI as "reliable tooling" rather than "contestable decision-making." We conclude that without a shift in design, such transparency artifacts risk automating accountability into a performative compliance exercise, offering visibility without contestability.

AI.tldr: Canada's AI Register is critiqued as performing transparency while obscuring the sociotechnical realities of algorithmic systems, potentially automating accountability into mere compliance.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by Canada's release of its Federal AI Register, which claims to enhance transparency. The authors question whether such registers actually achieve meaningful accountability or merely perform transparency while obscuring the sociotechnical realities of how AI systems function in government.
AI.method: The researchers analyzed the complete dataset of 409 systems in Canada's Federal AI Register using the Algorithmic Decision-Making Adapted for the Public Sector (ADMAPS) framework, combining quantitative mapping with deductive qualitative coding.
AI.result: The analysis revealed a sharp divergence between "sovereign AI" rhetoric and bureaucratic practice: 86% of systems are deployed internally for efficiency, and the Register systematically obscures human discretion, training needs, and uncertainty management required to operate AI systems. The Register constructs AI as "reliable tooling" rather than "contestable decision-making."
AI.conclusion: Without design changes, transparency artifacts like AI registers risk automating accountability into performative compliance exercises, offering visibility without meaningful contestability. They function as ontological design instruments that shape how AI is understood in government rather than neutral mirrors of activity.

title: Weak-Link Optimization for Multi-Agent Reasoning and Collaboration
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to CAAI Transactions on Intelligence Technology
summary: LLM-driven multi-agent frameworks address complex reasoning tasks through multi-role collaboration. However, existing approaches often suffer from reasoning instability, where individual agent errors are amplified through collaboration, undermining overall performance. Current research mainly focuses on enhancing high-capability agents or suppressing unreliable outputs to improve framework effectiveness, while systematic identification and reinforcement of performance-limiting agents receive less attention. To address this gap, we propose WORC, a \underline{w}eak-link \underline{o}ptimization framework for multi-agent \underline{r}easoning and \underline{c}ollaboration, grounded in the weak-link principle. WORC follows a two-stage workflow. In the weak agent localization stage, task features are constructed, and a meta-learning-based weight predictor trained on optimal configurations identified by swarm intelligence algorithms (SIAs) enables zero-shot mapping from these features to agent performance weights, where the agent with the lowest predicted weight is identified as the weak agent. In the weak-link optimization stage, an uncertainty-driven allocation strategy assigns additional reasoning budgets to weak agents, with lower predicted weights leading to larger repeated-sampling quotas to compensate for reliability deficiencies. Experimental results show that WORC achieves an average accuracy of 82.2\% on reasoning benchmarks while improving framework stability and cross-architecture generalization, suggesting that compensating for weak links, rather than reinforcing strengths alone, enhances the robustness of multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: WORC is a weak-link optimization framework for multi-agent reasoning systems that identifies and compensates for performance-limiting agents using meta-learning and uncertainty-driven resource allocation to improve overall system stability.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent frameworks suffer from reasoning instability where individual agent errors get amplified through collaboration. Current approaches focus on enhancing strong agents or suppressing unreliable outputs, but systematic identification and reinforcement of weak agents receives insufficient attention.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Weak agent localization: construct task features and use meta-learning-based weight predictor trained on optimal configurations from swarm intelligence algorithms to map features to performance weights, identifying the weakest agent. 2) Weak-link optimization: uncertainty-driven allocation strategy assigns additional reasoning budgets to weak agents, with larger repeated-sampling quotas for agents with lower predicted weights.
AI.result: Achieved average accuracy of 82.2% on reasoning benchmarks while improving framework stability and cross-architecture generalization. The approach demonstrates that compensating for weak links enhances robustness better than reinforcing strengths alone.
AI.conclusion: Systematically identifying and optimizing weak links in multi-agent systems through targeted resource allocation significantly improves overall performance and stability, providing a more effective approach than focusing only on strengthening high-capability agents.

title: Mapping High-Performance Regions in Battery Scheduling across Data Uncertainty, Battery Design, and Planning Horizons
summary: This study presents a triadic analysis of energy storage operation under multi-stage model predictive control, investigating the interplay between data characteristics, forecast uncertainty, planning horizon, and battery c-rate. Synthetic datasets are generated to systematically explore variations in data profiles and uncertainty, enabling parametrization and the construction of relationships that map these characteristics to optimal horizon length. Results reveal the presence of an effective horizon, defined as the look-ahead length beyond which additional forecast information provides limited operational benefit. Accounting for this horizon can reduce computational costs while maintaining optimal performance. The study provides optimal horizon lengths across a broad range of combinations of battery types, uncertainty levels, and data profiles, offering practical guidance for industrial storage operation. It also quantifies revenue losses due to forecast uncertainty, showing that errors can impact performance even for fast batteries. Finally, the framework lays the groundwork for future machine learning approaches that map dataset parametrization to optimal horizons, supporting continuous optimization in industrial settings without heavy computation.

AI.tldr: This study uses triadic analysis to determine optimal planning horizons for energy storage operation under multi-stage MPC, identifying effective horizons that balance computational cost and performance while quantifying forecast uncertainty impacts.
AI.motivation: Industrial energy storage operation needs to balance performance optimization with computational efficiency, but the optimal planning horizon depends on complex interactions between data characteristics, forecast uncertainty, battery properties, and control strategies.
AI.method: Triadic analysis of energy storage operation under multi-stage model predictive control using synthetic datasets to systematically explore variations in data profiles and uncertainty, enabling parametrization and construction of relationships mapping these characteristics to optimal horizon length.
AI.result: Revealed the presence of an "effective horizon" beyond which additional forecast information provides limited operational benefit, identified optimal horizon lengths across battery types/uncertainty/data profiles, quantified revenue losses due to forecast uncertainty, and showed that errors impact even fast batteries.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for effective horizons can reduce computational costs while maintaining optimal performance, providing practical guidance for industrial storage operation and laying groundwork for future ML approaches to map dataset parametrization to optimal horizons without heavy computation.

title: LACE: Lattice Attention for Cross-thread Exploration
summary: Current large language models reason in isolation. Although it is common to sample multiple reasoning paths in parallel, these trajectories do not interact, and often fail in the same redundant ways. We introduce LACE, a framework that transforms reasoning from a collection of independent trials into a coordinated, parallel process. By repurposing the model architecture to enable cross-thread attention, LACE allows concurrent reasoning paths to share intermediate insights and correct one another during inference. A central challenge is the absence of natural training data that exhibits such collaborative behavior. We address this gap with a synthetic data pipeline that explicitly teaches models to communicate and error-correct across threads. Experiments show that this unified exploration substantially outperforms standard parallel search, improving reasoning accuracy by over 7 points. Our results suggest that large language models can be more effective when parallel reasoning paths are allowed to interact.

AI.tldr: LACE enables parallel reasoning paths in LLMs to interact and correct each other during inference, improving accuracy by over 7% compared to standard parallel search.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs reason in isolation even when sampling multiple paths in parallel. These independent reasoning trajectories often fail in the same redundant ways because they don't interact or share insights.
AI.method: LACE transforms reasoning into a coordinated parallel process by repurposing model architecture for cross-thread attention, allowing concurrent reasoning paths to share insights and correct each other. A synthetic data pipeline teaches models to communicate and error-correct across threads since natural training data doesn't exhibit such collaborative behavior.
AI.result: Experiments show LACE substantially outperforms standard parallel search, improving reasoning accuracy by over 7 points. The unified exploration approach demonstrates that interactive parallel reasoning is more effective than independent reasoning paths.
AI.conclusion: Large language models can be more effective when parallel reasoning paths are allowed to interact. Enabling collaboration between concurrent reasoning trajectories through architectural modifications and synthetic training represents a promising direction for improving LLM reasoning capabilities.

title: SocialGrid: A Benchmark for Planning and Social Reasoning in Embodied Multi-Agent Systems
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition from text processors to autonomous agents, evaluating their social reasoning in embodied multi-agent settings becomes critical. We introduce SocialGrid, an embodied multi-agent environment inspired by Among Us that evaluates LLM agents on planning, task execution, and social reasoning. Our evaluations reveal that even the strongest open model (GPT-OSS-120B) achieves below 60% accuracy in task completion and planning, with agents getting stuck in repetitive behaviors or failing to navigate basic obstacles. Since poor navigation confounds evaluation of social intelligence, SocialGrid offers an optional Planning Oracle to isolate social reasoning from planning deficits. While planning assistance improves task completion, social reasoning remains a bottleneck: agents fail to detect deception at near-random chance regardless of scale, relying on shallow heuristics rather than accumulating behavioral evidence. SocialGrid provides automatic failure analysis and fine-grained metrics, enabling developers to diagnose and improve their agents. We also establish a competitive leaderboard using Elo ratings from adversarial league play.

AI.tldr: SocialGrid is an embodied multi-agent environment (inspired by Among Us) that evaluates LLM agents' planning, task execution, and social reasoning abilities, revealing significant limitations in both planning and social intelligence even in state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: As LLMs transition from text processors to autonomous agents, there's a critical need to evaluate their social reasoning in embodied multi-agent settings, where agents must navigate complex social dynamics, deception, and cooperative/competitive interactions.
AI.method: Introduced SocialGrid, an embodied multi-agent environment inspired by Among Us, featuring automated failure analysis, fine-grained metrics, an optional Planning Oracle to isolate social reasoning from navigation deficits, and a competitive leaderboard using Elo ratings from adversarial league play.
AI.result: Even the strongest open model (GPT-OSS-120B) achieves below 60% accuracy in task completion and planning, showing agents get stuck in repetitive behaviors or fail basic navigation. Social reasoning is particularly weak: agents fail to detect deception at near-random chance levels regardless of model scale, relying on shallow heuristics rather than accumulating behavioral evidence. Planning assistance improves task completion but social reasoning remains a bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: Social reasoning remains a major bottleneck for LLM agents in embodied multi-agent settings, with current models performing poorly at deception detection and complex social dynamics despite planning assistance. SocialGrid provides a comprehensive evaluation framework to diagnose and improve agents' social intelligence.

title: M3R: Localized Rainfall Nowcasting with Meteorology-Informed MultiModal Attention
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Multimedia and Expo (ICME) 2026
summary: Accurate and timely rainfall nowcasting is crucial for disaster mitigation and water resource management. Despite recent advances in deep learning, precipitation prediction remains challenging due to limitations in effectively leveraging diverse multimedia data sources. We introduce M3R, a Meteorology-informed MultiModal attention-based architecture for direct Rainfall prediction that synergistically combines visual NEXRAD radar imagery with numerical Personal Weather Station (PWS) measurements, using a comprehensive pipeline for temporal alignment of heterogeneous meteorological data. With specialized multimodal attention mechanisms, M3R novelly leverages weather station time series as queries to selectively attend to spatial radar features, enabling focused extraction of precipitation signatures. Experimental results for three spatial areas of 100 km * 100 km centered at NEXRAD radar stations demonstrate that M3R outperforms existing approaches, achieving substantial improvements in accuracy, efficiency, and precipitation detection capabilities. Our work establishes new benchmarks for multimedia-based precipitation nowcasting and provides practical tools for operational weather prediction systems. The source code is available at https://github.com/Sanjeev97/M3Rain

AI.tldr: M3R: A meteorology-informed multimodal attention architecture that combines radar imagery with weather station data for improved rainfall nowcasting, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Accurate and timely rainfall nowcasting is crucial for disaster mitigation and water resource management, but current deep learning approaches have limitations in effectively leveraging diverse multimedia data sources for precipitation prediction.
AI.method: Introduces M3R, a Meteorology-informed MultiModal attention-based architecture that synergistically combines visual NEXRAD radar imagery with numerical Personal Weather Station (PWS) measurements. Uses a comprehensive pipeline for temporal alignment of heterogeneous meteorological data and specialized multimodal attention mechanisms where weather station time series serve as queries to selectively attend to spatial radar features.
AI.result: Experimental results for three spatial areas of 100 km * 100 km centered at NEXRAD radar stations demonstrate that M3R outperforms existing approaches, achieving substantial improvements in accuracy, efficiency, and precipitation detection capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes new benchmarks for multimedia-based precipitation nowcasting, provides practical tools for operational weather prediction systems, and the source code is publicly available.

title: Lightweight Geometric Adaptation for Training Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: 22 pages, Chenhao Si and Kang An contributed equally to this work. Their authorship order was determined randomly
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) often suffer from slow convergence, training instability, and reduced accuracy on challenging partial differential equations due to the anisotropic and rapidly varying geometry of their loss landscapes. We propose a lightweight curvature-aware optimization framework that augments existing first-order optimizers with an adaptive predictive correction based on secant information. Consecutive gradient differences are used as a cheap proxy for local geometric change, together with a step-normalized secant curvature indicator to control the correction strength. The framework is plug-and-play, computationally efficient, and broadly compatible with existing optimizers, without explicitly forming second-order matrices. Experiments on diverse PDE benchmarks show consistent improvements in convergence speed, training stability, and solution accuracy over standard optimizers and strong baselines, including on the high-dimensional heat equation, Gray--Scott system, Belousov--Zhabotinsky system, and 2D Kuramoto--Sivashinsky system.

AI.tldr: A lightweight curvature-aware optimization framework improves PINN training by using gradient differences as a cheap proxy for local geometric changes, enhancing convergence speed and stability without explicit second-order matrices.
AI.motivation: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) suffer from slow convergence, training instability, and reduced accuracy on challenging PDEs due to anisotropic and rapidly varying loss landscapes.
AI.method: Proposes a curvature-aware optimization framework that augments existing first-order optimizers with adaptive predictive correction using consecutive gradient differences as a cheap proxy for local geometric change, with step-normalized secant curvature indicator to control correction strength.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse PDE benchmarks show consistent improvements in convergence speed, training stability, and solution accuracy over standard optimizers and strong baselines, including on high-dimensional heat equation, Gray-Scott system, Belousov-Zhabotinsky system, and 2D Kuramoto-Sivashinsky system.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight, plug-and-play optimization framework successfully addresses PINN training challenges by incorporating curvature awareness without computational overhead of explicit second-order methods, making it broadly compatible with existing optimizers.

title: Subliminal Transfer of Unsafe Behaviors in AI Agent Distillation
summary: Recent work on subliminal learning demonstrates that language models can transmit semantic traits through data that is semantically unrelated to those traits. However, it remains unclear whether behavioral traits can transfer in agentic systems, where policies are learned from trajectories rather than static text. In this work, we provide the first empirical evidence that unsafe agent behaviors can transfer subliminally through model distillation across two complementary experimental settings. In our primary setting, we construct a teacher agent exhibiting a strong deletion bias, a tendency to perform destructive file-system actions via an API-style tool interface, and distill it into a student using only trajectories from ostensibly safe tasks, with all explicit deletion keywords rigorously filtered. In our secondary setting, we replicate the threat model in a native Bash environment, replacing API tool calls with shell commands and operationalizing the bias as a preference for issuing chmod as the first permission-related command over semantically equivalent alternatives such as chown or setfacl. Despite full keyword sanitation in both settings, students inherit measurable behavioral biases. In the API setting the student's deletion rate reaches 100% (versus a 5% baseline) under homogeneous distillation; in the Bash setting the student's chmod-first rate reaches 30%-55% (versus a 0%-10% baseline), with the strongest transfer observed in large-to-small distillation. Our results demonstrate that explicit data sanitation is an insufficient defense, and behavioral biases are encoded implicitly in trajectory dynamics regardless of the tool interface.

AI.tldr: Behavioral biases can transfer subliminally through model distillation between agents, even when all explicit unsafe keywords are filtered out from training data.
AI.motivation: While previous work shows language models can transmit semantic traits through unrelated data, it's unclear if behavioral traits can transfer in agentic systems where policies are learned from trajectories rather than static text.
AI.method: Two complementary experimental settings: 1) Primary setting with teacher agent exhibiting strong deletion bias, distilled into student using only safe-task trajectories with all deletion keywords filtered. 2) Secondary setting in native Bash environment with teacher preferring chmod as first permission-related command over alternatives, with keyword sanitation.
AI.result: Despite full keyword sanitation, students inherited measurable behavioral biases: In API setting, student's deletion rate reached 100% vs 5% baseline; in Bash setting, student's chmod-first rate reached 30%-55% vs 0%-10% baseline, with strongest transfer in large-to-small distillation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit data sanitation is insufficient defense; behavioral biases are encoded implicitly in trajectory dynamics regardless of tool interface, showing subliminal transfer of unsafe behaviors is possible in agentic systems.

title: Python library supporting Discrete Variational Formulations and training solutions with Collocation-based Robust Variational Physics Informed Neural Networks (DVF-CRVPINN)
comment: Python library, Robust Variational Physics-Informed Neural Networks, Collocation Methods, Robust loss, Stokes Equations, Laplace problem
summary: We explore the possibility of solving Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) using discrete weak formulations. We propose a programming environment for defining a discrete computational domain, introducing discrete functions defined over a set of points, constructing discrete inner products, and introducing discrete weak formulations employing Kronecker delta test functions. Building on this setup, we propose a discrete neural network representation, training the solution function defined over a discrete set of points and employing discrete finite difference derivatives in the automatic differentiation procedures. As a challenging computational model example, we focus on Stokes equations in two-dimensions, defined over a discrete set of points. We train the solution using the discrete weak residual and the Adamax algorithm with discrete automatic differentiation of the discrete gradients. Despite introducing the python environment, we also provide a rigorous mathematical formulation based on discrete weak formulations, proving the well-posedness and robustness of the loss function. The solution of the discrete weak formulations is based on neural network training employing a robust loss function that is related to the true error. In this way, we have a robust control of the numerical error during the training of the neural networks. Besides the Stokes formulation, we also explain the functionality of the proposed library using the Laplace problem formulation.

AI.tldr: A discrete weak formulation approach for solving PDEs using neural networks with discrete computational domains, Kronecker delta test functions, and discrete automatic differentiation, applied to Stokes and Laplace equations with robust error control.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust framework for solving Partial Differential Equations using discrete weak formulations that provides better control over numerical error during neural network training, particularly for challenging problems like Stokes equations.
AI.method: Proposes a programming environment for discrete computational domains with discrete functions defined over point sets, discrete inner products, and discrete weak formulations using Kronecker delta test functions. Uses discrete neural network representations trained over discrete point sets with discrete finite difference derivatives in automatic differentiation procedures.
AI.result: Developed a discrete weak formulation framework with rigorous mathematical formulation proving well-posedness and robustness of the loss function. Applied successfully to Stokes equations in 2D and Laplace problems, demonstrating robust control of numerical error during neural network training using Adamax algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The discrete weak formulation approach provides a robust method for solving PDEs with neural networks, offering mathematical rigor and practical error control that bridges discrete computational domains with continuous PDE problems through carefully designed discrete weak formulations.

title: Bilevel Optimization of Agent Skills via Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: Agent \texttt{skills} are structured collections of instructions, tools, and supporting resources that help large language model (LLM) agents perform particular classes of tasks. Empirical evidence shows that the design of \texttt{skills} can materially affect agent task performance, yet systematically optimizing \texttt{skills} remains challenging. Since a \texttt{skill} comprises instructions, tools, and supporting resources in a structured way, optimizing it requires jointly determining both the structure of these components and the content each component contains. This gives rise to a complex decision space with strong interdependence across structure and components. We therefore represent these two coupled decisions as \texttt{skill} structure and component content, and formulate \texttt{skill} optimization as a bilevel optimization problem. We propose a bilevel optimization framework in which an outer loop employs Monte Carlo Tree Search to determine the \texttt{skill} structure, while an inner loop refines the component content within the structure selected by the outer loop. In both loops, we employ LLMs to assist the optimization procedure. We evaluate the proposed framework on an open-source Operations Research Question Answering dataset, and the experimental results suggest that the bilevel optimization framework improves the performance of the agents with the optimized \texttt{skill}.

AI.tldr: Bilevel optimization framework using Monte Carlo Tree Search and LLMs to jointly optimize skill structure and component content for improved LLM agent performance
AI.motivation: Agent skills (structured collections of instructions, tools, and resources) significantly affect LLM agent performance, but systematic optimization is challenging due to the complex decision space with strong interdependence between structure and component content.
AI.method: Formulate skill optimization as a bilevel optimization problem: outer loop uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to determine skill structure, inner loop refines component content within selected structure. Both loops use LLMs to assist the optimization procedure.
AI.result: Evaluation on an open-source Operations Research Question Answering dataset shows that the bilevel optimization framework improves agent performance with optimized skills.
AI.conclusion: The proposed bilevel optimization framework effectively addresses the challenge of jointly optimizing skill structure and component content, leading to better performing LLM agents through systematic skill optimization.

title: Hallucination as Trajectory Commitment: Causal Evidence for Asymmetric Attractor Dynamics in Transformer Generation
comment: 21 pages, 12 figures, 8 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/akarlaraytu/trajectory-commitment
summary: We present causal evidence that hallucination in autoregressive language models is an early trajectory commitment governed by asymmetric attractor dynamics. Using same-prompt bifurcation, in which we repeatedly sample identical inputs to observe spontaneous divergence, we isolate trajectory dynamics from prompt-level confounds. On Qwen2.5-1.5B across 61 prompts spanning six categories, 27 prompts (44.3%) bifurcate with factual and hallucinated trajectories diverging at the first generated token (KL = 0 at step 0, KL > 1.0 at step 1). Activation patching across 28 layers reveals a pronounced causal asymmetry: injecting a hallucinated activation into a correct trajectory corrupts output in 87.5% of trials (layer 20), while the reverse recovers only 33.3% (layer 24); both exceed the 10.4% baseline (p = 0.025) and 12.5% random-patch control. Window patching shows correction requires sustained multi-step intervention, whereas corruption needs only a single perturbation. Probing the prompt encoding itself, step-0 residual states predict per-prompt hallucination rate at Pearson r = 0.776 at layer 15 (p < 0.001 against a 1000-permutation null); unsupervised clustering identifies five regime-like groups (eta^2 = 0.55) whose saddle-adjacent cluster concentrates 12 of the 13 bifurcating false-premise prompts, indicating that the basin structure is organized around regime commitments fixed at prompt encoding. These findings characterize hallucination as a locally stable attractor basin: entry is probabilistic and rapid, exit demands coordinated intervention across layers and steps, and the relevant basins are selected by clusterable regimes already discernible at step 0.

AI.tldr: Hallucination in language models is driven by early asymmetric attractor dynamics where trajectory commitment happens at first token generation, making recovery difficult without coordinated intervention.
AI.motivation: To understand the causal mechanisms behind hallucination in autoregressive language models, particularly how trajectory divergence occurs and why hallucinated content is difficult to correct once generated.
AI.method: Used same-prompt bifurcation to isolate trajectory dynamics, activation patching across layers to test causal asymmetry, window patching to test intervention requirements, and probing prompt encoding states to predict hallucination rates and identify regime clusters.
AI.result: 44.3% of prompts showed immediate divergence at first token; 87.5% corruption rate from hallucinated activations vs 33.3% recovery rate; step-0 residual states predicted hallucination rate with r=0.776; identified five regime-like groups including a saddle-adjacent cluster concentrating false-premise prompts.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination operates as a locally stable attractor basin: entry is probabilistic and rapid (at step 0), exit requires coordinated multi-step intervention, and the basin structure is organized around regime commitments fixed during prompt encoding.

title: The World Leaks the Future: Harness Evolution for Future Prediction Agents
summary: Many consequential decisions must be made before the relevant outcome is known. Such problems are commonly framed as \emph{future prediction}, where an LLM agent must form a prediction for an unresolved question using only the public information available at the prediction time. The setting is difficult because public evidence evolves while useful supervision arrives only after the question is resolved, so most existing approaches still improve mainly from final outcomes. Yet final outcomes are too coarse to guide earlier factor tracking, evidence gathering and interpretation, or uncertainty handling. When the same unresolved question is revisited over time, temporal contrasts between earlier and later predictions can expose omissions in the earlier prediction process; we call this signal \emph{internal feedback}. We introduce \emph{Milkyway}, a self-evolving agent system that keeps the base model fixed and instead updates a persistent \emph{future prediction harness} for factor tracking, evidence gathering and interpretation, and uncertainty handling. Across repeated predictions on the same unresolved question, \emph{Milkyway} extracts internal feedback and writes reusable guidance back into the harness, so later predictions on that question can improve before the outcome is known. After the question is resolved, the final outcome provides a \emph{retrospective check} before the updated harness is carried forward to subsequent questions. On FutureX and FutureWorld, Milkyway achieves the best overall score among the compared methods, improving FutureX from 44.07 to 60.90 and FutureWorld from 62.22 to 77.96.

AI.tldr: Milkyway is a self-evolving agent system that improves future predictions by extracting internal feedback from temporal contrasts between earlier and later predictions on the same unresolved question, without updating the base model.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to future prediction problems mainly improve from final outcomes, but these are too coarse to guide early-stage processes like factor tracking, evidence gathering, interpretation, and uncertainty handling. There's a need for more granular feedback during the prediction process before outcomes are known.
AI.method: Milkyway maintains a persistent future prediction harness that is updated through internal feedback. The system makes repeated predictions on the same unresolved question over time, extracts feedback from temporal contrasts between earlier and later predictions, and writes reusable guidance back into the harness. The base model remains fixed while the harness evolves.
AI.result: On FutureX and FutureWorld benchmarks, Milkyway achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving FutureX from 44.07 to 60.90 and FutureWorld from 62.22 to 77.96 overall scores.
AI.conclusion: Internal feedback from temporal prediction contrasts provides valuable supervision for improving future predictions before outcomes are known. By evolving a persistent prediction harness rather than the base model, Milkyway enables continuous improvement in factor tracking, evidence handling, and uncertainty management in future prediction tasks.

title: Dispatch-Aware Ragged Attention for Pruned Vision Transformers
summary: Token pruning methods for Vision Transformers (ViTs) promise quadratic reductions in attention FLOPs by dropping uninformative patches. Yet when pruned sequences are executed with state-of-the-art variable-length attention APIs -- including FlashAttention-2's varlen and PyTorch's NestedTensor SDPA-the wall-clock attention latency doesn't scale accordingly. We trace this to a dispatch-overhead bottleneck: at the short, post-pruning sequence lengths typical of ViTs (<=197 tokens), actual matrix arithmetic completes in single-digit microseconds while the host-side dispatch path consumes 60-90 us. We present a lightweight, bidirectional Triton attention kernel whose dispatch floor is 40 us roughly 1.5x lower than FlashAttention-2 varlen-allowing pruning savings to become more visible in wall-clock time. Integrated into a complete pack-attend-unpack pipeline, our system achieves up to 2.24x end-to-end throughput over padded PyTorch SDPA consistently across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS), scales across DeiT-T/S/B, and maintains bit-exact classification predictions with <0.007 max absolute logit difference.

AI.tldr: A new lightweight Triton attention kernel reduces dispatch overhead to make token pruning actually translate to wall-clock speedups in Vision Transformers, achieving up to 2.24x throughput improvement over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current token pruning methods for Vision Transformers reduce theoretical FLOPs but fail to deliver proportional wall-clock speedups due to high dispatch overhead in variable-length attention implementations like FlashAttention-2 and PyTorch SDPA.
AI.method: Developed a lightweight, bidirectional Triton attention kernel with reduced dispatch overhead (40 us vs 60-90 us in existing methods). Integrated into a complete pack-attend-unpack pipeline that works with various pruning algorithms.
AI.result: Achieved up to 2.24x end-to-end throughput improvement over padded PyTorch SDPA, consistent across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS) and scaling across DeiT-T/S/B models, while maintaining bit-exact classification predictions.
AI.conclusion: The dispatch overhead bottleneck in current variable-length attention implementations prevents token pruning from translating theoretical FLOPs reductions into actual speedups. The proposed Triton kernel overcomes this limitation, making pruning benefits visible in wall-clock time.

title: LLM Reasoning Is Latent, Not the Chain of Thought
summary: This position paper argues that large language model (LLM) reasoning should be studied as latent-state trajectory formation rather than as faithful surface chain-of-thought (CoT). This matters because claims about faithfulness, interpretability, reasoning benchmarks, and inference-time intervention all depend on what the field takes the primary object of reasoning to be. We ask what that object should be once three often-confounded factors are separated and formalize three competing hypotheses: H1, reasoning is primarily mediated by latent-state trajectories; H2, reasoning is primarily mediated by explicit surface CoT; and H0, most apparent reasoning gains are better explained by generic serial compute than by any privileged representational object. Reorganizing recent empirical, mechanistic, and survey work under this framework, and adding compute-audited worked exemplars that factorize surface traces, latent interventions, and matched budget expansions, we find that current evidence most strongly supports H1 as a default working hypothesis rather than as a task-independent verdict. We therefore make two recommendations: the field should treat latent-state dynamics as the default object of study for LLM reasoning, and it should evaluate reasoning with designs that explicitly disentangle surface traces, latent states, and serial compute.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that LLM reasoning should be studied as latent-state trajectory formation rather than faithful surface chain-of-thought, and recommends focusing research on latent-state dynamics while using designs that disentangle surface traces, latent states, and serial compute.
AI.motivation: Current research on LLM reasoning conflates three factors: surface chain-of-thought, latent-state trajectories, and generic serial compute. This matters because claims about faithfulness, interpretability, reasoning benchmarks, and inference-time interventions all depend on what researchers consider the primary object of reasoning to be. The paper aims to establish a clearer framework for studying LLM reasoning.
AI.method: The authors formalize three competing hypotheses about LLM reasoning: H1 (mediated by latent-state trajectories), H2 (mediated by explicit surface CoT), and H0 (explained by generic serial compute). They reorganize recent empirical, mechanistic, and survey work under this framework, and add "compute-audited worked exemplars" that factorize surface traces, latent interventions, and matched budget expansions.
AI.result: Current evidence most strongly supports H1 (latent-state trajectories) as a default working hypothesis, though not as a task-independent verdict. The paper concludes that latent-state dynamics should be the default object of study for LLM reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The field should treat latent-state dynamics as the default object of study for LLM reasoning and evaluate reasoning with designs that explicitly disentangle surface traces, latent states, and serial compute. This represents a shift from focusing on surface chain-of-thought to studying the underlying latent-state formation processes.

title: The Illusion of Equivalence: Systematic FP16 Divergence in KV-Cached Autoregressive Inference
summary: KV caching is a ubiquitous optimization in autoregressive transformer inference, long presumed to be numerically equivalent to cache-free computation. This assumption fails under standard FP16 precision: cache-ON and cache-OFF execution paths employ different floating-point accumulation orderings which, due to FP16 non-associativity, produce a deterministic divergence in decoded token sequences. Across three open-weight models (LLaMA-2-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Gemma-2-2B) evaluated on GSM8K, we observe a 100\% token divergence rate across all sampling strategies, including greedy decoding, which rules out sampling randomness as a cause, and also with cache-ON yielding higher accuracy in 8 of 9 conditions, where the accuracy difference serves as an indicator that the divergence direction is systematic rather than random. Controlled FP32 falsification reduces divergence by eight orders of magnitude, eliminates token flips, and drops the flip rate to exactly 0.0\%, confirming FP16 non-associativity as the sole causal driver. Layer-wise drift profiling reveals architecturally predictable propagation patterns: models using Grouped-Query Attention exhibit sharp divergence at the first layer, while Gemma's larger head dimension and sliding window attention produce uniform accumulation across all layers. Finally, activation patching of the entire residual stream fails to recover the cache-free trajectory, localizing the causal variable to the stateful KV cache. These findings establish that FP16 KV cache inference is fundamentally non-equivalent to recomputation and provide a mechanistic framework for understanding numerical instability in modern LLM inference systems.

AI.tldr: FP16 KV caching in transformers creates numerical divergence from cache-free computation due to floating-point non-associativity, affecting all sampled tokens and model accuracy.
AI.motivation: KV caching is widely used to optimize transformer inference but has been assumed numerically equivalent to recomputation. The paper investigates whether this assumption holds under standard FP16 precision.
AI.method: Analyzed three open-weight models (LLaMA-2-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Gemma-2-2B) on GSM8K, comparing cache-ON vs cache-OFF execution. Used controlled FP32 experiments to isolate FP16 effects, layer-wise drift profiling, and activation patching to localize causes.
AI.result: Found 100% token divergence rate under FP16, with cache-ON generally yielding higher accuracy. FP32 falsification reduced divergence by 8 orders of magnitude to 0%. Layer-wise profiling revealed architectural patterns in divergence propagation. Activation patching couldn't recover cache-free trajectories.
AI.conclusion: FP16 KV cache inference is fundamentally non-equivalent to recomputation due to floating-point non-associativity. The paper provides a mechanistic framework for understanding numerical instability in modern LLM inference systems.

title: Structured Abductive-Deductive-Inductive Reasoning for LLMs via Algebraic Invariants
comment: 10 pages + 3 pages references. Accepted as a poster at the ICLR 2026 Workshop for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models exhibit systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning: they conflate hypothesis generation with verification, cannot distinguish conjecture from validated knowledge, and allow weak reasoning steps to propagate unchecked through inference chains. We present a symbolic reasoning scaffold that operationalizes Peirce's tripartite inference -- abduction, deduction, and induction -- as an explicit protocol for LLM-assisted reasoning. The framework enforces logical consistency through five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet), the strongest of which -- the Weakest Link bound -- ensures that no conclusion in a reasoning chain can exceed the reliability of its least-supported premise. This principle, independently grounded as weakest link resolution in possibilistic logic and empirically validated for chain-of-thought reasoning, prevents logical inconsistencies from accumulating across multi-step inference. We verify all invariants through a property-based testing suite of 100 properties and 16 fuzz tests over 10^5+ generated cases, providing a verified reference implementation of the invariants suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A symbolic reasoning framework based on Peirce's tripartite inference (abduction, deduction, induction) enforces logical consistency through algebraic invariants (Gamma Quintet), with the Weakest Link bound ensuring no conclusion exceeds the reliability of its least-supported premise.
AI.motivation: Large language models have systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning: they conflate hypothesis generation with verification, cannot distinguish conjecture from validated knowledge, and allow weak reasoning steps to propagate unchecked through inference chains.
AI.method: A symbolic reasoning scaffold that operationalizes Peirce's tripartite inference (abduction, deduction, and induction) as an explicit protocol for LLM-assisted reasoning, enforcing logical consistency through five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet). The strongest invariant is the Weakest Link bound, which ensures no conclusion can exceed the reliability of its least-supported premise.
