AI.result: The invariants were verified through a property-based testing suite of 100 properties and 16 fuzz tests over 100,000+ generated cases, providing a verified reference implementation suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework addresses LLMs' limitations in logical reasoning by enforcing mathematical invariants that prevent logical inconsistencies from accumulating across multi-step inference, grounded in both theoretical principles (possibilistic logic) and empirical validation.

title: PRL-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark Evaluating LLMs' Capabilities in Frontier Physics Research
summary: The paradigm of agentic science requires AI systems to conduct robust reasoning and engage in long-horizon, autonomous exploration. However, current scientific benchmarks remain confined to domain knowledge comprehension and complex reasoning, failing to evaluate the exploratory nature and procedural complexity of real-world research. In this work, we present research-oriented evaluations in theoretical and computational physics, a natural testbed with comprehensive domain knowledge, complex reasoning, and verifiable end-to-end workflows without reliance on experiments. Here we introduce PRL-Bench (Physics Research by LLMs), a benchmark designed to systematically map the capability boundaries of LLMs in executing end-to-end physics research. Constructed from 100 curated papers from the latest issues of Physical Review Letters since August 2025 and validated by domain experts, PRL-Bench covers five major theory- and computation-intensive subfields of modern physics: astrophysics, condensed matter physics, high-energy physics, quantum information, and statistical physics. Each task in the benchmark is designed to replicate the core properties of authentic scientific research, including exploration-oriented formulation, long-horizon workflows, and objective verifiability, thereby reconstructing the essential reasoning processes and research workflows of real physics research. Evaluation across frontier models shows that performance remains limited, with the best overall score below 50, revealing a pronounced gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of real scientific research. PRL-Bench serves a reliable testbed for accessing next generation AI scientists advancing AI systems toward autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: PRL-Bench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to conduct end-to-end physics research, created from 100 recent Physical Review Letters papers, revealing current models perform poorly (below 50% score) on authentic scientific research tasks.
AI.motivation: Current scientific AI benchmarks focus only on domain knowledge and reasoning, but lack evaluation of exploratory nature and procedural complexity required for real-world research. There's a need to assess AI systems' capability to conduct autonomous, long-horizon scientific exploration.
AI.method: Created PRL-Bench by curating 100 papers from Physical Review Letters since August 2025, validated by domain experts. Covers five major physics subfields: astrophysics, condensed matter, high-energy, quantum information, and statistical physics. Tasks replicate core properties of authentic research including exploration-oriented formulation, long-horizon workflows, and objective verifiability.
AI.result: Evaluation of frontier LLMs shows limited performance with best overall score below 50, revealing significant gap between current LLM capabilities and requirements of real scientific research.
AI.conclusion: PRL-Bench provides a reliable testbed for developing next-generation AI scientists and advancing AI systems toward autonomous scientific discovery, highlighting substantial room for improvement in AI's ability to conduct authentic physics research.

title: KWBench: Measuring Unprompted Problem Recognition in Knowledge Work
summary:
We introduce the first version of KWBench (Knowledge Work Bench), a benchmark for unprompted problem recognition in large language models: can an LLM identify a professional scenario before attempting to solve it. Existing frontier benchmarks have saturated, and most knowledge-work evaluations to date reduce to extraction or task completion against a specification. KWBench targets the step before that: recognizing the governing structure of the situation from raw inputs alone.
  The benchmark contains 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across acquisitions, contract negotiations, clinical pharmacy, organizational politics, fraud analysis, and incentive design. Each task encodes a formal game-theoretic pattern (principal-agent conflict, signaling, mechanism design failure, strategic omission, coalitional dynamics, strategic interdependence) and carries structured ground truth recording the expert reading of the situation and the anticipated failure modes. Models receive raw data and a task prompt with no indication of problem type. Scoring is a three-tier rubric gated by a mandatory conjunctive check. Mandatory criteria encode the predicted wrong paths.
  We evaluate 16 models. The best model passes on 27.9% of tasks. The top two models agree on only 31.7% of their passes. Among the top 8, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model; routing across the top 8 covers 50.7% of the benchmark, nearly double the best single model. Conditional on passing, quality scores converge (approx 83% across models); unconditional scores do not. Same models articulate the relevant game-theoretic concept correctly when asked, then fail to apply it unprompted. We release KWBench to shift how frontier models are evaluated on knowledge work, scoring them on whether they recognize the right problem from the situation alone, not only on how well they execute once the problem has been framed for them.

AI.tldr: KWBench is the first benchmark for testing LLMs' ability to recognize professional problems without prompting, featuring 223 tasks across diverse domains with game-theory patterns, where models must identify situations from raw data alone rather than solve already-framed problems.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks have saturated and current knowledge-work evaluations mainly test extraction or task completion against specifications. There's a need to evaluate the crucial step before problem-solving: recognizing the governing structure of professional situations from raw inputs alone without being told what type of problem it is.
AI.method: KWBench contains 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across acquisitions, contract negotiations, clinical pharmacy, organizational politics, fraud analysis, and incentive design. Each task encodes formal game-theoretic patterns (principal-agent conflict, signaling, mechanism design failure, etc.) with structured ground truth. Models receive raw data and a task prompt with no problem type indication. Scoring uses a three-tier rubric gated by a mandatory conjunctive check that encodes predicted wrong paths.
AI.result: Evaluation of 16 models shows the best model passes only 27.9% of tasks. Top two models agree on only 31.7% of their passes. Among top 8 models, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model. Routing across top 8 models covers 50.7% of the benchmark (nearly double the best single model). Conditional on passing, quality scores converge around 83%, but unconditional scores don't. Models can articulate relevant game-theoretic concepts when asked but fail to apply them unprompted.
AI.conclusion: KWBench reveals significant gaps in LLMs' ability to recognize professional problems unprompted, showing this is a challenging frontier. The benchmark aims to shift evaluation focus to whether models can identify the right problem from situations alone, not just execute well on already-framed problems. The low performance and high model disagreement suggest this capability remains underdeveloped.

title: Beyond Single-Model Optimization: Preserving Plasticity in Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Continual reinforcement learning must balance retention with adaptation, yet many methods still rely on \emph{single-model preservation}, committing to one evolving policy as the main reusable solution across tasks. Even when a previously successful policy is retained, it may no longer provide a reliable starting point for rapid adaptation after interference, reflecting a form of \emph{loss of plasticity} that single-policy preservation cannot address. Inspired by quality-diversity methods, we introduce \textsc{TeLAPA} (Transfer-Enabled Latent-Aligned Policy Archives), a continual RL framework that organizes behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods into per-task archives and maintains a shared latent space so that archived policies remain comparable and reusable under non-stationary drift. This perspective shifts continual RL from retaining isolated solutions to maintaining \emph{skill-aligned neighborhoods} with competent and behaviorally related policies that support future relearning. In our MiniGrid CL setting, \textsc{TeLAPA} learns more tasks successfully, recovers competence faster on revisited tasks after interference, and retains higher performance across a sequence of tasks. Our analyses show that source-optimal policies are often not transfer-optimal, even within a local competent neighborhood, and that effective reuse depends on retaining and selecting among multiple nearby alternatives rather than collapsing them to one representative. Together, these results reframe continual RL around reusable and competent policy neighborhoods, providing a route beyond single-model preservation toward more plastic lifelong agents.

AI.tldr: TeLAPA is a continual RL framework that organizes behaviorally diverse policies into per-task archives with shared latent space, shifting from single-model preservation to skill-aligned neighborhoods for better retention and adaptation.
AI.motivation: Current continual RL methods rely on single-model preservation which can't address loss of plasticity - retained policies may not provide reliable starting points for rapid adaptation after interference.
AI.method: Inspired by quality-diversity methods, TeLAPA organizes behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods into per-task archives and maintains a shared latent space so that archived policies remain comparable and reusable under non-stationary drift.
AI.result: In MiniGrid CL setting, TeLAPA learns more tasks successfully, recovers competence faster on revisited tasks after interference, and retains higher performance across a sequence of tasks.
AI.conclusion: TeLAPA reframes continual RL around reusable and competent policy neighborhoods, providing a route beyond single-model preservation toward more plastic lifelong agents.

title: StoSignSGD: Unbiased Structural Stochasticity Fixes SignSGD for Training Large Language Models
summary: Sign-based optimization algorithms, such as SignSGD, have garnered significant attention for their remarkable performance in distributed learning and training large foundation models. Despite their empirical superiority, SignSGD is known to diverge on non-smooth objectives, which are ubiquitous in modern machine learning due to ReLUs, max-pools, and mixture-of-experts. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose \textbf{StoSignSGD}, an algorithm that injects structural stochasticity into the sign operator while maintaining an unbiased update step. In the regime of (online) convex optimization, our theoretical analysis shows that StoSignSGD rigorously resolves the non-convergence issues of SignSGD, achieving a sharp convergence rate matching the lower bound. For the more challenging non-convex non-smooth optimization, we introduce generalized stationary measures that encompass prior definitions, proving that StoSignSGD improves upon the best-known complexity bounds by dimensional factors. Empirically, StoSignSGD exhibits robust stability and superior efficiency across diverse large language model (LLM) training regimes. Notably, in low-precision FP8 pretraining -- a setting where AdamW fails catastrophically -- StoSignSGD remains highly stable and yields a remarkable 1.44$\times$ to 2.14$\times$ speedup relative to established baselines. Furthermore, when fine-tuning 7B LLMs on mathematical reasoning tasks, StoSignSGD delivers substantial performance gains over both AdamW and SignSGD. Finally, to dissect the mechanisms driving its success, we develop a sign conversion framework capable of transforming any general optimizer into its unbiased, sign-based counterpart. Utilizing this framework, we deconstruct the core components of StoSignSGD and present a comprehensive ablation study to empirically validate our algorithmic design choices.

AI.tldr: StoSignSGD introduces structural stochasticity into SignSGD to overcome divergence on non-smooth objectives, achieving improved convergence rates, superior LLM training performance (especially in low-precision settings), and a theoretical framework for sign-based optimization.
AI.motivation: SignSGD and similar sign-based algorithms have shown strong empirical performance in distributed learning and training foundation models, but they fundamentally diverge on non-smooth objectives which are common in modern ML due to ReLUs, max-pools, and mixture-of-experts architectures.
AI.method: StoSignSGD injects structural stochasticity into the sign operator while maintaining an unbiased update step. The paper also develops a sign conversion framework that can transform any general optimizer into its unbiased, sign-based counterpart.
AI.result:
1) Theoretical: Achieves sharp convergence rate matching lower bounds for convex optimization; improves best-known complexity bounds by dimensional factors for non-convex non-smooth problems.
2) Empirical: Robust stability and superior efficiency in LLM training; 1.44-2.14x speedup in low-precision FP8 pretraining; substantial performance gains in 7B LLM fine-tuning on math reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: StoSignSGD successfully resolves the fundamental non-convergence issues of SignSGD on non-smooth objectives while maintaining empirical superiority. The proposed sign conversion framework provides a general approach for creating unbiased sign-based optimizers, with comprehensive ablation studies validating the design choices.

title: Discover and Prove: An Open-source Agentic Framework for Hard Mode Automated Theorem Proving in Lean 4
summary: Most ATP benchmarks embed the final answer within the formal statement -- a convention we call "Easy Mode" -- a design that simplifies the task relative to what human competitors face and may lead to optimistic estimates of model capability. We call the stricter, more realistic setting "Hard Mode": the system must independently discover the answer before constructing a formal proof. To enable Hard Mode research, we make two contributions. First, we release MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard, expert-reannotated Hard Mode variants of two widely-used ATP benchmarks. Second, we introduce Discover And Prove (DAP), an agentic framework that uses LLM natural-language reasoning with explicit self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrites Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode ones for existing ATP provers. DAP sets the state of the art: on CombiBench it raises solved problems from 7 (previous SOTA, Pass@16) to 10; on PutnamBench it is the first system to formally prove 36 theorems in Hard Mode -- while simultaneously revealing that state-of-the-art LLMs exceed 80% answer accuracy on the same problems where formal provers manage under 10%, exposing a substantial gap that Hard Mode benchmarks are uniquely suited to measure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Hard Mode" benchmarking for automated theorem proving, creates Hard Mode versions of existing benchmarks, and proposes DAP framework that outperforms previous methods.
AI.motivation: Current ATP benchmarks embed answers in formal statements ("Easy Mode"), which simplifies the task compared to what human competitors face and may lead to overestimating model capabilities. The authors aim to create more realistic "Hard Mode" settings where systems must independently discover answers before proving them.
AI.method: 1) Created expert-reannotated Hard Mode variants (MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard) of existing benchmarks. 2) Developed Discover And Prove (DAP) framework: uses LLM natural-language reasoning with self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrites Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode for existing ATP provers.
AI.result: DAP achieves state-of-the-art results: raises solved problems on CombiBench from 7 (previous SOTA) to 10; on PutnamBench it proves 36 theorems in Hard Mode (first system to do so). Also reveals that LLMs achieve >80% answer accuracy where formal provers manage <10%, exposing a substantial capability gap.
AI.conclusion: Hard Mode benchmarking provides a more realistic evaluation of ATP systems, revealing significant gaps between LLM answer discovery and formal proof capabilities. The DAP framework demonstrates effective integration of LLM reasoning with ATP systems to address Hard Mode challenges.

title: Transfer Learning from Foundational Optimization Embeddings to Unsupervised SAT Representations
summary: Foundational optimization embeddings have recently emerged as powerful pre-trained representations for mixed-integer programming (MIP) problems. These embeddings were shown to enable cross-domain transfer and reduce reliance on solver-generated labels. In this work, we investigate whether such representations generalize beyond optimization to decision problems, focusing on Boolean satisfiability (SAT). We adapt the foundational optimization architecture to SAT by mapping CNF formulas into the same bipartite constraint-variable graph representation used for MIPs. This allows direct reuse of the pre-trained embedding model without architectural changes or supervised fine-tuning. Our results show that these embeddings capture structural regularities in SAT instances and support unsupervised tasks such as instance clustering and distribution identification. We demonstrate, for the first time, that foundational optimization embeddings can transfer to constraint satisfaction domains. Our findings is a step toward a unified representational framework for both optimization and decision problems.

AI.tldr: Foundational optimization embeddings, originally trained for mixed-integer programming, can be directly applied to Boolean satisfiability (SAT) problems without architectural changes or fine-tuning, enabling unsupervised tasks like clustering and distribution identification across both optimization and decision domains.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether foundational optimization embeddings that were successful for mixed-integer programming (MIP) problems can generalize to decision problems like Boolean satisfiability (SAT), aiming to develop a unified representational framework for both optimization and decision problems.
AI.method: The authors adapt the foundational optimization architecture to SAT by mapping CNF formulas into the same bipartite constraint-variable graph representation used for MIPs. This allows direct reuse of the pre-trained embedding model without any architectural modifications or supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: The results show that these embeddings capture structural regularities in SAT instances and support unsupervised tasks such as instance clustering and distribution identification. This demonstrates, for the first time, that foundational optimization embeddings can transfer to constraint satisfaction domains.
AI.conclusion: Foundational optimization embeddings generalize beyond optimization to decision problems like SAT, representing a step toward a unified representational framework that bridges optimization and decision problems in a single representational system.

title: Evaluating LLM Simulators as Differentially Private Data Generators
summary: LLM-based simulators offer a promising path for generating complex synthetic data where traditional differentially private (DP) methods struggle with high-dimensional user profiles. But can LLMs faithfully reproduce statistical distributions from DP-protected inputs? We evaluate this using PersonaLedger, an agentic financial simulator, seeded with DP synthetic personas derived from real user statistics. We find that PersonaLedger achieves promising fraud detection utility (AUC 0.70 at epsilon=1) but exhibits significant distribution drift due to systematic LLM biases--learned priors overriding input statistics for temporal and demographic features. These failure modes must be addressed before LLM-based methods can handle the richer user representations where they might otherwise excel.

AI.tldr: LLM-based simulators like PersonaLedger show promise for fraud detection using DP synthetic personas, but suffer from distribution drift due to LLM biases overriding input statistics.
AI.motivation: Traditional differentially private (DP) methods struggle with high-dimensional user profiles, so researchers explore whether LLM-based simulators can faithfully reproduce statistical distributions from DP-protected inputs for complex synthetic data generation.
AI.method: Evaluated using PersonaLedger, an agentic financial simulator seeded with DP synthetic personas derived from real user statistics, testing LLM's ability to reproduce distributions from DP-protected inputs.
AI.result: PersonaLedger achieves promising fraud detection utility (AUC 0.70 at epsilon=1) but exhibits significant distribution drift due to systematic LLM biases where learned priors override input statistics for temporal and demographic features.
AI.conclusion: The failure modes of distribution drift and LLM biases overriding input statistics must be addressed before LLM-based methods can handle richer user representations where they might otherwise excel.

title: Towards Rigorous Explainability by Feature Attribution
summary: For around a decade, non-symbolic methods have been the option of choice when explaining complex machine learning (ML) models. Unfortunately, such methods lack rigor and can mislead human decision-makers. In high-stakes uses of ML, the lack of rigor is especially problematic. One prime example of provable lack of rigor is the adoption of Shapley values in explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), with the tool SHAP being a ubiquitous example. This paper overviews the ongoing efforts towards using rigorous symbolic methods of XAI as an alternative to non-rigorous non-symbolic approaches, concretely for assigning relative feature importance.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques non-symbolic explainable AI methods for lacking rigor, uses SHAP/Shapley values as an example, and advocates for symbolic XAI approaches for feature importance analysis.
AI.motivation: Non-symbolic XAI methods have been dominant but lack rigor and can mislead users, especially problematic in high-stakes ML applications where reliable explanations are critical for trust and decision-making.
AI.method: The paper advocates for symbolic (logic-based, formal) methods of XAI as a rigorous alternative to non-symbolic approaches for feature importance attribution, suggesting formal verification and logical reasoning techniques.
AI.result: The paper presents SHAP/Shapley values as a prime example of non-rigorous XAI tools and proposes that symbolic methods can provide provably correct, mathematically rigorous explanations for ML model behavior.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic XAI methods offer a more rigorous, trustworthy alternative to non-symbolic approaches, particularly important for high-stakes applications where explanation reliability is paramount.

title: Harmonizing Multi-Objective LLM Unlearning via Unified Domain Representation and Bidirectional Logit Distillation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) unlearning is crucial for removing hazardous or privacy-leaking information from the model. Practical LLM unlearning demands satisfying multiple challenging objectives simultaneously: removing undesirable knowledge, preserving general utility, avoiding over-refusal of neighboring concepts, and, crucially, ensuring robustness against adversarial probing attacks. However, existing unlearning methods primarily focus on a limited subset of these goals, typically unlearning efficacy and utility preservation while overlooking robustness and boundary behaviors. Naively extending these methods to multi-objective settings may lead to unlearning task interference. We propose a novel multi-objective unlearning framework that harmonizes multiple unlearning objectives through a data and optimization co-design: We standardize training corpora into a unified data representation to reduce the domain gap, and then introduce a bidirectional distillation method that simultaneously elicits desired behavior from a context-instructed teacher while suppressing undesirable behavior in the student model. Theoretical and empirical analyses show that our method aligns domain distributions and converts seemingly irrelevant unlearning tasks into cooperative optimization. Evaluation demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, which enables balanced and reliable unlearning across diverse, challenging requirements.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-objective LLM unlearning framework that uses data standardization and bidirectional distillation to simultaneously achieve effective unlearning, utility preservation, proper boundary behavior, and robustness against adversarial attacks.
AI.motivation: Practical LLM unlearning requires satisfying multiple challenging objectives simultaneously: removing hazardous/private information, preserving general utility, avoiding over-refusal of neighboring concepts, and ensuring robustness against adversarial attacks. Existing methods focus on limited subsets of these goals and may suffer from task interference when extended to multi-objective settings.
AI.method: A data and optimization co-design approach: 1) Standardizing training corpora into a unified data representation to reduce domain gaps, and 2) Introducing bidirectional distillation that simultaneously elicits desired behavior from a context-instructed teacher while suppressing undesirable behavior in the student model.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance, enabling balanced and reliable unlearning across diverse challenging requirements. Theoretical and empirical analyses show the approach aligns domain distributions and converts seemingly irrelevant unlearning tasks into cooperative optimization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-objective unlearning framework effectively addresses the complex requirements of practical LLM unlearning by harmonizing multiple objectives through innovative data standardization and bidirectional distillation techniques, outperforming existing methods that only address subsets of the necessary goals.

title: Integrating Graphs, Large Language Models, and Agents: Reasoning and Retrieval
summary: Generative AI, particularly Large Language Models, increasingly integrates graph-based representations to enhance reasoning, retrieval, and structured decision-making. Despite rapid advances, there remains limited clarity regarding when, why, where, and what types of graph-LLM integrations are most appropriate across applications. This survey provides a concise, structured overview of the design choices underlying the integration of graphs with LLMs. We categorize existing methods based on their purpose (reasoning, retrieval, generation, recommendation), graph modality (knowledge graphs, scene graphs, interaction graphs, causal graphs, dependency graphs), and integration strategies (prompting, augmentation, training, or agent-based use). By mapping representative works across domains such as cybersecurity, healthcare, materials science, finance, robotics, and multimodal environments, we highlight the strengths, limitations, and best-fit scenarios for each technique. This survey aims to offer researchers a practical guide for selecting the most suitable graph-LLM approach depending on task requirements, data characteristics, and reasoning complexity.

AI.tldr: This survey provides a structured overview of graph-LLM integration methods, categorizing them by purpose, graph modality, and integration strategies, while offering practical guidance for selecting appropriate approaches across various domains.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid advances in generative AI and LLMs integrating graph-based representations, there's limited clarity about when, why, where, and what types of graph-LLM integrations are most appropriate across different applications.
AI.method: The paper categorizes existing methods based on three dimensions: purpose (reasoning, retrieval, generation, recommendation), graph modality (knowledge graphs, scene graphs, interaction graphs, causal graphs, dependency graphs), and integration strategies (prompting, augmentation, training, or agent-based use). It maps representative works across multiple domains.
AI.result: The survey highlights strengths, limitations, and best-fit scenarios for each graph-LLM integration technique across domains like cybersecurity, healthcare, materials science, finance, robotics, and multimodal environments.
AI.conclusion: The paper aims to provide researchers with a practical guide for selecting the most suitable graph-LLM approach based on task requirements, data characteristics, and reasoning complexity, bridging the gap between theoretical advances and practical application.

title: $π_{0.7}$: a Steerable Generalist Robotic Foundation Model with Emergent Capabilities
summary: We present a new robotic foundation model, called $π_{0.7}$, that can enable strong out-of-the-box performance in a wide range of scenarios. $π_{0.7}$ can follow diverse language instructions in unseen environments, including multi-stage tasks with various kitchen appliances, provide zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, for example enabling a robot to fold laundry without seeing the task before, and perform challenging tasks such as operating an espresso machine out of the box at a level of performance that matches much more specialized RL-finetuned models. The main idea behind $π_{0.7}$ is to use diverse context conditioning during training. This conditioning information, contained in the prompt, makes it possible to steer the model precisely to perform many tasks with different strategies. It is conditioned not just on a language command that describes what it should do, but on additional multimodal information that also describes the manner or strategy in which it should do it, including metadata about task performance and subgoal images. This enables $π_{0.7}$ to use very diverse data, including demonstrations, potentially suboptimal (autonomous) data including failures, and data from non-robot sources. Our experiments evaluate $π_{0.7}$ across numerous tasks with multiple robot platforms, on tasks that require speed and dexterity, language following, and compositional task generalization.

AI.tldr: π₀.₇ is a new robotic foundation model that achieves strong out-of-the-box performance across diverse scenarios through diverse context conditioning, enabling it to follow language instructions, generalize across embodiments, and perform complex tasks without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Current robotic systems often require extensive task-specific training and lack the ability to generalize across different environments, embodiments, and tasks. There's a need for foundation models that can perform diverse robotic tasks out-of-the-box without specialized fine-tuning.
AI.method: The key innovation is diverse context conditioning during training. The model is conditioned not just on language commands, but also on additional multimodal information including task performance metadata, subgoal images, and strategy descriptions. This allows the model to leverage very diverse data sources including demonstrations, autonomous data (even failures), and non-robot data.
AI.result: π₀.₇ demonstrates strong out-of-the-box performance across numerous tasks with multiple robot platforms. It can follow diverse language instructions in unseen environments, perform zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, and execute challenging tasks like operating espresso machines at performance levels matching specialized RL-finetuned models.
AI.conclusion: Diverse context conditioning enables robotic foundation models to achieve strong generalization across tasks, embodiments, and strategies. π₀.₇ represents a significant advancement toward more capable, general-purpose robotic systems that can leverage diverse data sources and perform complex tasks without extensive task-specific training.

title: FineSteer: A Unified Framework for Fine-Grained Inference-Time Steering in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit undesirable behaviors, such as safety violations and hallucinations. Although inference-time steering offers a cost-effective way to adjust model behavior without updating its parameters, existing methods often fail to be simultaneously effective, utility-preserving, and training-efficient due to their rigid, one-size-fits-all designs and limited adaptability. In this work, we present FineSteer, a novel steering framework that decomposes inference-time steering into two complementary stages: conditional steering and fine-grained vector synthesis, allowing fine-grained control over when and how to steer internal representations. In the first stage, we introduce a Subspace-guided Conditional Steering (SCS) mechanism that preserves model utility by avoiding unnecessary steering. In the second stage, we propose a Mixture-of-Steering-Experts (MoSE) mechanism that captures the multimodal nature of desired steering behaviors and generates query-specific steering vectors for improved effectiveness. Through tailored designs in both SCS and MoSE, FineSteer maintains robust performance on general queries while adaptively optimizing steering vectors for targeted inputs in a training-efficient manner. Extensive experiments on safety and truthfulness benchmarks show that FineSteer outperforms state-of-the-art methods in overall performance, achieving stronger steering performance with minimal utility loss. Code is available at https://github.com/YukinoAsuna/FineSteer

AI.tldr: FineSteer is a two-stage inference-time steering framework that decomposes steering into conditional steering and fine-grained vector synthesis, enabling precise control over when and how to steer LLM representations while preserving utility and training efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit undesirable behaviors like safety violations and hallucinations. Existing inference-time steering methods fail to simultaneously be effective, utility-preserving, and training-efficient due to rigid designs and limited adaptability.
AI.method: FineSteer decomposes steering into two stages: 1) Subspace-guided Conditional Steering (SCS) that preserves utility by avoiding unnecessary steering, and 2) Mixture-of-Steering-Experts (MoSE) that generates query-specific steering vectors to capture multimodal steering behaviors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on safety and truthfulness benchmarks show FineSteer outperforms state-of-the-art methods in overall performance, achieving stronger steering with minimal utility loss.
AI.conclusion: FineSteer provides an effective, training-efficient framework for inference-time steering of LLMs that maintains robust performance on general queries while adaptively optimizing steering for targeted inputs.

title: ReactBench: A Benchmark for Topological Reasoning in MLLMs on Chemical Reaction Diagrams
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at recognizing individual visual elements and reasoning over simple linear diagrams. However, when faced with complex topological structures involving branching paths, converging flows, and cyclic dependencies, their reasoning capabilities degrade sharply, even on tasks as basic as counting endpoints. Existing benchmarks fail to probe this gap, focusing on semantic comprehension rather than structural reasoning. We introduce ReactBench, a benchmark that reveals fundamental limitations in structural reasoning through chemical reaction diagrams. These real-world scientific diagrams offer an ideal testbed because they naturally span diverse structures from linear chains to cyclic graphs, while requiring both precise local recognition and coherent global reasoning. Our benchmark comprises 1,618 expert-annotated QA pairs across four hierarchical task dimensions. Extensive evaluation across 17 MLLMs reveals a significant performance gap exceeding 30% between anchor-based tasks and holistic structural reasoning tasks. Controlled ablations confirm this bottleneck lies in reasoning, not perception. These findings expose a fundamental deficit in structural understanding and establish directions for advancing visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: MLLMs struggle with complex topological structures in visual diagrams; ReactBench reveals this gap through chemical reaction diagrams, showing over 30% performance drop on structural reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) perform well on recognizing individual elements and simple linear diagrams but degrade sharply on complex topological structures (branching paths, converging flows, cyclic dependencies). Existing benchmarks focus on semantic comprehension rather than structural reasoning, failing to probe this critical gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce ReactBench, a benchmark using chemical reaction diagrams as ideal testbed due to their natural span of diverse structures from linear chains to cyclic graphs, requiring both precise local recognition and coherent global reasoning. The benchmark comprises 1,618 expert-annotated QA pairs across four hierarchical task dimensions.
AI.result: Evaluation across 17 MLLMs reveals a significant performance gap exceeding 30% between anchor-based tasks (simple) and holistic structural reasoning tasks. Controlled ablations confirm the bottleneck lies in reasoning capabilities rather than perception abilities.
AI.conclusion: The findings expose a fundamental deficit in structural understanding in current MLLMs and establish important directions for advancing visual reasoning capabilities, particularly for complex topological structures in scientific and technical diagrams.

title: ProtoTTA: Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Deep networks that rely on prototypes-interpretable representations that can be related to the model input-have gained significant attention for balancing high accuracy with inherent interpretability, which makes them suitable for critical domains such as healthcare. However, these models are limited by their reliance on training data, which hampers their robustness to distribution shifts. While test-time adaptation (TTA) improves the robustness of deep networks by updating parameters and statistics, the prototypes of interpretable models have not been explored for this purpose. We introduce ProtoTTA, a general framework for prototypical models that leverages intermediate prototype signals rather than relying solely on model outputs. ProtoTTA minimizes the entropy of the prototype-similarity distribution to encourage more confident and prototype-specific activations on shifted data. To maintain stability, we employ geometric filtering to restrict updates to samples with reliable prototype activations, regularized by prototype-importance weights and model-confidence scores. Experiments across four prototypical backbones on four diverse benchmarks spanning fine-grained vision, histopathology, and NLP demonstrate that ProtoTTA improves robustness over standard output entropy minimization while restoring correct semantic focus in prototype activations. We also introduce novel interpretability metrics and a vision-language model (VLM) evaluation framework to explain TTA dynamics, confirming ProtoTTA restores human-aligned semantic focus and correlates reliably with VLM-rated reasoning quality. Code is available at: https://github.com/DeepRCL/ProtoTTA.

AI.tldr: ProtoTTA introduces test-time adaptation for prototypical models by optimizing prototype-similarity distributions rather than just model outputs, improving robustness to distribution shifts while maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Prototypical models balance accuracy with interpretability for critical domains like healthcare, but suffer from poor robustness to distribution shifts. While test-time adaptation (TTA) helps standard deep networks, it hasn't been explored for prototypical models' interpretable prototypes.
AI.method: ProtoTTA minimizes entropy of prototype-similarity distribution to encourage confident, prototype-specific activations. Uses geometric filtering to restrict updates to reliable samples, regularized by prototype-importance weights and model-confidence scores for stability.
AI.result: Experiments across four prototypical backbones on four diverse benchmarks (fine-grained vision, histopathology, NLP) show ProtoTTA improves robustness over standard output entropy minimization and restores correct semantic focus in prototype activations.
AI.conclusion: ProtoTTA successfully adapts prototypical models at test time using prototype signals, enhancing robustness while preserving interpretability. Novel interpretability metrics and VLM evaluation confirm it restores human-aligned semantic focus and correlates with reasoning quality.

title: MEDLEY-BENCH: Scale Buys Evaluation but Not Control in AI Metacognition
summary: Metacognition, the ability to monitor and regulate one's own reasoning, remains under-evaluated in AI benchmarking. We introduce MEDLEY-BENCH, a benchmark of behavioural metacognition that separates independent reasoning, private self-revision, and socially influenced revision under genuine inter-model disagreement. The benchmark evaluates 35 models from 12 families on 130 ambiguous instances across five domains and reports two complementary scores: the Medley Metacognition Score (MMS), a tier-based aggregate of reflective updating, social robustness, and epistemic articulation, and the Medley Ability Score (MAS), derived from four metacognitive sub-abilities. Results show a robust evaluation/control dissociation: evaluation ability increases with model size within families, whereas control does not. In a follow-up progressive adversarial analysis of 11 models, we observed two behavioural profiles, i.e., models that revise primarily in response to argument quality and models that track consensus statistics. Under within-model relative profiling (ipsative scoring), evaluation was the weakest relative ability in all 35 models, indicating a systematic knowing/doing gap. Smaller and cheaper models often matched or outperformed larger counterparts, suggesting that metacognitive competence is not simply a function of scale. These findings position MEDLEY-BENCH as a tool for measuring belief revision under social pressure and suggest that future training should reward calibrated, proportional updating rather than output quality alone.

AI.tldr: MEDLEY-BENCH introduces a benchmark for evaluating AI metacognition, separating reasoning, self-revision, and socially-influenced revision, revealing that evaluation ability scales with model size while control doesn't, with smaller models sometimes outperforming larger ones in metacognitive competence.
AI.motivation: Current AI benchmarking under-evaluates metacognition - the ability to monitor and regulate one's own reasoning. There's a need to systematically measure how AI models handle belief revision, especially under conditions of genuine disagreement and social influence.
AI.method: Created MEDLEY-BENCH with 130 ambiguous instances across 5 domains. The benchmark evaluates 35 models from 12 families, separating independent reasoning, private self-revision, and socially influenced revision. Uses two scores: MMS (tier-based aggregate of reflective updating, social robustness, epistemic articulation) and MAS (derived from 4 metacognitive sub-abilities). Includes progressive adversarial analysis and within-model relative profiling.
AI.result: Evaluation ability increases with model size within families, but control ability doesn't. Two behavioral profiles emerged: models revising based on argument quality vs. those tracking consensus. Evaluation was weakest relative ability in all models (knowing/doing gap). Smaller/cheaper models often matched or outperformed larger counterparts in metacognitive competence.
AI.conclusion: MEDLEY-BENCH provides a tool for measuring belief revision under social pressure. Metacognitive competence isn't simply a function of scale. Future training should reward calibrated, proportional updating rather than output quality alone.

title: Optimizing Stochastic Gradient Push under Broadcast Communications
summary: We consider the problem of minimizing the convergence time for decentralized federated learning (DFL) in wireless networks under broadcast communications, with focus on mixing matrix design. The mixing matrix is a critical hyperparameter for DFL that simultaneously controls the convergence rate across iterations and the communication demand per iteration, both strongly influencing the convergence time. Although the problem has been studied previously, existing solutions are mostly designed for decentralized parallel stochastic gradient descent (D-PSGD), which requires the mixing matrix to be symmetric and doubly stochastic. These constraints confine the activated communication graph to undirected (i.e., bidirected) graphs, which limits design flexibility. In contrast, we consider mixing matrix design for stochastic gradient push (SGP), which allows asymmetric mixing matrices and hence directed communication graphs. By analyzing how the convergence rate of SGP depends on the mixing matrices, we extract an objective function that explicitly depends on graph-theoretic parameters of the activated communication graph, based on which we develop an efficient design algorithm with performance guarantees. Our evaluations based on real data show that the proposed solution can notably reduce the convergence time compared to the state of the art without compromising the quality of the trained model.

AI.tldr: TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel mixing matrix design for Stochastic Gradient Push (SGP) in decentralized federated learning over wireless networks, enabling directed communication graphs to reduce convergence time compared to existing symmetric methods.
AI.motivation: Existing decentralized federated learning methods like D-PSGD require symmetric, doubly stochastic mixing matrices, which confine communication to undirected graphs. This limits design flexibility and optimization opportunities for reducing convergence time in wireless networks with broadcast communications.
AI.method: The paper analyzes how SGP's convergence rate depends on mixing matrices, extracts an objective function based on graph-theoretic parameters of the communication graph, and develops an efficient design algorithm with performance guarantees that supports asymmetric mixing matrices and directed graphs.
AI.result: The proposed solution significantly reduces convergence time compared to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining model quality, as demonstrated through evaluations using real data.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging asymmetric mixing matrices and directed communication graphs in SGP, the paper provides a more flexible and efficient approach to mixing matrix design that outperforms existing symmetric methods for decentralized federated learning in wireless networks.

title: Natural gradient descent with momentum
summary: We consider the problem of approximating a function by an element of a nonlinear manifold which admits a differentiable parametrization, typical examples being neural networks with differentiable activation functions or tensor networks. Natural gradient descent (NGD) for the optimization of a loss function can be seen as a preconditioned gradient descent where updates in the parameter space are driven by a functional perspective. In a spirit similar to Newton's method, a NGD step uses, instead of the Hessian, the Gram matrix of the generating system of the tangent space to the approximation manifold at the current iterate, with respect to a suitable metric. This corresponds to a locally optimal update in function space, following a projected gradient onto the tangent space to the manifold. Still, both gradient and natural gradient descent methods get stuck in local minima. Furthermore, when the model class is a nonlinear manifold or the loss function is not ideally conditioned (e.g., the KL-divergence for density estimation, or a norm of the residual of a partial differential equation in physics informed learning), even the natural gradient might yield non-optimal directions at each step. This work introduces a natural version of classical inertial dynamic methods like Heavy-Ball or Nesterov and show how it can improve the learning process when working with nonlinear model classes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a natural version of inertial optimization methods (like Heavy-Ball and Nesterov) that improves learning for nonlinear model classes like neural networks, addressing limitations of natural gradient descent.
AI.motivation: Natural gradient descent (NGD) for nonlinear manifolds (like neural networks or tensor networks) can get stuck in local minima and yields non-optimal directions when model classes are nonlinear or loss functions are poorly conditioned (e.g., KL-divergence, PDE residuals). Even with a functional perspective and tangent space projections, NGD struggles with suboptimal search directions in complex optimization landscapes.
AI.method: The authors develop a natural version of inertial dynamic optimization methods (specifically Heavy-Ball and Nesterov methods). This approach incorporates momentum concepts into the natural gradient framework, operating in the parameter space while maintaining the functional perspective of NGD that uses the Gram matrix of the tangent space generating system instead of the Hessian.
AI.result: The proposed natural inertial methods show improved learning processes when working with nonlinear model classes, addressing the limitations of both standard gradient descent and natural gradient descent that get stuck in local minima or follow non-optimal directions at each optimization step.
AI.conclusion: Extending inertial optimization methods to the natural gradient framework provides superior optimization performance for nonlinear model classes and poorly conditioned loss functions, overcoming fundamental limitations of existing gradient-based methods in complex function approximation settings.

title: MARCH: Multi-Agent Radiology Clinical Hierarchy for CT Report Generation
summary: Automated 3D radiology report generation often suffers from clinical hallucinations and a lack of the iterative verification found in human practice. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have advanced the field, they typically operate as monolithic "black-box" systems without the collaborative oversight characteristic of clinical workflows. To address these challenges, we propose MARCH (Multi-Agent Radiology Clinical Hierarchy), a multi-agent framework that emulates the professional hierarchy of radiology departments and assigns specialized roles to distinct agents. MARCH utilizes a Resident Agent for initial drafting with multi-scale CT feature extraction, multiple Fellow Agents for retrieval-augmented revision, and an Attending Agent that orchestrates an iterative, stance-based consensus discourse to resolve diagnostic discrepancies. On the RadGenome-ChestCT dataset, MARCH significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both clinical fidelity and linguistic accuracy. Our work demonstrates that modeling human-like organizational structures enhances the reliability of AI in high-stakes medical domains.

AI.tldr: MARCH is a multi-agent framework for 3D radiology report generation that emulates clinical hierarchies to reduce hallucinations and improve reliability through collaborative verification.
AI.motivation: Current automated 3D radiology report generation suffers from clinical hallucinations and lacks the iterative verification process characteristic of human clinical workflows. Most Vision-Language Models operate as monolithic "black-box" systems without the collaborative oversight found in actual radiology practice.
AI.method: Proposes MARCH (Multi-Agent Radiology Clinical Hierarchy), a multi-agent framework that emulates the professional hierarchy of radiology departments. It assigns specialized roles to distinct agents: Resident Agent for initial drafting with multi-scale CT feature extraction, multiple Fellow Agents for retrieval-augmented revision, and an Attending Agent that orchestrates an iterative, stance-based consensus discourse to resolve diagnostic discrepancies.
AI.result: On the RadGenome-ChestCT dataset, MARCH significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both clinical fidelity and linguistic accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Modeling human-like organizational structures enhances the reliability of AI in high-stakes medical domains, demonstrating that hierarchical multi-agent systems can improve clinical decision-making processes in radiology.

title: Learning Affine-Equivariant Proximal Operators
summary: Proximal operators are fundamental across many applications in signal processing and machine learning, including solving ill-posed inverse problems. Recent work has introduced Learned Proximal Networks (LPNs), providing parametric functions that compute exact proximals for data-driven and potentially non-convex regularizers. However, in many settings it is important to include additional structure to these regularizers--and their corresponding proximals--such as shift and scale equivariance. In this work, we show how to obtain learned functions parametrized by neural networks that provably compute exact proximal operators while being equivariant to shifts and scaling, which we dub Affine-Equivariant Learned Proximal Networks (AE-LPNs). We demonstrate our results on synthetic, constructive examples, and then on real data via denoising in out-of-distribution settings. Our equivariant learned proximals enhance robustness to noise distributions and affine shifts far beyond training distributions, improving the practical utility of learned proximal operators

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Affine-Equivariant Learned Proximal Networks (AE-LPNs) that compute exact proximal operators while being provably equivariant to shifts and scaling, improving robustness to out-of-distribution data.
AI.motivation: Proximal operators are crucial in signal processing and machine learning, but existing Learned Proximal Networks (LPNs) lack structural constraints like shift and scale equivariance, which is important for robustness and practical utility in real-world applications.
AI.method: Develop learned functions parameterized by neural networks that provably compute exact proximal operators while being equivariant to affine transformations (shifts and scaling). The method extends LPNs by incorporating explicit equivariance constraints into the network architecture.
AI.result: The proposed AE-LPNs demonstrate enhanced robustness to noise distributions and affine shifts beyond training distributions. Validation on synthetic examples and real denoising tasks shows improved performance in out-of-distribution settings compared to standard LPNs.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating affine equivariance constraints into learned proximal operators significantly improves their practical utility and robustness, making them more suitable for real-world applications where data distributions may shift or scale differently than training data.

title: Characterising LLM-Generated Competency Questions: a Cross-Domain Empirical Study using Open and Closed Models
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2507.02989
summary: Competency Questions (CQs) are a cornerstone of requirement elicitation in ontology engineering. CQs represent requirements as a set of natural language questions that an ontology should satisfy; they are traditionally modelled by ontology engineers together with domain experts as part of a human-centred, manual elicitation process. The use of Generative AI automates CQ creation at scale, therefore democratising the process of generation, widening stakeholder engagement, and ultimately broadening access to ontology engineering. However, given the large and heterogeneous landscape of LLMs, varying in dimensions such as parameter scale, task and domain specialisation, and accessibility, it is crucial to characterise and understand the intrinsic, observable properties of the CQs they produce (e.g., readability, structural complexity) through a systematic, cross-domain analysis. This paper introduces a set of quantitative measures for the systematic comparison of CQs across multiple dimensions. Using CQs generated from well defined use cases and scenarios, we identify their salient properties, including readability, relevance with respect to the input text and structural complexity of the generated questions. We conduct our experiments over a set of use cases and requirements using a range of LLMs, including both open (KimiK2-1T, LLama3.1-8B, LLama3.2-3B) and closed models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT 4.1). Our analysis demonstrates that LLM performance reflects distinct generation profiles shaped by the use case.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces quantitative measures to systematically analyze and compare Competency Questions (CQs) generated by different LLMs, examining properties like readability, relevance, and structural complexity across various use cases.
AI.motivation: Competency Questions are traditionally created manually in ontology engineering, but Generative AI can automate CQ creation at scale. However, with many diverse LLMs available, there's a need to systematically characterize the properties of CQs they produce to understand their generation capabilities across different dimensions.
AI.method: The authors developed quantitative measures for systematic comparison of CQs across multiple dimensions. They used well-defined use cases and scenarios to generate CQs, then analyzed their properties including readability, relevance to input text, and structural complexity. Experiments were conducted using both open (KimiK2-1T, LLama3.1-8B, LLama3.2-3B) and closed models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT 4.1).
AI.result: The analysis demonstrates that LLM performance reflects distinct generation profiles shaped by the specific use case. Different LLMs produce CQs with varying characteristics in terms of readability, relevance, and structural complexity.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can automate Competency Question generation at scale, but their performance varies significantly based on model characteristics and use case context. Systematic quantitative analysis is essential for understanding and comparing CQ generation capabilities across different LLMs in ontology engineering applications.

title: Predicting Where Steering Vectors Succeed
comment: 19 pages, incl. 10 appendix pages, 4 figures, 20 tables
summary: Steering vectors work for some concepts and layers but fail for others, and practitioners have no way to predict which setting applies before running an intervention. We introduce the Linear Accessibility Profile (LAP), a per-layer diagnostic that repurposes the logit lens as a predictor of steering vector effectiveness. The key measure, $A_{\mathrm{lin}}$, applies the model's unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden states, requiring no training. Across 24 controlled binary concept families on five models (Pythia-2.8B to Llama-8B), peak $A_{\mathrm{lin}}$ predicts steering effectiveness at $ρ= +0.86$ to $+0.91$ and layer selection at $ρ= +0.63$ to $+0.92$. A three-regime framework explains when difference-of-means steering works, when nonlinear methods are needed, and when no method can work. An entity-steering demo confirms the prediction end-to-end: steering at the LAP-recommended layer redirects completions on Gemma-2-2B and OLMo-2-1B-Instruct, while the middle layer (the standard heuristic) has no effect on either model.

AI.tldr: LAP (Linear Accessibility Profile) is a diagnostic tool that predicts when steering vectors will work for concept editing in LLMs without needing training.
AI.motivation: Current steering vector methods are unpredictable - they work for some concepts/layers but fail for others, with no way to know beforehand if an intervention will succeed.
AI.method: Uses linear accessibility measure A_lin that applies the model's unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden states (repurposing logit lens). Analyzes 24 binary concept families across 5 models (Pythia-2.8B to Llama-8B).
AI.result: Peak A_lin predicts steering effectiveness with ρ=0.86-0.91 and layer selection with ρ=0.63-0.92. Identifies three regimes: when difference-of-means works, when nonlinear methods needed, and when no method works.
AI.conclusion: LAP provides reliable diagnostic for steering vector effectiveness, enabling practitioners to predict success before intervention. Entity-steering demo confirms LAP recommendations outperform standard heuristic.

title: Learning to Reason with Insight for Informal Theorem Proving
summary: Although most of the automated theorem-proving approaches depend on formal proof systems, informal theorem proving can align better with large language models' (LLMs) strength in natural language processing. In this work, we identify a primary bottleneck in informal theorem proving as a lack of insight, namely the difficulty of recognizing the core techniques required to solve complex problems. To address this, we propose a novel framework designed to cultivate this essential reasoning skill and enable LLMs to perform insightful reasoning. We propose $\mathtt{DeepInsightTheorem}$, a hierarchical dataset that structures informal proofs by explicitly extracting core techniques and proof sketches alongside the final proof. To fully exploit this dataset, we design a Progressive Multi-Stage SFT strategy that mimics the human learning process, guiding the model from basic proof writing to insightful thinking. Our experiments on challenging mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that this insight-aware generation strategy significantly outperforms baselines. These results demonstrate that teaching models to identify and apply core techniques can substantially improve their mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: A framework called DeepInsightTheorem that trains LLMs to recognize and apply core mathematical techniques for better informal theorem proving through hierarchical proof structuring and progressive training.
AI.motivation: Current automated theorem proving relies on formal proof systems, but informal theorem proving better aligns with LLMs' natural language processing strengths. The primary bottleneck identified is the lack of insight - difficulty in recognizing core techniques needed to solve complex mathematical problems.
AI.method: Proposed DeepInsightTheorem dataset that structures informal proofs hierarchically by explicitly extracting core techniques and proof sketches alongside final proofs. Designed Progressive Multi-Stage Supervised Fine-Tuning strategy mimicking human learning: starting from basic proof writing and gradually moving to insightful thinking and technique recognition.
AI.result: Experiments on challenging mathematical benchmarks show the insight-aware generation strategy significantly outperforms baselines. The approach demonstrates that teaching models to identify and apply core techniques substantially improves their mathematical reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Teaching language models to recognize and apply core mathematical techniques through structured datasets and progressive training can overcome the insight bottleneck in informal theorem proving, leading to significantly better mathematical reasoning performance.

title: Reward Weighted Classifier-Free Guidance as Policy Improvement in Autoregressive Models
summary: Consider an auto-regressive model that produces outputs x (e.g., answers to questions, molecules) each of which can be summarized by an attribute vector y (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness, or bio-availability vs. lipophilicity). An arbitrary reward function r(y) encodes tradeoffs between these properties. Typically, tilting the model's sampling distribution to increase this reward is done at training time via reinforcement learning. However, if the reward function changes, re-alignment requires re-training. In this paper, we show that a reward weighted classifier-free guidance (RCFG) can act as a policy improvement operator in this setting, approximating tilting the sampling distribution by the Q function. We apply RCFG to molecular generation, demonstrating that it can optimize novel reward functions at test time. Finally, we show that using RCFG as a teacher and distilling into the base policy to serve as a warm start significantly speeds up convergence for standard RL.

AI.tldr: RCFG enables test-time optimization of novel reward functions via guidance sampling, eliminating need for retraining when rewards change.
AI.motivation: Current methods require retraining models via reinforcement learning when reward functions change, which is inefficient and time-consuming. There's a need for methods that can optimize novel reward functions at test time without retraining.
AI.method: Proposes reward weighted classifier-free guidance (RCFG) as a policy improvement operator that approximates tilting the sampling distribution by the Q function. RCFG can be applied at inference time to optimize novel reward functions without retraining.
AI.result: Demonstrates RCFG's effectiveness in molecular generation, showing it can optimize novel reward functions at test time. Also shows that using RCFG as a teacher and distilling into the base policy significantly speeds up convergence for standard RL.
AI.conclusion: RCFG provides an efficient alternative to retraining when reward functions change, enabling test-time optimization and serving as a valuable warm start for traditional RL methods.

title: Using Large Language Models and Knowledge Graphs to Improve the Interpretability of Machine Learning Models in Manufacturing
summary: Explaining Machine Learning (ML) results in a transparent and user-friendly manner remains a challenging task of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI). In this paper, we present a method to enhance the interpretability of ML models by using a Knowledge Graph (KG). We store domain-specific data along with ML results and their corresponding explanations, establishing a structured connection between domain knowledge and ML insights. To make these insights accessible to users, we designed a selective retrieval method in which relevant triplets are extracted from the KG and processed by a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate user-friendly explanations of ML results. We evaluated our method in a manufacturing environment using the XAI Question Bank. Beyond standard questions, we introduce more complex, tailored questions that highlight the strengths of our approach. We evaluated 33 questions, analyzing responses using quantitative metrics such as accuracy and consistency, as well as qualitative ones such as clarity and usefulness. Our contribution is both theoretical and practical: from a theoretical perspective, we present a novel approach for effectively enabling LLMs to dynamically access a KG in order to improve the explainability of ML results. From a practical perspective, we provide empirical evidence showing that such explanations can be successfully applied in real-world manufacturing environments, supporting better decision-making in manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: Enhances ML interpretability using a Knowledge Graph and LLMs to generate user-friendly explanations, validated in manufacturing with XAI Question Bank
AI.motivation: Explaining ML results in a transparent and user-friendly manner remains challenging in XAI. There's a need to make ML insights accessible to users by connecting domain knowledge with ML explanations.
AI.method: Stores domain-specific data with ML results and explanations in a Knowledge Graph, then uses a selective retrieval method to extract relevant triplets which are processed by an LLM to generate user-friendly explanations.
AI.result: Evaluated 33 questions using XAI Question Bank in manufacturing environment. Analyzed responses with quantitative metrics (accuracy, consistency) and qualitative metrics (clarity, usefulness). Showed that such explanations can be successfully applied in real-world manufacturing environments.
AI.conclusion: The approach is both theoretical and practical: theoretically presents novel method for LLMs to dynamically access KGs to improve ML explainability; practically provides empirical evidence for successful application in manufacturing to support better decision-making.

title: PAWN: Piece Value Analysis with Neural Networks
summary: Predicting the relative value of any given chess piece in a position remains an open challenge, as a piece's contribution depends on its spatial relationships with every other piece on the board. We demonstrate that incorporating the state of the full chess board via latent position representations derived using a CNN-based autoencoder significantly improves accuracy for MLP-based piece value prediction architectures. Using a dataset of over 12 million piece-value pairs gathered from Grandmaster-level games, with ground-truth labels generated by Stockfish 17, our enhanced piece value predictor significantly outperforms context-independent MLP-based systems, reducing validation mean absolute error by 16% and predicting relative piece value within approximately 0.65 pawns. More generally, our findings suggest that encoding the full problem state as context provides useful inductive bias for predicting the contribution of any individual component.

AI.tldr: CNN-based autoencoder with full board context improves chess piece value prediction accuracy by 16% over context-independent models.
AI.motivation: Predicting relative chess piece values is challenging because a piece's contribution depends on its spatial relationships with all other pieces on the board. Current approaches often lack full board context.
AI.method: Used a CNN-based autoencoder to create latent position representations of the full chess board, which were then incorporated into MLP-based piece value prediction architectures. Trained on over 12 million piece-value pairs from Grandmaster-level games with Stockfish 17 ground-truth labels.
AI.result: The enhanced piece value predictor significantly outperformed context-independent MLP-based systems, reducing validation mean absolute error by 16% and achieving prediction accuracy within approximately 0.65 pawns.
AI.conclusion: Encoding the full problem state as context provides useful inductive bias for predicting the contribution of individual components, with implications extending beyond chess to other complex systems.

title: ASMR-Bench: Auditing for Sabotage in ML Research
summary: As AI systems are increasingly used to conduct research autonomously, misaligned systems could introduce subtle flaws that produce misleading results while evading detection. We introduce ASMR-Bench (Auditing for Sabotage in ML Research), a benchmark for evaluating the ability of auditors to detect sabotage in ML research codebases. ASMR-Bench consists of 9 ML research codebases with sabotaged variants that produce qualitatively different experimental results. Each sabotage modifies implementation details, such as hyperparameters, training data, or evaluation code, while preserving the high-level methodology described in the paper. We evaluated frontier LLMs and LLM-assisted human auditors on ASMR-Bench and found that both struggled to reliably detect sabotage: the best performance was an AUROC of 0.77 and a top-1 fix rate of 42%, achieved by Gemini 3.1 Pro. We also tested LLMs as red teamers and found that LLM-generated sabotages were weaker than human-generated ones but still sometimes evaded same-capability LLM auditors. We release ASMR-Bench to support research on monitoring and auditing techniques for AI-conducted research.

AI.tldr: ASMR-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating auditors' ability to detect subtle sabotage in ML research codebases, showing current LLMs and human auditors struggle to reliably identify these flaws.
AI.motivation: As AI systems increasingly conduct autonomous research, misaligned systems could introduce subtle flaws that produce misleading results while evading detection, creating a need for effective auditing methods.
AI.method: Created 9 ML research codebases with sabotaged variants that modify implementation details (hyperparameters, training data, evaluation code) while preserving high-level methodology. Evaluated frontier LLMs and LLM-assisted human auditors on detection capability.
AI.result: Both LLMs and human auditors struggled: best performance was Gemini 3.1 Pro with AUROC of 0.77 and top-1 fix rate of 42%. LLM-generated sabotages were weaker than human-generated ones but still sometimes evaded same-capability LLM auditors.
AI.conclusion: Current auditing capabilities are insufficient for reliably detecting subtle sabotage in AI-conducted research, highlighting the need for improved monitoring and auditing techniques.

title: Adapting in the Dark: Efficient and Stable Test-Time Adaptation for Black-Box Models
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) for black-box models accessible only via APIs remains a largely unexplored challenge. Existing approaches such as post-hoc output refinement offer limited adaptive capacity, while Zeroth-Order Optimization (ZOO) enables input-space adaptation but faces high query costs and optimization challenges in the unsupervised TTA setting. We introduce BETA (Black-box Efficient Test-time Adaptation), a framework that addresses these limitations by employing a lightweight, local white-box steering model to create a tractable gradient pathway. Through a prediction harmonization technique combined with consistency regularization and prompt learning-oriented filtering, BETA enables stable adaptation with no additional API calls and negligible latency beyond standard inference. On ImageNet-C, BETA achieves a +7.1% accuracy gain on ViT-B/16 and +3.4% on CLIP, surpassing strong white-box and gray-box methods including TENT and TPT. On a commercial API, BETA achieves comparable performance to ZOO at 250x lower cost while maintaining real-time inference speed, establishing it as a practical and efficient solution for real-world black-box TTA.

AI.tldr: BETA is a black-box test-time adaptation framework that uses a lightweight local steering model to create gradient pathways without additional API calls, achieving significant accuracy gains with 250x lower cost than previous methods.
AI.motivation: Current test-time adaptation (TTA) approaches for black-box models accessible only via APIs are inadequate - post-hoc refinement offers limited adaptation capacity, while zeroth-order optimization (ZOO) has high query costs and optimization challenges in unsupervised TTA settings.
AI.method: BETA employs a lightweight local white-box steering model to create tractable gradient pathways. It combines prediction harmonization with consistency regularization and prompt learning-oriented filtering, enabling stable adaptation without additional API calls and minimal latency beyond standard inference.
AI.result: On ImageNet-C, BETA achieves +7.1% accuracy gain on ViT-B/16 and +3.4% on CLIP, surpassing strong white-box and gray-box methods including TENT and TPT. On a commercial API, it achieves comparable performance to ZOO at 250x lower cost while maintaining real-time inference speed.
AI.conclusion: BETA establishes itself as a practical and efficient solution for real-world black-box test-time adaptation, addressing key limitations of existing approaches while maintaining computational efficiency and competitive performance.

title: VoodooNet: Achieving Analytic Ground States via High-Dimensional Random Projections
summary: We present VoodooNet, a non-iterative neural architecture that replaces the stochastic gradient descent (SGD) paradigm with a closed-form analytic solution via Galactic Expansion. By projecting input manifolds into a high-dimensional, high-entropy "Galactic" space ($d \gg 784$), we demonstrate that complex features can be untangled without the thermodynamic cost of backpropagation. Utilizing the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to solve for the output layer in a single step, VoodooNet achieves a classification accuracy of \textbf{98.10\% on MNIST} and \textbf{86.63\% on Fashion-MNIST}. Notably, our results on Fashion-MNIST surpass a 10-epoch SGD baseline (84.41\%) while reducing the training time by orders of magnitude. We observe a near-logarithmic scaling law between dimensionality and accuracy, suggesting that performance is a function of "Galactic" volume rather than iterative refinement. This "Magic Hat" approach offers a new frontier for real-time Edge AI, where the traditional training phase is bypassed in favor of instantaneous manifold discovery.

AI.tldr: VoodooNet is a non-iterative neural architecture that replaces SGD with a closed-form solution via Galactic Expansion, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST without traditional training.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the computational inefficiency of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and backpropagation in neural networks. Traditional training requires iterative updates with significant thermodynamic costs, which limits real-time Edge AI applications. The authors aim to bypass the training phase entirely by developing an instantaneous learning approach.
AI.method: The core method is Galactic Expansion - projecting input manifolds into a high-dimensional, high-entropy "Galactic" space (d >> 784). Instead of iterative SGD, they use the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to solve for the output layer weights in a single step. This creates a "Magic Hat" approach where complex features are untangled through dimensionality rather than iterative refinement.
AI.result: VoodooNet achieves 98.10% accuracy on MNIST and 86.63% on Fashion-MNIST. The Fashion-MNIST result surpasses a 10-epoch SGD baseline (84.41%) while reducing training time by orders of magnitude. The authors observe a near-logarithmic scaling law between dimensionality and accuracy, suggesting performance depends on "Galactic" volume rather than iterative training.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that performance in neural networks can be achieved through high-dimensional feature space expansion rather than iterative training. This "Magic Hat" approach offers a new paradigm for real-time Edge AI where traditional training phases can be bypassed in favor of instantaneous manifold discovery, potentially revolutionizing how neural networks are designed and deployed.

title: Flexible Empowerment at Reasoning with Extended Best-of-N Sampling
