summary: This paper proposes a novel method that incorporates empowerment when reasoning actions in reinforcement learning (RL), thereby achieving the flexibility of exploration-exploitation dilemma (EED). In previous methods, empowerment for promoting exploration has been provided as a bonus term to the task-specific reward function as an intrinsically-motivated RL. However, this approach introduces a delay until the policy that accounts for empowerment is learned, making it difficult to adjust the emphasis on exploration as needed. On the other hand, a trick devised for fine-tuning recent foundation models at reasoning, so-called best-of-N (BoN) sampling, allows for the implicit acquisition of modified policies without explicitly learning them. It is expected that applying this trick to exploration-promoting terms, such as empowerment, will enable more flexible adjustment of EED. Therefore, this paper investigates BoN sampling for empowerment. Furthermore, to adjust the degree of policy modification in a generalizable manner while maintaining computational cost, this paper proposes a novel BoN sampling method extended by Tsalis statistics. Through toy problems, the proposed method's cability to balance EED is verified. In addition, it is demonstrated that the proposed method improves RL performance to solve complex locomotion tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel method that uses best-of-N sampling with empowerment to flexibly handle the exploration-exploitation dilemma in reinforcement learning, achieving better performance on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional empowerment methods in RL add exploration bonuses as intrinsic rewards, but this approach introduces learning delays and makes it difficult to dynamically adjust exploration emphasis. The authors want to achieve more flexible control over the exploration-exploitation balance.
AI.method: The paper proposes using best-of-N (BoN) sampling - a technique from foundation model fine-tuning - to incorporate empowerment. Instead of adding empowerment as a reward bonus, BoN sampling implicitly acquires modified policies. The authors further extend BoN sampling using Tsalis statistics to allow generalizable adjustment of policy modification while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.result: Through toy problems, the method demonstrates effective balancing of the exploration-exploitation dilemma. Furthermore, it improves RL performance on complex locomotion tasks compared to traditional empowerment approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BoN sampling approach with empowerment provides a more flexible and effective way to handle exploration-exploitation trade-offs in RL, overcoming limitations of traditional intrinsic reward methods while maintaining computational feasibility through Tsalis statistics extension.

title: Majority Voting for Code Generation
summary: We investigate Functional Majority Voting (FMV), a method based on functional consensus for code generation with Large Language Models, which identifies a representative solution from multiple generations using their runtime execution signatures on test inputs. We find that FMV is an effective test-time inference strategy, substantially boosting performance on LiveCodeBench without a large compute overhead. Furthermore, we extend the utility of functional consensus and apply it as an aggregation strategy for label-free Test-Time Reinforcement Learning. We demonstrate that this increases pass@1 on holdout tasks, but find no evidence of self-improvement beyond the base model's performance ceiling.

AI.tldr: FMV uses functional consensus on execution signatures to select representative code solutions, boosting performance without high compute costs, and extends to label-free Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for aggregation.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of improving code generation with LLMs by finding a way to identify the best solution from multiple generations without relying on expensive compute resources or needing labeled test data.
AI.method: Functional Majority Voting (FMV) analyzes runtime execution signatures of multiple code generations on test inputs to identify a representative solution through functional consensus. It also extends this approach as an aggregation strategy for label-free Test-Time Reinforcement Learning.
AI.result: FMV substantially boosts performance on LiveCodeBench without large compute overhead. When applied to Test-Time Reinforcement Learning, it increases pass@1 on holdout tasks but does not enable self-improvement beyond the base model's performance ceiling.
AI.conclusion: FMV is an effective test-time inference strategy for code generation that leverages functional consensus, offering performance gains with manageable compute costs, though it doesn't enable models to surpass their inherent performance limitations.

title: PINNACLE: An Open-Source Computational Framework for Classical and Quantum PINNs
summary: We present PINNACLE, an open-source computational framework for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrates modern training strategies, multi-GPU acceleration, and hybrid quantum-classical architectures within a unified modular workflow. The framework enables systematic evaluation of PINN performance across benchmark problems including 1D hyperbolic conservation laws, incompressible flows, and electromagnetic wave propagation. It supports a range of architectural and training enhancements, including Fourier feature embeddings, random weight factorization, strict boundary condition enforcement, adaptive loss balancing, curriculum training, and second-order optimization strategies, with extensibility to additional methods. We provide a comprehensive benchmark study quantifying the impact of these methods on convergence, accuracy, and computational cost, and analyze distributed data parallel scaling in terms of runtime and memory efficiency. In addition, we extend the framework to hybrid quantum-classical PINNs and derive a formal estimate for circuit-evaluation complexity under parameter-shift differentiation. Results highlight the sensitivity of PINNs to architectural and training choices, confirm their high computational cost relative to classical solvers, and identify regimes where hybrid quantum models offer improved parameter efficiency. PINNACLE provides a foundation for benchmarking physics-informed learning methods and guiding future developments through quantitative assessment of their trade-offs.

AI.tldr: PINNACLE is an open-source computational framework for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrates modern training strategies, multi-GPU acceleration, and hybrid quantum-classical architectures with comprehensive benchmarking capabilities.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified modular framework for systematic evaluation of PINN performance across benchmark problems, and to enable quantitative assessment of architectural and training enhancements for physics-informed learning methods.
AI.method: Developed an open-source framework supporting Fourier feature embeddings, random weight factorization, strict boundary condition enforcement, adaptive loss balancing, curriculum training, second-order optimization, multi-GPU acceleration, and extensibility to hybrid quantum-classical architectures with formal complexity analysis.
AI.result: Comprehensive benchmark study quantifies impact of methods on convergence, accuracy, and computational cost; distributed scaling analysis shows runtime and memory efficiency; hybrid quantum-classical PINNs demonstrate improved parameter efficiency in certain regimes.
AI.conclusion: PINNACLE provides a foundation for benchmarking physics-informed learning methods, highlighting sensitivity to architectural/training choices, high computational cost relative to classical solvers, and guiding future developments through quantitative trade-off assessment.

title: Stargazer: A Scalable Model-Fitting Benchmark Environment for AI Agents under Astrophysical Constraints
summary: The rise of autonomous AI agents suggests that dynamic benchmark environments with built-in feedback on scientifically grounded tasks are needed to evaluate the capabilities of these agents in research work. We introduce Stargazer, a scalable environment for evaluating AI agents on dynamic, iterative physics-grounded model-fitting tasks using inference on radial-velocity (RV) time series data. Stargazer comprises 120 tasks across three difficulty tiers, including 20 real archival cases, covering diverse scenarios ranging from high-SNR single-planet systems to complex multi-planetary configurations requiring involved low-SNR analysis. Our evaluation of eight frontier agents reveals a gap between numerical optimization and adherence to physical constraints: although agents often achieve a good statistical fit, they frequently fail to recover correct physical system parameters, a limitation that persists even when agents are equipped with vanilla skills. Furthermore, increasing test-time compute yields only marginal gains, with excessive token usage often reflecting recursive failure loops rather than meaningful exploration. Stargazer presents an opportunity to train, evaluate, scaffold, and scale strategies on a model-fitting problem of practical research relevance today. Our methodology to design a simulation-driven environment for AI agents presumably generalizes to many other model-fitting problems across scientific domains. Source code and the project website are available at https://github.com/Gudmorning2025/Stargazer and https://gudmorning2025.github.io/Stargazer, respectively.

AI.tldr: Stargazer is a dynamic benchmark environment for evaluating AI agents on physics-grounded model-fitting tasks using radial-velocity time series data, revealing that current agents achieve good statistical fits but fail to recover correct physical parameters.
AI.motivation: The rise of autonomous AI agents requires dynamic benchmark environments with built-in feedback on scientifically grounded tasks to properly evaluate these agents' capabilities in research work, particularly for physics-grounded model-fitting problems.
AI.method: Created Stargazer, a scalable environment with 120 tasks across three difficulty tiers (including 20 real archival cases) using radial-velocity time series data for model-fitting. Tasks range from high-SNR single-planet systems to complex multi-planetary configurations requiring low-SNR analysis.
AI.result: Evaluation of eight frontier AI agents revealed a gap between numerical optimization and physical constraint adherence: agents often achieve good statistical fits but fail to recover correct physical system parameters, even with vanilla skills. Increased test-time compute yields marginal gains, with excessive token usage indicating recursive failure loops.
AI.conclusion: Stargazer provides a valuable environment for training, evaluating, and scaling strategies on practical model-fitting problems, and the methodology likely generalizes to other scientific model-fitting domains.

title: NK-GAD: Neighbor Knowledge-Enhanced Unsupervised Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection aims to identify irregular patterns in graph-structured data. Most unsupervised GNN-based methods rely on the homophily assumption that connected nodes share similar attributes. However, real-world graphs often exhibit attribute-level heterophily, where connected nodes have dissimilar attributes. Our analysis of attribute-level heterophily graphs reveals two phenomena indicating that current approaches are not practical for unsupervised graph anomaly detection: 1) attribute similarities between connected nodes show nearly identical distributions across different connected node pair types, and 2) anomalies cause consistent variation trends between the graph with and without anomalous edges in the low- and high-frequency components of the spectral energy distributions, while the mid-part exhibits more erratic variations. Based on these observations, we propose NK-GAD, a neighbor knowledge-enhanced unsupervised graph anomaly detection framework. NK-GAD integrates a joint encoder capturing both similar and dissimilar neighbor features, a neighbor reconstruction module modeling normal distributions, a center aggregation module refining node features, and dual decoders for reconstructing attributes and structures. Experiments on seven datasets show NK-GAD achieves an average 3.29\% AUC improvement.

AI.tldr: NK-GAD: A neighbor knowledge-enhanced unsupervised graph anomaly detection framework that addresses attribute-level heterophily by capturing both similar and dissimilar neighbor features, outperforming existing methods on seven datasets.
AI.motivation: Current unsupervised GNN-based methods rely on homophily assumptions, but real-world graphs often exhibit attribute-level heterophily where connected nodes have dissimilar attributes. Analysis reveals that attribute similarities between connected nodes show nearly identical distributions across different pair types, and anomalies cause consistent variation trends in spectral energy distributions that current approaches fail to capture.
AI.method: NK-GAD includes: 1) A joint encoder capturing both similar and dissimilar neighbor features, 2) A neighbor reconstruction module modeling normal distributions, 3) A center aggregation module refining node features, and 4) Dual decoders for reconstructing attributes and structures.
AI.result: Experiments on seven datasets show NK-GAD achieves an average 3.29% AUC improvement over existing methods, demonstrating superior performance in unsupervised graph anomaly detection on heterophilous graphs.
AI.conclusion: NK-GAD effectively addresses the limitations of current approaches for graph anomaly detection on heterophilous graphs by integrating neighbor knowledge enhancement, demonstrating the importance of considering both similar and dissimilar neighbor features in practical applications.

title: Faster LLM Inference via Sequential Monte Carlo
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates language model inference by drafting tokens from a cheap proposal model and verifying them against an expensive target model via rejection sampling. Because rejection truncates the draft block at the first error, throughput degrades when draft and target diverge. Rather than rejecting draft tokens outright, we propose to reweight them. To this end, we introduce sequential Monte Carlo speculative decoding (SMC-SD), which replaces token-level rejection with importance-weighted resampling over a population of draft particles. SMC-SD is a principled approximate inference scheme that trades exactness for additional speed, while preserving theoretical bounds on its per-step approximation error. Because LLM inference is memory bandwidth-bound, the arithmetic needed to draft particles and to score them in parallel comes nearly for free -- SMC-SD uses idle compute to turn verification into a vectorized, fixed-size operation with no rollback. Empirically, SMC-SD achieves 2.36x speed-up over speculative decoding and a 5.2x speed-up over autoregressive decoding, while remaining within 3% of the target model's accuracy on reasoning, instruction-following, and coding benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SMC-SD replaces speculative decoding's token-level rejection with importance-weighted resampling over multiple draft particles, achieving 2.36x speed-up over speculative decoding while maintaining target model accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard speculative decoding suffers from throughput degradation when draft and target models diverge because rejection truncates the draft block at the first error. The authors want to address this limitation by proposing a more flexible approach that doesn't reject draft tokens outright but reweights them.
AI.method: Introduces Sequential Monte Carlo Speculative Decoding (SMC-SD), which replaces token-level rejection with importance-weighted resampling over a population of draft particles. It's a principled approximate inference scheme that uses idle compute to make verification a vectorized, fixed-size operation with no rollback, leveraging that LLM inference is memory bandwidth-bound.
AI.result: Achieves 2.36x speed-up over speculative decoding and 5.2x speed-up over autoregressive decoding, while remaining within 3% of the target model's accuracy on reasoning, instruction-following, and coding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SMC-SD is an effective method that trades exactness for additional speed while preserving theoretical bounds on per-step approximation error, making it a promising approach for accelerating language model inference.

title: Hierarchical Active Inference using Successor Representations
comment: Accepted for publication in Neural Computation (MIT Press). 82 pages, 29 figures
summary: Active inference, a neurally-inspired model for inferring actions based on the free energy principle (FEP), has been proposed as a unifying framework for understanding perception, action, and learning in the brain. Active inference has previously been used to model ecologically important tasks such as navigation and planning, but scaling it to solve complex large-scale problems in real-world environments has remained a challenge. Inspired by the existence of multi-scale hierarchical representations in the brain, we propose a model for planning of actions based on hierarchical active inference. Our approach combines a hierarchical model of the environment with successor representations for efficient planning. We present results demonstrating (1) how lower-level successor representations can be used to learn higher-level abstract states, (2) how planning based on active inference at the lower-level can be used to bootstrap and learn higher-level abstract actions, and (3) how these learned higher-level abstract states and actions can facilitate efficient planning. We illustrate the performance of the approach on several planning and reinforcement learning (RL) problems including a variant of the well-known four rooms task, a key-based navigation task, a partially observable planning problem, the Mountain Car problem, and PointMaze, a family of navigation tasks with continuous state and action spaces. Our results represent, to our knowledge, the first application of learned hierarchical state and action abstractions to active inference in FEP-based theories of brain function.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hierarchical active inference model that combines hierarchical environment modeling with successor representations to enable efficient planning in complex real-world tasks, addressing the scalability limitations of traditional active inference approaches.
AI.motivation: Active inference, while promising as a brain-inspired framework for understanding perception and action, struggles to scale to complex real-world problems. The paper is motivated by the brain's multi-scale hierarchical representations and aims to develop a hierarchical approach to overcome these scalability limitations.
AI.method: The authors propose a hierarchical active inference model that combines: (1) hierarchical environment modeling, (2) successor representations for efficient planning, (3) using lower-level successor representations to learn higher-level abstract states, (4) bootstrapping higher-level abstract actions from lower-level active inference planning, and (5) leveraging learned hierarchical abstractions for efficient planning.
AI.result: The model successfully demonstrates: (1) learning higher-level abstract states from lower-level successor representations, (2) learning higher-level abstract actions through lower-level active inference planning, and (3) enabling efficient planning through hierarchical abstractions. The approach is validated on diverse tasks including four rooms, key-based navigation, partially observable planning, Mountain Car, and PointMaze with continuous spaces.
AI.conclusion: This work represents the first application of learned hierarchical state and action abstractions to active inference within FEP-based theories of brain function, successfully addressing scalability challenges and enabling efficient planning in complex environments through brain-inspired hierarchical representations.

title: Neural Continuous-Time Markov Chain: Discrete Diffusion via Decoupled Jump Timing and Direction
summary: Discrete diffusion models based on continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) have shown strong performance on language and discrete data generation, yet existing approaches typically parameterize the reverse rate matrix as a single object -- via concrete scores, clean-data predictions ($x_0$-parameterization), or denoising distributions -- rather than aligning the parameterization with the intrinsic CTMC decomposition into jump timing and jump direction. Since a CTMC is fundamentally a Poisson process fully determined by these two quantities, decomposing along this structure is closer to first principles and naturally leads to our formulation. We propose \textbf{Neural CTMC}, which separately parameterizes the reverse process through an \emph{exit rate} (when to jump) and a \emph{jump distribution} (where to jump) using two dedicated network heads. We show that the evidence lower bound (ELBO) differs from a path-space KL divergence between the true and learned reverse processes by a $θ$-independent constant, so that the training objective is fully governed by the exit rate and jump distribution we parameterize. Moreover, this KL factorizes into a Poisson KL for timing and a categorical KL for direction. We further show that the tractable conditional surrogate preserves the gradients and minimizers of the corresponding marginal reverse-process objective under standard regularity assumptions. Our theoretical framework also covers masked and GIDD-style noise schedules. Empirically, while the uniform forward process has been explored in prior work, our model, to our best of the knowledge, is the first pure-uniform method to outperform mask-based methods on the OpenWebText dataset.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our pretrained weights at https://huggingface.co/Jiangxy1117/Neural-CTMC.

AI.tldr: Neural CTMC proposes a novel discrete diffusion model that parameterizes the reverse process through separate exit rate (when to jump) and jump distribution (where to jump) heads, aligning with CTMC decomposition principles.
AI.motivation: Existing discrete diffusion models parameterize the reverse rate matrix as a single object rather than aligning with the intrinsic CTMC decomposition into jump timing and direction, which is more fundamental to continuous-time Markov chains.
AI.method: Neural CTMC uses two dedicated network heads to separately parameterize: 1) exit rate (when to jump), and 2) jump distribution (where to jump), directly corresponding to the Poisson process structure of CTMCs.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows the ELBO aligns with path-space KL divergence, which factorizes into Poisson KL for timing and categorical KL for direction. Empirically, it's the first pure-uniform method to outperform mask-based methods on OpenWebText dataset.
AI.conclusion: By decomposing CTMC reverse processes along first principles (timing + direction), Neural CTMC achieves stronger theoretical grounding and empirical performance, establishing a new framework for discrete diffusion models.

title: Graph self-supervised learning based on frequency corruption
comment: 11 pages, 4 tables, 3 figures. Accepted at The ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW 2026)
summary: Graph self-supervised learning can reduce the need for labeled graph data and has been widely used in recommendation, social networks, and other web applications. However, existing methods often underuse high-frequency signals and may overfit to specific local patterns, which limits representation quality and generalization. We propose Frequency-Corrupt Based Graph Self-Supervised Learning (FC-GSSL), a method that builds corrupted graphs biased toward high-frequency information by corrupting nodes and edges according to their low-frequency contributions. These corrupted graphs are used as inputs to an autoencoder, while low-frequency and general features are reconstructed as supervision targets, forcing the model to fuse information from multiple frequency bands. We further design multiple sampling strategies and generate diverse corrupted graphs from the intersections and unions of the sampling results. By aligning node representations from these views, the model can discover useful frequency combinations, reduce reliance on specific high-frequency components, and improve robustness. Experiments on 14 datasets across node classification, graph prediction, and transfer learning show that FC-GSSL consistently improves performance and generalization.

AI.tldr: FC-GSSL is a graph self-supervised learning method that uses frequency-aware corruption to create biased graphs emphasizing high-frequency information, forcing the model to fuse multiple frequency bands and improving generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing graph self-supervised learning methods often underuse high-frequency signals and may overfit to specific local patterns, limiting representation quality and generalization across web applications like recommendation systems and social networks.
AI.method: The method corrupts graphs by targeting nodes and edges based on their low-frequency contributions, creating corrupted graphs biased toward high-frequency information. These corrupted graphs serve as autoencoder inputs while reconstructing low-frequency and general features as supervision targets. Multiple sampling strategies generate diverse corrupted graphs from intersections/unions of sampling results, and node representations are aligned across these views.
AI.result: Experiments on 14 datasets across node classification, graph prediction, and transfer learning tasks demonstrate that FC-GSSL consistently improves performance and generalization compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FC-GSSL effectively addresses limitations of existing graph SSL methods by forcing frequency fusion through targeted corruption, reducing overfitting to specific high-frequency components, and discovering useful frequency combinations, leading to better generalization across diverse graph tasks.

title: Towards Robust Endogenous Reasoning: Unifying Drift Adaptation in Non-Stationary Tuning
summary: Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) has established itself as a critical paradigm for the alignment of Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with complex human values and domain-specific requirements. Nevertheless, current research primarily focuses on mitigating exogenous distribution shifts arising from data-centric factors, the non-stationarity inherent in the endogenous reasoning remains largely unexplored. In this work, a critical vulnerability is revealed within MLLMs: they are highly susceptible to endogenous reasoning drift, across both thinking and perception perspectives. It manifests as unpredictable distribution changes that emerge spontaneously during the autoregressive generation process, independent of external environmental perturbations. To adapt it, we first theoretically define endogenous reasoning drift within the RFT of MLLMs as the multi-modal concept drift. In this context, this paper proposes Counterfactual Preference Optimization ++ (CPO++), a comprehensive and autonomous framework adapted to the multi-modal concept drift. It integrates counterfactual reasoning with domain knowledge to execute controlled perturbations across thinking and perception, employing preference optimization to disentangle spurious correlations. Extensive empirical evaluations across two highly dynamic and safety-critical domains: medical diagnosis and autonomous driving. They demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior performance in reasoning coherence, decision-making precision, and inherent robustness against extreme interference. The methodology also exhibits exceptional zero-shot cross-domain generalization, providing a principled foundation for reliable multi-modal reasoning in safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive framework called CPO++ addresses the vulnerability of multi-modal LLMs to endogenous reasoning drift during autoregressive generation by integrating counterfactual reasoning with preference optimization across thinking and perception modalities.
AI.motivation: Current RFT research focuses on exogenous distribution shifts from data factors, but ignores the non-stationarity in endogenous reasoning. The paper reveals a critical vulnerability: MLLMs are highly susceptible to endogenous reasoning drift that emerges spontaneously during generation, independent of external perturbations.
AI.method: The paper proposes Counterfactual Preference Optimization++ (CPO++), a comprehensive framework that adapts to multi-modal concept drift. It integrates counterfactual reasoning with domain knowledge to execute controlled perturbations across thinking and perception modalities, employing preference optimization to disentangle spurious correlations.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations in medical diagnosis and autonomous driving domains demonstrate superior performance in reasoning coherence, decision-making precision, and inherent robustness against extreme interference. The method also shows exceptional zero-shot cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: CPO++ provides a principled foundation for reliable multi-modal reasoning in safety-critical applications by addressing the previously unexplored problem of endogenous reasoning drift in MLLMs through a comprehensive counterfactual preference optimization framework.

title: Reasoning-targeted Jailbreak Attacks on Large Reasoning Models via Semantic Triggers and Psychological Framing
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in generating step-by-step reasoning chains alongside final answers, enabling their deployment in high-stakes domains such as healthcare and education. While prior jailbreak attack studies have focused on the safety of final answers, little attention has been given to the safety of the reasoning process. In this work, we identify a novel problem that injects harmful content into the reasoning steps while preserving unchanged answers. This type of attack presents two key challenges: 1) manipulating the input instructions may inadvertently alter the LRM's final answer, and 2) the diversity of input questions makes it difficult to consistently bypass the LRM's safety alignment mechanisms and embed harmful content into its reasoning process. To address these challenges, we propose the Psychology-based Reasoning-targeted Jailbreak Attack (PRJA) Framework, which integrates a Semantic-based Trigger Selection module and a Psychology-based Instruction Generation module. Specifically, the proposed PRJA automatically selects manipulative reasoning triggers via semantic analysis and leverages psychological theories of obedience to authority and moral disengagement to generate adaptive instructions for enhancing the LRM's compliance with harmful content generation. Extensive experiments on five question-answering datasets demonstrate that PRJA achieves an average attack success rate of 83.6\% against several commercial LRMs, including DeepSeek R1, Qwen2.5-Max, and OpenAI o4-mini.

AI.tldr: A new jailbreak attack framework called PRJA that injects harmful content into reasoning steps of Large Reasoning Models while keeping final answers unchanged, achieving high success rates against commercial models.
AI.motivation: Existing jailbreak research focuses only on final answer safety, ignoring potential harm in reasoning steps. LRMs are used in high-stakes domains where unsafe reasoning could be dangerous even if answers appear correct.
AI.method: PRJA Framework with two modules: Semantic-based Trigger Selection (analyzes semantics to find manipulative reasoning triggers) and Psychology-based Instruction Generation (uses theories of obedience to authority and moral disengagement to create adaptive instructions that bypass safety alignment).
AI.result: PRJA achieves average 83.6% attack success rate against commercial LRMs including DeepSeek R1, Qwen2.5-Max, and OpenAI o4-mini across five question-answering datasets, demonstrating effectiveness of psychology-based attacks.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning steps in LRMs are vulnerable to targeted attacks, current safety measures are insufficient against psychology-based manipulation, and new defense mechanisms focusing on reasoning safety are needed for secure LRM deployment.

title: Why Colors Make Clustering Harder:Global Integrality Gaps, the Price of Fairness, and Color-Coupled Algorithms in Chromatic Correlation Clustering
summary:
Chromatic Correlation Clustering (CCC) extends Correlation Clustering by assigning semantic colors to edges and requiring each cluster to receive a single color label. Unlike standard CC, whose LP relaxation has integrality gap 2 on complete graphs and admits a 2.06-approximation, the analogous LP for CCC has a strict lower bound of 2.11, and the best known LP-rounding algorithm achieves 2.15. We explain this gap by isolating the source of difficulty: cross-edge chromatic interference. Neutral edges, whose color does not match the candidate cluster color, create an irreducible cost absent from standard CC and force any color-independent rounding scheme to pay an additional mismatch penalty.
  We make four contributions. First, we prove a Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem showing that the gap of any color-independent CCC rounding algorithm equals the standard CC gap plus an irreducible chromatic penalty Delta(L) > 0. Second, we solve the associated min-max problem and derive the staircase formula Delta(L) = ((L-1)/L) Delta_infinity, where Delta_infinity is approximately 0.0734. In particular, the two-color gap is 2.0967, separating CCC from standard CC already at L = 2. Third, we introduce Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4). Adding the valid global constraint sum_c x_uv^c >= L-1 and a correlated interval-packing rounding scheme makes neutral edges behave like classical negative edges, recovering the optimal 2.06 approximation and bypassing the 2.11 lower bound for the uncoupled LP. Fourth, experiments on extremal instances, real multi-relational networks, and fairness benchmarks validate the theory: empirical LP gaps follow the predicted staircase, and C4 matches the unconstrained approximation ratio under fairness constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes Chromatic Correlation Clustering (CCC), explains its harder approximation gap compared to standard Correlation Clustering via chromatic interference, derives a staircase formula for the gap, and introduces Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4) to bypass the lower bound and achieve optimal approximation.
AI.motivation: Chromatic Correlation Clustering extends standard Correlation Clustering by assigning semantic colors to edges, requiring each cluster to have a single color label. While standard CC has a known 2.06-approximation, CCC faces a worse approximation gap (2.15 vs theoretical lower bound 2.11), creating a gap that needs explanation and solution.
AI.method: 1. Prove Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem showing CCC gap equals standard CC gap plus chromatic penalty. 2. Solve min-max problem to derive staircase formula Δ(L) = ((L-1)/L)Δ∞ for gap. 3. Introduce Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4) with global constraint Σ_c x_uv^c ≥ L-1 and correlated interval-packing rounding to bypass the lower bound. 4. Validate with experiments on extremal instances, real networks, and fairness benchmarks.
AI.result: 1. Identified cross-edge chromatic interference as the difficulty source. 2. Derived precise staircase formula for the chromatic penalty. 3. Showed two-color gap is 2.0967, separating CCC from CC at L=2. 4. C4 achieves optimal 2.06 approximation, bypassing 2.11 lower bound. 5. Experimental validation confirms theoretical predictions and shows C4 matches unconstrained ratio under fairness constraints.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully explains and overcomes the approximation gap in Chromatic Correlation Clustering by isolating chromatic interference as the core difficulty, deriving its mathematical characterization, and introducing C4 to achieve optimal approximation while maintaining practical applicability in real-world and fairness-constrained settings.

title: Collective Kernel EFT for Pre-activation ResNets
summary: In finite-width deep neural networks, the empirical kernel $G$ evolves stochastically across layers. We develop a collective kernel effective field theory (EFT) for pre-activation ResNets based on a $G$-only closure hierarchy and diagnose its finite validity window. Exploiting the exact conditional Gaussianity of residual increments, we derive an exact stochastic recursion for $G$. Applying Gaussian approximations systematically yields a continuous-depth ODE system for the mean kernel $K_0$, the kernel covariance $V_4$, and the $1/n$ mean correction $K_{1,\mathrm{EFT}}$, which emerges diagrammatically as a one-loop tadpole correction. Numerically, $K_0$ remains accurate at all depths. However, the $V_4$ equation residual accumulates to an $O(1)$ error at finite time, primarily driven by approximation errors in the $G$-only transport term. Furthermore, $K_{1,\mathrm{EFT}}$ fails due to the breakdown of the source closure, which exhibits a systematic mismatch even at initialization. These findings highlight the limitations of $G$-only state-space reduction and suggest extending the state space to incorporate the sigma-kernel.

AI.tldr: Developed a kernel effective field theory for pre-activation ResNets using G-only closure, but found limitations in its accuracy for covariance and mean corrections at finite depths.
AI.motivation: In finite-width deep neural networks, the empirical kernel G evolves stochastically across layers, requiring theoretical understanding. The paper aims to develop a collective kernel effective field theory (EFT) for pre-activation ResNets to analyze this stochastic evolution.
AI.method: Developed G-only closure hierarchy for pre-activation ResNets, exploited exact conditional Gaussianity of residual increments to derive exact stochastic recursion for G, applied Gaussian approximations systematically to obtain continuous-depth ODE system for mean kernel K₀, kernel covariance V₄, and 1/n mean correction K₁,EFT.
AI.result: K₀ remains accurate at all depths numerically. However, V₄ equation accumulates O(1) error at finite time due to approximation errors in G-only transport term. K₁,EFT fails due to breakdown of source closure with systematic mismatch even at initialization.
AI.conclusion: The G-only state-space reduction approach has limitations for accurately modeling kernel covariance and mean corrections. Findings suggest extending the state space to incorporate the sigma-kernel for more accurate theoretical descriptions.

title: DepCap: Adaptive Block-Wise Parallel Decoding for Efficient Diffusion LM Inference
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive language generation due to their potential for parallel decoding and global refinement of the entire sequence. To unlock this potential, DLM inference must carefully balance generation quality and decoding speed. Recent block-wise DLM decoding methods improve this trade-off by performing diffusion-based decoding sequentially in blocks. However, existing methods typically rely on fixed block schedules or current-step local signals to determine block boundaries, and use conservative confidence-based parallel decoding to avoid conflicts, limiting the quality-speed trade-off. In this paper, we argue that block-wise DLM inference requires more suitable signals for its two core decisions: cross-step signals for determining block boundaries, and token-level conflict signals for parallel decoding. Based on this view, we propose DepCap, a training-free framework for efficient block-wise DLM inference. Specifically, DepCap instantiates the cross-step signal as the influence of the last decoded block and uses it to adaptively determine how far the next block should extend, while identifying a conflict-free subset of tokens for safe parallel decoding within each block, enabling substantial inference acceleration with negligible quality degradation. DepCap is a plug-and-play method applicable to various DLMs, and compatible with existing KV-cache strategies for block-wise DLM. An information-theoretic analysis further suggests that the cumulative last-block influence on a candidate block is approximately additive across tokens, supporting the proposed block-partitioning criterion. Experimental results show that DepCap achieves favorable speed-quality trade-offs across multiple DLM backbones and reasoning and coding benchmarks, with up to 5.63$\times$ speedup without significant performance degradation.

AI.tldr: DepCap is a training-free framework for efficient block-wise diffusion language model inference that uses cross-step signals for adaptive block partitioning and token-level conflict signals for safe parallel decoding, achieving up to 5.63× speedup with negligible quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models (DLMs) offer parallel decoding and global refinement advantages over autoregressive models, but existing block-wise DLM decoding methods have limitations. They use fixed block schedules or local signals for block boundaries and conservative confidence-based parallel decoding, which restricts the quality-speed trade-off optimization.
AI.method: DepCap uses cross-step signals (specifically the influence of the last decoded block) to adaptively determine how far the next block should extend, while identifying conflict-free token subsets for safe parallel decoding within each block. It's a plug-and-play, training-free framework compatible with existing KV-cache strategies for block-wise DLMs.
AI.result: DepCap achieves favorable speed-quality trade-offs across multiple DLM backbones and reasoning/coding benchmarks, with up to 5.63× speedup without significant performance degradation. An information-theoretic analysis shows that cumulative last-block influence on candidate blocks is approximately additive across tokens.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that using appropriate signals (cross-step signals for block boundaries and token-level conflict signals for parallel decoding) enables more efficient block-wise DLM inference. DepCap provides substantial acceleration while maintaining generation quality, making it a practical solution for deploying diffusion language models.

title: Multi-objective Reinforcement Learning With Augmented States Requires Rewards After Deployment
summary: This research note identifies a previously overlooked distinction between multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL), and more conventional single-objective reinforcement learning (RL). It has previously been noted that the optimal policy for an MORL agent with a non-linear utility function is required to be conditioned on both the current environmental state and on some measure of the previously accrued reward. This is generally implemented by concatenating the observed state of the environment with the discounted sum of previous rewards to create an augmented state. While augmented states have been widely-used in the MORL literature, one implication of their use has not previously been reported -- namely that they require the agent to have continued access to the reward signal (or a proxy thereof) after deployment, even if no further learning is required. This note explains why this is the case, and considers the practical repercussions of this requirement.

AI.tldr: MORL with non-linear utility functions requires tracking previous rewards via augmented states, creating an often-overlooked requirement for continuous reward signal access even after deployment.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies an overlooked distinction between MORL and single-objective RL: while augmented states are commonly used in MORL for non-linear utility functions, their implications haven't been fully understood.
AI.method: Analysis of how MORL agents with non-linear utility functions require policies conditioned on both current state and previous accrued rewards, typically implemented through augmented states concatenating environmental state with discounted reward sums.
AI.result: The key finding is that using augmented states in MORL necessitates continuous access to reward signals (or proxies) after deployment, even when no further learning occurs - a requirement not previously reported in the literature.
AI.conclusion: This previously unreported requirement has practical repercussions for MORL system design and deployment, as agents must maintain reward signal access indefinitely, unlike single-objective RL systems.

title: Zero-Shot Scalable Resilience in UAV Swarms: A Decentralized Imitation Learning Framework with Physics-Informed Graph Interactions
summary: Large-scale Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) failures can split an unmanned aerial vehicle swarm network into disconnected sub-networks, making decentralized recovery both urgent and difficult. Centralized recovery methods depend on global topology information and become communication-heavy after severe fragmentation. Decentralized heuristics and multi-agent reinforcement learning methods are easier to deploy, but their performance often degrades when the swarm scale and damage severity vary. We present Physics-informed Graph Adversarial Imitation Learning algorithm (PhyGAIL) that adopts centralized training with decentralized execution. PhyGAIL builds bounded local interaction graphs from heterogeneous observations, and uses physics-informed graph neural network to encode directional local interactions as gated message passing with explicit attraction and repulsion. This gives the policy a physically grounded coordination bias while keeping local observations scale-invariant. It also uses scenario-adaptive imitation learning to improve training under fragmented topologies and variable-length recovery episodes. Our analysis establishes bounded local graph amplification, bounded interaction dynamics, and controlled variance of the terminal success signal. A policy trained on 20-UAV swarms transfers directly to swarms of up to 500 UAVs without fine-tuning, and achieves better performance across reconnection reliability, recovery speed, motion safety, and runtime efficiency than representative baselines.

AI.tldr: PhyGAIL uses physics-informed graph neural networks with adversarial imitation learning for decentralized UAV swarm recovery after network fragmentation, enabling scale-invariant policies that transfer from 20 to 500 UAVs without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Large-scale UAV failures fragment swarm networks into disconnected sub-networks, making decentralized recovery urgent and difficult. Centralized methods are communication-heavy after severe fragmentation, while decentralized methods degrade when swarm scale and damage severity vary.
AI.method: Physics-informed Graph Adversarial Imitation Learning (PhyGAIL) with centralized training and decentralized execution. It builds bounded local interaction graphs from heterogeneous observations, uses physics-informed GNNs with gated message passing (explicit attraction/repulsion), and employs scenario-adaptive imitation learning for fragmented topologies.
AI.result: Policy trained on 20-UAV swarms transfers directly to swarms up to 500 UAVs without fine-tuning. Outperforms baselines in reconnection reliability, recovery speed, motion safety, and runtime efficiency. Analysis shows bounded local graph amplification, bounded interaction dynamics, and controlled variance of terminal success signal.
AI.conclusion: PhyGAIL provides a physically-grounded, scale-invariant approach for decentralized UAV swarm recovery that generalizes well across varying swarm scales and fragmentation severity, addressing limitations of both centralized and existing decentralized methods.

title: When Do Early-Exit Networks Generalize? A PAC-Bayesian Theory of Adaptive Depth
summary: Early-exit neural networks enable adaptive computation by allowing confident predictions to exit at intermediate layers, achieving 2-8$\times$ inference speedup. Despite widespread deployment, their generalization properties lack theoretical understanding -- a gap explicitly identified in recent surveys. This paper establishes a unified PAC-Bayesian framework for adaptive-depth networks. (1) Novel Entropy-Based Bounds: We prove the first generalization bounds depending on exit-depth entropy $H(D)$ and expected depth $\mathbb{E}[D]$ rather than maximum depth $K$, with sample complexity $\mathcal{O}((\mathbb{E}[D] \cdot d + H(D))/ε^2)$. (2) Explicit Constructive Constants: Our analysis yields the leading coefficient $\sqrt{2\ln 2} \approx 1.177$ with complete derivation. (3) Provable Early-Exit Advantages: We establish sufficient conditions under which adaptive-depth networks strictly outperform fixed-depth counterparts. (4) Extension to Approximate Label Independence: We relax the label-independence assumption to $ε$-approximate policies, broadening applicability to learned routing. (5) Comprehensive Validation: Experiments across 6 architectures on 7 benchmarks demonstrate tightness ratios of 1.52-3.87$\times$ (all $p < 0.001$) versus $>$100$\times$ for classical bounds. Bound-guided threshold selection matches validation-tuned performance within 0.1-0.3%.

AI.tldr: A theoretical analysis establishing PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds for early-exit neural networks, showing they depend on exit-depth entropy rather than maximum depth, with experimental validation showing tight bounds.
AI.motivation: Early-exit neural networks provide 2-8× inference speedup through adaptive computation, but lack theoretical understanding of their generalization properties, which has been explicitly identified as a gap in recent surveys.
AI.method: Developed a unified PAC-Bayesian framework for adaptive-depth networks, proving novel entropy-based generalization bounds depending on exit-depth entropy and expected depth rather than maximum depth, with explicit constructive constants and extension to approximate label independence.
AI.result: Proved generalization bounds with sample complexity O((E[D]·d + H(D))/ε²) with leading coefficient √(2ln2) ≈ 1.177, established sufficient conditions for adaptive-depth networks outperforming fixed-depth ones, and validated with experiments showing tightness ratios of 1.52-3.87× vs >100× for classical bounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first theoretical foundation for early-exit networks with practical, tight generalization bounds that explain why adaptive-depth architectures work well and how to design them effectively, bridging a significant gap in understanding these widely deployed systems.

title: Closing the Theory-Practice Gap in Spiking Transformers via Effective Dimension
summary: Spiking transformers achieve competitive accuracy with conventional transformers while offering $38$-$57\times$ energy efficiency on neuromorphic hardware, yet no theoretical framework guides their design. This paper establishes the first comprehensive expressivity theory for spiking self-attention. We prove that spiking attention with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons is a universal approximator of continuous permutation-equivariant functions, providing explicit spike circuit constructions including a novel lateral inhibition network for softmax normalization with proven $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence. We derive tight spike-count lower bounds via rate-distortion theory: $\varepsilon$-approximation requires $Ω(L_f^2 nd/\varepsilon^2)$ spikes, with rigorous information-theoretic derivation. Our key insight is input-dependent bounds using measured effective dimensions ($d_{\text{eff}}=47$--$89$ for CIFAR/ImageNet), explaining why $T=4$ timesteps suffice despite worst-case $T \geq 10{,}000$ predictions. We provide concrete design rules with calibrated constants ($C=2.3$, 95\% CI: $[1.9, 2.7]$). Experiments on Spikformer, QKFormer, and SpikingResformer across vision and language benchmarks validate predictions with $R^2=0.97$ ($p<0.001$). Our framework provides the first principled foundation for neuromorphic transformer design.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive expressivity theory for spiking transformers proves they can approximate continuous functions, provides circuit constructions with convergence guarantees, establishes input-dependent spike count bounds via rate-distortion theory, and gives concrete design rules validated across vision/language benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Spiking transformers offer 38-57× energy efficiency on neuromorphic hardware but lack theoretical foundation for design guidance, creating a gap between practical benefits and theoretical understanding of their capabilities and limitations.
AI.method: Develop expressivity theory proving spiking attention with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons is universal approximator; construct explicit spike circuits including lateral inhibition network for softmax normalization; derive spike-count lower bounds via rate-distortion theory; analyze input-dependent bounds using measured effective dimensions; provide design rules with calibrated constants.
AI.result: Proved spiking attention can approximate continuous permutation-equivariant functions with O(1/√T) convergence; derived ε-approximation requires Ω(L_f² nd/ε²) spikes; found effective dimensions d_eff=47-89 explain why T=4 timesteps suffice vs worst-case T≥10,000 predictions; validated predictions on Spikformer, QKFormer, SpikingResformer with R²=0.97 (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes the first principled theoretical foundation for neuromorphic transformer design, explaining the efficiency of practical spiking transformers and providing rigorous guidance for future architecture development through comprehensive expressivity theory with empirical validation.

title: Federated Learning with Quantum Enhanced LSTM for Applications in High Energy Physics
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, accepted at IEEE WCCI, 2026
summary: Learning with large-scale datasets and information-critical applications, such as in High Energy Physics (HEP), demands highly complex, large-scale models that are both robust and accurate. To tackle this issue and cater to the learning requirements, we envision using a federated learning framework with a quantum-enhanced model. Specifically, we design a hybrid quantum-classical long-shot-term-memory model (QLSTM) for local training at distributed nodes. It combines the representative power of quantum models in understanding complex relationships within the feature space, and an LSTM-based model to learn necessary correlations across data points. Given the computing limitations and unprecedented cost of current stand-alone noisy-intermediate quantum (NISQ) devices, we propose to use a federated learning setup, where the learning load can be distributed to local servers as per design and data availability. We demonstrate the benefits of such a design on a classification task for the Supersymmetry(SUSY) dataset, having 5M rows. Our experiments indicate that the performance of this design is not only better that some of the existing work using variational quantum circuit (VQC) based quantum machine learning (QML) techniques, but is also comparable ($Δ\sim \pm 1\%$) to that of classical deep-learning benchmarks. An important observation from this study is that the designed framework has $<$300 parameters and only needs 20K data points to give a comparable performance. Which also turns out to be a 100$\times$ improvement than the compared baseline models. This shows an improved learning capability of the proposed framework with minimal data and resource requirements, due to the joint model with an LSTM based architecture and a quantum enhanced VQC.

AI.tldr: Proposes a federated learning framework with hybrid quantum-classical LSTM model for high-energy physics, achieving comparable performance to classical deep learning with minimal parameters and data.
AI.motivation: High Energy Physics (HEP) requires complex, large-scale models that are robust and accurate, but faces challenges with large datasets and limitations of current noisy quantum (NISQ) devices. Need to combine quantum advantages with practical computing constraints.
AI.method: Design a federated learning framework with hybrid quantum-classical long-short-term-memory model (QLSTM) at distributed nodes. Combines quantum models' representational power for complex feature relationships with LSTM-based models for correlations across data points.
AI.result: The framework achieves comparable performance (Δ∼ ±1%) to classical deep-learning benchmarks on SUSY dataset classification. Requires <300 parameters and only 20K data points (100× improvement over baselines). Better than existing VQC-based QML techniques.
AI.conclusion: The proposed federated quantum-classical LSTM framework demonstrates improved learning capability with minimal data and resources, showing practical viability for HEP applications despite current quantum computing limitations.

title: Pruning Unsafe Tickets: A Resource-Efficient Framework for Safer and More Robust LLMs
summary: Machine learning models are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, but even aligned models such as Mistral and LLaVA still exhibit unsafe behaviors inherited from pre-training. Current alignment methods like SFT and RLHF primarily encourage models to generate preferred responses, but do not explicitly remove the unsafe subnetworks that trigger harmful outputs. In this work, we introduce a resource-efficient pruning framework that directly identifies and removes parameters associated with unsafe behaviors while preserving model utility. Our method employs a gradient-free attribution mechanism, requiring only modest GPU resources, and generalizes across architectures and quantized variants. Empirical evaluations on ML models show substantial reductions in unsafe generations and improved robustness against jailbreak attacks, with minimal utility loss. From the perspective of the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, our results suggest that ML models contain "unsafe tickets" responsible for harmful behaviors, and pruning reveals "safety tickets" that maintain performance while aligning outputs. This provides a lightweight, post-hoc alignment strategy suitable for deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: A lightweight pruning framework that identifies and removes unsafe subnetwork parameters in ML models to reduce harmful behaviors while preserving utility, using gradient-free attribution and minimal resources.
AI.motivation: Current alignment methods (SFT, RLHF) only encourage preferred responses but don't explicitly remove unsafe subnetworks that trigger harmful outputs. Aligned models like Mistral and LLaVA still exhibit unsafe behaviors inherited from pre-training.
AI.method: A resource-efficient pruning framework with gradient-free attribution mechanism to directly identify and remove parameters associated with unsafe behaviors. The method generalizes across architectures and quantized variants and requires only modest GPU resources.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show substantial reductions in unsafe generations and improved robustness against jailbreak attacks, with minimal utility loss. The framework works across different ML models.
AI.conclusion: ML models contain "unsafe tickets" responsible for harmful behaviors (per Lottery Ticket Hypothesis), and pruning reveals "safety tickets" that maintain performance while aligning outputs. This provides a lightweight, post-hoc alignment strategy suitable for resource-constrained deployment settings.

title: EVIL: Evolving Interpretable Algorithms for Zero-Shot Inference on Event Sequences and Time Series with LLMs
summary: We introduce EVIL (\textbf{EV}olving \textbf{I}nterpretable algorithms with \textbf{L}LMs), an approach that uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to discover simple, interpretable algorithms for dynamical systems inference. Rather than training neural networks on large datasets, EVIL evolves pure Python/NumPy programs that perform zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets. We apply EVIL to three distinct tasks: next-event prediction in temporal point processes, rate matrix estimation for Markov jump processes, and time series imputation. In each case, a single evolved algorithm generalizes across all evaluation datasets without per-dataset training (analogous to an amortized inference model). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to show that LLM-guided program evolution can discover a single compact inference function for these dynamical-systems problems. Across the three domains, the discovered algorithms are often competitive with, and even outperform, state-of-the-art deep learning models while being orders of magnitudes faster, and remaining fully interpretable.

AI.tldr: EVIL evolves simple Python/NumPy programs for zero-shot dynamical systems inference using LLM-guided evolutionary search, outperforming SOTA deep learning models while being faster and interpretable.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning approaches for dynamical systems inference require large datasets and neural network training, resulting in complex black-box models that lack interpretability and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to evolve pure Python/NumPy programs that perform zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets without per-dataset training (amortized inference).
AI.result: Evolved algorithms generalize across all evaluation datasets for three tasks (temporal point processes, Markov jump processes, time series imputation), often outperforming SOTA deep learning models while being orders of magnitude faster and fully interpretable.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided program evolution can discover compact, interpretable inference functions for dynamical systems that compete with or outperform complex deep learning models, offering a promising alternative for explainable and efficient inference.

title: Fusing Cellular Network Data and Tollbooth Counts for Urban Traffic Flow Estimation
summary: Traffic simulations, essential for planning urban transit infrastructure interventions, require vehicle-category-specific origin-destination (OD) data. Existing data sources are imperfect: sparse tollbooth sensors provide accurate vehicle counts by category, while extensive mobility data from cellular network activity captures aggregated crowd movement, but lack modal disaggregation and have systematic biases. This study develops a machine learning framework to correct and disaggregate cellular network data using sparse tollbooth counts as ground truth. The model uses temporal and spatial features to learn the complex relationship between aggregated mobility data and vehicular data. The framework infers destinations from transit routes and implements routing logic to distribute corrected flows between OD pairs. This approach is applied to a bus depot expansion in Trondheim, Norway, generating hourly OD matrices by vehicle length category. The results show how limited but accurate sensor measurements can correct extensive but aggregated mobility data to produce grounded estimates of background vehicular traffic flows. These macro-scale estimates can be refined for micro-scale analysis at desired locations. The framework provides a generalisable approach for generating origin-destination data from cellular network data. This enables downstream tasks, like detailed traffic simulations for infrastructure planning in data-scarce contexts, supporting urban planners in making informed decisions.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework that uses sparse tollbooth sensor data to correct and disaggregate aggregated cellular network mobility data, generating accurate vehicle-category-specific origin-destination matrices for traffic simulations.
AI.motivation: Traffic simulations for urban transit planning require detailed vehicle-category-specific OD data, but existing data sources are inadequate: tollbooth sensors provide accurate vehicle counts but are spatially sparse, while cellular network data captures extensive mobility patterns but lacks modal disaggregation and has systematic biases.
AI.method: Developed a machine learning framework using temporal and spatial features to learn the relationship between aggregated cellular network data and vehicular data. The model uses sparse tollbooth counts as ground truth, infers destinations from transit routes, implements routing logic to distribute corrected flows between OD pairs, and generates hourly OD matrices by vehicle length category.
AI.result: Successfully applied to a bus depot expansion in Trondheim, Norway, showing how limited but accurate sensor measurements can correct extensive but aggregated mobility data to produce grounded estimates of background vehicular traffic flows. The framework generates macro-scale estimates that can be refined for micro-scale analysis.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a generalizable approach for generating OD data from cellular network data, enabling detailed traffic simulations for infrastructure planning in data-scarce contexts and supporting urban planners in making informed decisions.

title: Self-Distillation as a Performance Recovery Mechanism for LLMs: Counteracting Compression and Catastrophic Forgetting
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success, underpinning diverse AI applications. However, they often suffer from performance degradation due to factors such as catastrophic forgetting during Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), quantization, and pruning. In this work, we introduce a performance recovery framework based on Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) that effectively restores model capabilities. Complementing this practical contribution, we provide a rigorous theoretical explanation for the underlying recovery mechanism. We posit that an LLM's generative capability fundamentally relies on the high-dimensional manifold constructed by its hidden layers. To investigate this, we employ Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the alignment between student and teacher activation trajectories, leveraging its invariance to orthogonal transformations and scaling. Our experiments demonstrate a strong correlation between performance recovery and manifold alignment, substantiating the claim that self-distillation effectively aligns the student's high-dimensional manifold with the optimal structure represented by the teacher. This study bridges the gap between practical recovery frameworks and geometric representation theory, offering new insights into the internal mechanisms of self-distillation.

AI.tldr: A framework for recovering LLM performance degradation using self-distillation fine-tuning with theoretical justification based on manifold alignment.
AI.motivation: LLMs experience performance degradation from factors like catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning, quantization, and pruning. There's a need for effective recovery methods with theoretical understanding.
AI.method: Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) framework that restores model capabilities by aligning student model's hidden layer manifold with teacher model's optimal structure using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) for measurement.
AI.result: Experiments show strong correlation between performance recovery and manifold alignment, demonstrating self-distillation effectively aligns student's high-dimensional manifold with teacher's optimal structure.
AI.conclusion: The study bridges practical recovery frameworks with geometric representation theory, showing self-distillation works by aligning model manifolds and providing theoretical insights into internal mechanisms.

title: Similarity-Based Bike Station Expansion via Hybrid Denoising Autoencoders
summary: Urban bike-sharing systems require strategic station expansion to meet growing demand. Traditional allocation approaches rely on explicit demand modelling that may not capture the urban characteristics distinguishing successful stations. This study addresses the need to exploit patterns from existing stations to inform expansion decisions, particularly in data-constrained environments. We present a data-driven framework leveraging existing stations deemed desirable by operational metrics. A hybrid denoising autoencoder (HDAE) learns compressed latent representations from multi-source grid-level features (socio-demographic, built environment, and transport network), with a supervised classification head regularising the embedding space structure. Expansion candidates are selected via greedy allocation with spatial constraints based on latent-space similarity to existing stations. Evaluation on Trondheim's bike-sharing network demonstrates that HDAE embeddings yield more spatially coherent clusters and allocation patterns than raw features. Sensitivity analyses across similarity methods and distance metrics confirm robustness. A consensus-based procedure across multiple parametrisations distils 32 high-confidence extension zones where all parametrisations agree. The results demonstrate how representation learning captures complex patterns that raw features miss, enabling evidence-based expansion planning without explicit demand modelling. The consensus procedure strengthens recommendations by requiring agreement across parametrisations, while framework configurability allows planners to incorporate operational knowledge. The methodology generalises to any location-allocation problem where existing desirable instances inform the selection of new candidates.

AI.tldr: A hybrid denoising autoencoder framework learns latent representations from urban features to identify optimal bike-sharing station expansion locations based on similarity to existing successful stations.
AI.motivation: Traditional bike-sharing station expansion approaches rely on explicit demand modelling that may not capture complex urban characteristics distinguishing successful stations, especially in data-constrained environments where patterns from existing successful stations could better inform expansion decisions.
AI.method: A hybrid denoising autoencoder (HDAE) learns compressed latent representations from multi-source grid-level features (socio-demographic, built environment, and transport network). A supervised classification head regularizes the embedding space structure. Expansion candidates are selected via greedy allocation with spatial constraints based on latent-space similarity to existing stations.
AI.result: HDAE embeddings yield more spatially coherent clusters and allocation patterns than raw features. Sensitivity analyses confirm robustness, and a consensus-based procedure across multiple parametrisations identifies 32 high-confidence extension zones where all parametrisations agree. The framework successfully applied to Trondheim's bike-sharing network.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning captures complex patterns that raw features miss, enabling evidence-based expansion planning without explicit demand modelling. The consensus procedure strengthens recommendations by requiring agreement across parametrisations, and the framework generalizes to any location-allocation problem where existing desirable instances inform new candidate selection.

title: ECG-Lens: Benchmarking ML & DL Models on PTB-XL Dataset
summary: Automated classification of electrocardiogram (ECG) signals is a useful tool for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases. This study compares three traditional machine learning algorithms (Decision Tree Classifier, Random Forest Classifier, and Logistic Regression) and three deep learning models (Simple Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Complex CNN (ECGLens)) for the classification of ECG signals from the PTB-XL dataset, which contains 12-lead recordings from normal patients and patients with various cardiac conditions. The DL models were trained on raw ECG signals, allowing them to automatically extract discriminative features. Data augmentation using the Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) was applied to enhance model performance, increase the diversity of training samples, and preserve the essential characteristics of the ECG signals. The models were evaluated using multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. The ECG-Lens model achieved the highest performance, with 80% classification accuracy and a 90% ROC-AUC. These findings demonstrate that deep learning architectures, particularly complex CNNs substantially outperform traditional ML methods on raw 12-lead ECG data, and provide a practical benchmark for selecting automated ECG classification models and identifying directions for condition-specific model development.

AI.tldr: Deep learning models, especially complex CNNs like ECG-Lens, outperform traditional machine learning methods for automated classification of 12-lead ECG signals from the PTB-XL dataset, achieving 80% accuracy with 90% ROC-AUC.
AI.motivation: Automated ECG classification is useful for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases, but there's a need to compare performance between traditional machine learning methods and modern deep learning approaches on raw ECG signals to establish effective benchmarks.
AI.method: Compared three traditional ML algorithms (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) with three DL models (simple CNN, LSTM, complex CNN called ECG-Lens) on PTB-XL dataset of 12-lead ECG signals. DL models were trained on raw signals to extract features automatically. Applied Stationary Wavelet Transform for data augmentation to enhance performance and sample diversity while preserving ECG characteristics.
AI.result: ECG-Lens model achieved highest performance with 80% classification accuracy and 90% ROC-AUC. Deep learning architectures, particularly complex CNNs, substantially outperformed traditional ML methods on raw 12-lead ECG data.
AI.conclusion: Complex CNN architectures like ECG-Lens provide superior performance for automated ECG classification, establishing practical benchmarks and guiding future development of condition-specific models.

title: DPrivBench: Benchmarking LLMs' Reasoning for Differential Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) has a wide range of applications for protecting data privacy, but designing and verifying DP algorithms requires expert-level reasoning, creating a high barrier for non-expert practitioners. Prior works either rely on specialized verification languages that demand substantial domain expertise or remain semi-automated and require human-in-the-loop guidance. In this work, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can automate DP reasoning. We introduce DPrivBench, a benchmark in which each instance asks whether a function or algorithm satisfies a stated DP guarantee under specified assumptions. The benchmark is carefully designed to cover a broad range of DP topics, span diverse difficulty levels, and resist shortcut reasoning through trivial pattern matching. Experiments show that while the strongest models handle textbook mechanisms well, all models struggle with advanced algorithms, revealing substantial gaps in current DP reasoning capabilities. Through further analytic study and failure-mode analysis, we identify several promising directions for improving automated DP reasoning. Our benchmark provides a solid foundation for developing and evaluating such methods, and complements existing benchmarks for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called DPrivBench is introduced to evaluate whether large language models can automate differential privacy reasoning, revealing that current models struggle with advanced algorithms despite handling basic textbook mechanisms well.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy (DP) algorithm design and verification requires expert-level reasoning, creating a high barrier for non-expert practitioners. Existing approaches either need specialized verification languages with substantial domain expertise or remain semi-automated requiring human guidance.
AI.method: Introduces DPrivBench, a benchmark designed to test LLMs' ability to reason about DP algorithms. The benchmark covers a broad range of DP topics, spans diverse difficulty levels, and is designed to resist shortcut reasoning through trivial pattern matching. Experiments evaluate various models on this benchmark.
AI.result: Experiments show that while the strongest models can handle textbook DP mechanisms well, all models struggle with advanced DP algorithms, revealing substantial gaps in current DP reasoning capabilities. Further analysis identifies promising directions for improving automated DP reasoning.
AI.conclusion: DPrivBench provides a solid foundation for developing and evaluating methods for automated DP reasoning, complementing existing benchmarks for mathematical reasoning. The work reveals current limitations of LLMs in advanced DP reasoning and identifies research directions for improvement.

title: Convolutionally Low-Rank Models with Modified Quantile Regression for Interval Time Series Forecasting
summary: The quantification of uncertainty in prediction models is crucial for reliable decision-making, yet remains a significant challenge. Interval time series forecasting offers a principled solution to this problem by providing prediction intervals (PIs), which indicates the probability that the true value falls within the predicted range. We consider a recently established point forecasts (PFs) method termed Learning-Based Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization (LbCNNM), which directly generates multi-step ahead forecasts by leveraging the convolutional low-rankness property derived from training data. While theoretically complete and empirically effective, LbCNNM lacks inherent uncertainty estimation capabilities, a limitation shared by many advanced forecasting methods. To resolve the issue, we modify the well-known Quantile Regression (QR) and integrate it into LbCNNM, resulting in a novel interval forecasting method termed LbCNNM with Modified Quantile Regression (LbCNNM-MQR). In addition, we devise interval calibration techniques to further improve the accuracy of PIs. Extensive experiments on over 100,000 real-world time series demonstrate the superior performance of LbCNNM-MQR.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LbCNNM-MQR, a novel interval forecasting method that combines Learning-Based Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization with Modified Quantile Regression for uncertainty-aware time series predictions.
AI.motivation: Current prediction models lack inherent uncertainty estimation capabilities, which is crucial for reliable decision-making. While LbCNNM is effective for point forecasts, it doesn't provide prediction intervals to quantify uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors modify Quantile Regression and integrate it with LbCNNM (Learning-Based Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization) to create LbCNNM-MQR. They also develop interval calibration techniques to improve prediction interval accuracy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on over 100,000 real-world time series demonstrate the superior performance of LbCNNM-MQR compared to existing methods.
