AI.conclusion: LbCNNM-MQR effectively addresses the uncertainty estimation challenge in time series forecasting by providing accurate prediction intervals while maintaining the advantages of the original LbCNNM method.

title: QuantSightBench: Evaluating LLM Quantitative Forecasting with Prediction Intervals
summary: Forecasting has become a natural benchmark for reasoning under uncertainty. Yet existing evaluations of large language models remain limited to judgmental tasks in simple formats, such as binary or multiple-choice questions. In practice, however, forecasting spans a far broader scope. Across domains such as economics, public health, and social demographics, decisions hinge on numerical estimates over continuous quantities, a capability that current benchmarks do not capture. Evaluating such estimates requires a format that makes uncertainty explicit and testable. We propose prediction intervals as a natural and rigorous interface for this purpose. They demand scale awareness, internal consistency across confidence levels, and calibration over a continuum of outcomes, making them a more suitable evaluation format than point estimates for numerical forecasting. To assess this capability, we introduce a new benchmark QuantSightBench, and evaluate frontier models under multiple settings, assessing both empirical coverage and interval sharpness. Our results show that none of the 11 evaluated frontier and open-weight models achieves the 90\% coverage target, with the top performers Gemini 3.1 Pro (79.1\%), Grok 4 (76.4\%), and GPT-5.4 (75.3\%) all falling at least 10 percentage points short. Calibration degrades sharply at extreme magnitudes, revealing systematic overconfidence across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces prediction intervals as a rigorous benchmark for evaluating LLMs' numerical forecasting capabilities, revealing that current frontier models systematically fail to achieve proper calibration, especially at extreme magnitudes.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of large language models are limited to judgmental tasks in simple formats like binary or multiple-choice questions, while real-world forecasting in domains like economics and public health requires numerical estimates over continuous quantities with explicit uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Proposed prediction intervals as a natural interface for evaluating numerical forecasting, introduced QuantSightBench benchmark, and evaluated 11 frontier models under multiple settings assessing empirical coverage and interval sharpness.
AI.result: None of the 11 evaluated frontier and open-weight models achieved the 90% coverage target. Top performers were Gemini 3.1 Pro (79.1%), Grok 4 (76.4%), and GPT-5.4 (75.3%), all falling at least 10 percentage points short. Calibration degrades sharply at extreme magnitudes, revealing systematic overconfidence across all models.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs demonstrate significant limitations in numerical forecasting capabilities, particularly in providing properly calibrated uncertainty estimates. Prediction intervals provide a more rigorous evaluation framework than point estimates, revealing systematic overconfidence that needs to be addressed for reliable forecasting applications.

title: Prototype-Grounded Concept Models for Verifiable Concept Alignment
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) aim to improve interpretability in Deep Learning by structuring predictions through human-understandable concepts, but they provide no way to verify whether learned concepts align with the human's intended meaning, hurting interpretability. We introduce Prototype-Grounded Concept Models (PGCMs), which ground concepts in learned visual prototypes: image parts that serve as explicit evidence for the concepts. This grounding enables direct inspection of concept semantics and supports targeted human intervention at the prototype level to correct misalignments. Empirically, PGCMs match the predictive performance of state-of-the-art CBMs while substantially improving transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.

AI.tldr: PGCMs improve upon Concept Bottleneck Models by grounding concepts in visual prototypes, enabling better verification of concept alignment with human meaning and supporting prototype-level interventions.
AI.motivation: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) lack verification mechanisms to ensure learned concepts align with human-intended meaning, which undermines interpretability despite their structured prediction approach.
AI.method: Introduce Prototype-Grounded Concept Models (PGCMs) that ground concepts in learned visual prototypes - image parts serving as explicit evidence for concepts. This grounding enables direct inspection of concept semantics and supports targeted human intervention at the prototype level.
AI.result: Empirically, PGCMs match the predictive performance of state-of-the-art CBMs while substantially improving transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.
AI.conclusion: PGCMs offer a practical approach to enhance concept-based interpretable models by addressing the verification problem through prototype grounding, making concept alignment checkable and correctable.

title: Unveiling Stochasticity: Universal Multi-modal Probabilistic Modeling for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting is a challenging spatio-temporal modeling task and a critical component of urban transportation management. Current studies mainly focus on deterministic predictions, with limited considerations on the uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics. Therefore, this paper proposes an elegant yet universal approach that transforms existing models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a novel Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer. The modified model requires no changes to the training pipeline and can be trained using only the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss, without any auxiliary or regularization terms. Experiments on multiple traffic datasets show that our approach generalizes from classic to modern model architectures while preserving deterministic performance. Furthermore, we propose a systematic evaluation procedure based on cumulative distributions and confidence intervals, and demonstrate that our approach is considerably more accurate and informative than unimodal or deterministic baselines. Finally, a more detailed study on a real-world dense urban traffic network is presented to examine the impact of data quality on uncertainty quantification and to show the robustness of our approach under imperfect data conditions. Code available at https://github.com/Weijiang-Xiong/OpenSkyTraffic

AI.tldr: Proposes a universal approach to convert existing traffic forecasting models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer, requiring minimal changes to training while improving uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Current traffic forecasting studies mainly focus on deterministic predictions with limited consideration of uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics, creating a need for better probabilistic forecasting methods.
AI.method: Transforms existing models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a novel GMM layer, trained using only Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss without auxiliary or regularization terms, requiring no changes to the training pipeline.
AI.result: Approach generalizes from classic to modern model architectures while preserving deterministic performance; outperforms unimodal/deterministic baselines in accuracy and informativeness; robust under imperfect data conditions; validated through systematic evaluation based on cumulative distributions and confidence intervals.
AI.conclusion: The proposed elegant and universal GMM layer approach successfully addresses uncertainty quantification in traffic forecasting with minimal implementation overhead, providing more accurate and informative predictions that generalize well across different architectures and data quality conditions.

title: Placing Puzzle Pieces Where They Matter: A Question Augmentation Framework for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning has become a powerful approach for enhancing large language model reasoning, but faces a fundamental dilemma: training on easy problems can cause overfitting and pass@k degradation, while training on hard problems often results in sparse rewards. Recent question augmentation methods address this by prepending partial solutions as hints. However, uniform hint provision may introduce redundant information while missing critical reasoning bottlenecks, and excessive hints can reduce reasoning diversity, causing pass@k degradation. We propose \textbf{PieceHint}, a hint injection framework that strategically identifies and provides critical reasoning steps during training. By scoring the importance of different reasoning steps, selectively allocating hints based on problem difficulty, and progressively withdrawing scaffolding, PieceHint enables models to transition from guided learning to independent reasoning. Experiments on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that our 1.5B model achieves comparable average performance to 32B baselines while preserving pass@k diversity across all $k$ values.

AI.tldr: PieceHint is a reinforcement learning framework for LLM reasoning that strategically provides only critical reasoning step hints during training, avoiding overfitting on easy problems and sparse rewards on hard problems, while preserving solution diversity.
AI.motivation: RL for LLM reasoning faces a dilemma: training on easy problems causes overfitting and pass@k degradation, while training on hard problems results in sparse rewards. Existing hint methods provide uniform hints which can introduce redundant information, miss critical reasoning bottlenecks, and reduce reasoning diversity.
AI.method: PieceHint scores the importance of different reasoning steps, selectively allocates hints based on problem difficulty, and progressively withdraws scaffolding (hints) to enable models to transition from guided learning to independent reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that their 1.5B model achieves comparable average performance to 32B baselines while preserving pass@k diversity across all k values.
AI.conclusion: Strategic hint injection (PieceHint) enables effective RL training for LLM reasoning by balancing guidance and independence, achieving strong performance with smaller models while maintaining solution diversity.

title: SCRIPT: Implementing an Intelligent Tutoring System for Programming in a German University Context
comment: In: Cristea, A.I., Walker, E., Lu, Y., Santos, O.C., Isotani, S. (eds) Artificial Intelligence in Education. Posters and Late Breaking Results, Workshops and Tutorials, Industry and Innovation Tracks, Practitioners, Doctoral Consortium, Blue Sky, and WideAIED. AIED 2025. Communications in Computer and Information Science, vol 2590 . Springer, Cham
summary: Practice and extensive exercises are essential in programming education. Intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) are a viable option to provide individualized hints and advice to programming students even when human tutors are not available. However, prior ITS for programming rarely support the Python programming language, mostly focus on introductory programming, and rarely take recent developments in generative models into account. We aim to establish a novel ITS for Python programming that is highly adaptable, serves both as a teaching and research platform, provides interfaces to plug in hint mechanisms (e.g.\ via large language models), and works inside the particularly challenging regulatory environment of Germany, that is, conforming to the European data protection regulation, the European AI act, and ethical framework of the German Research Foundation. In this paper, we present the description of the current state of the ITS along with future development directions, as well as discuss the challenges and opportunities for improving the system.

AI.tldr: Developing a new intelligent tutoring system for Python programming that integrates generative AI models while complying with strict European data protection regulations, serving as both educational and research platform.
AI.motivation: Practice is essential for programming education, but existing ITS systems have limitations: they rarely support Python, mostly focus on introductory programming, and don't incorporate recent generative AI advancements. There's also a need for systems that comply with strict European/German regulations (GDPR, AI Act, DFG ethics).
AI.method: Creating a novel ITS for Python programming that is highly adaptable, serves dual purposes (teaching and research), provides interfaces for plug-in hint mechanisms (including large language models), and is designed to work within Germany's strict regulatory environment.
AI.result: The paper presents the current state of this ITS development project, describes future development directions, and discusses both challenges and opportunities for improving the system.
AI.conclusion: There is a significant opportunity to create a Python-focused ITS that leverages modern AI capabilities while addressing regulatory compliance needs, potentially improving programming education through personalized, AI-powered tutoring that respects European privacy and ethics standards.

title: Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial Spatiotemporal Neural Network
summary: Spatiotemporal modeling has evolved beyond simple time series analysis to become fundamental in structural time series analysis. While current research extensively employs graph neural networks (GNNs) for spatial feature extraction with notable success, these networks are limited to capturing only pairwise relationships, despite real-world networks containing richer topological relationships. Additionally, GNN-based models face computational challenges that scale with graph complexity, limiting their applicability to large networks. To address these limitations, we present Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial SpatioTemporal neural network (ModernSASST), the first approach to leverage simplicial complex structures for spatiotemporal modeling. Our method employs spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplicial complexes and integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency. Our source code is publicly available on GitHub\footnote{Code is available at: https://github.com/ComplexNetTSP/ST_RUM.

AI.tldr: ModernSASST is the first approach to use simplicial complexes for spatiotemporal modeling, addressing limitations of GNNs by capturing high-order topological relationships while maintaining computational efficiency through parallelizable temporal networks.
AI.motivation: Current graph neural networks (GNNs) for spatiotemporal modeling are limited to capturing only pairwise relationships, despite real-world networks containing richer topological relationships. Additionally, GNN-based models face computational challenges that scale with graph complexity, limiting their applicability to large networks.
AI.method: The method employs spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplicial complexes and integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.result: The approach is the first to leverage simplicial complex structures for spatiotemporal modeling, enabling capture of high-order topological relationships that go beyond pairwise interactions while addressing computational efficiency issues of GNNs.
AI.conclusion: ModernSASST represents a significant advancement in spatiotemporal modeling by overcoming the limitations of traditional GNN approaches through the use of simplicial complexes, enabling more accurate modeling of complex real-world networks while maintaining computational tractability.

title: Training Time Prediction for Mixed Precision-based Distributed Training
summary: Accurate prediction of training time in distributed deep learning is crucial for resource allocation, cost estimation, and job scheduling. We observe that the floating-point precision setting is a key determinant of training time, leading to training time variations of ~2.4x over its minimum. However, existing studies on distributed training time prediction rely on static model computation graphs that do not capture precision variations, including mixed precision. According to our experiments, training time prediction without considering precision results in significant prediction errors - reaching up to 147.85% in mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). To address this issue, we propose a precision-aware distributed training time predictor that achieves robust accuracy across diverse precision settings, including mixed precision, with 9.8% MAPE.

AI.tldr: A precision-aware predictor for distributed deep learning training time that addresses the limitations of existing methods by accounting for floating-point precision variations, achieving significantly lower error rates.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of training time in distributed deep learning is crucial for resource allocation, cost estimation, and job scheduling. Existing prediction methods fail to account for floating-point precision settings (including mixed precision), which can cause training time variations of up to ~2.4x and lead to prediction errors up to 147.85% MAPE.
AI.method: The paper proposes a precision-aware distributed training time predictor that specifically accounts for floating-point precision variations. Unlike existing approaches that rely on static model computation graphs, this method captures the impact of different precision settings (including mixed precision) on training performance.
AI.result: The proposed precision-aware predictor achieves robust accuracy across diverse precision settings with 9.8% mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), dramatically reducing the error compared to precision-unaware methods which had MAPE up to 147.85%.
AI.conclusion: Floating-point precision is a critical factor in distributed deep learning training time that must be accounted for in prediction models. The proposed precision-aware predictor provides significantly more accurate training time estimates, enabling better resource management, cost control, and scheduling decisions in distributed training environments.

title: Reversible Residual Normalization Alleviates Spatio-Temporal Distribution Shift
summary: Distribution shift severely degrades the performance of deep forecasting models. While this issue is well-studied for individual time series, it remains a significant challenge in the spatio-temporal domain. Effective solutions like instance normalization and its variants can mitigate temporal shifts by standardizing statistics. However, distribution shift on a graph is far more complex, involving not only the drift of individual node series but also heterogeneity across the spatial network where different nodes exhibit distinct statistical properties. To tackle this problem, we propose Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN), a novel framework that performs spatially-aware invertible transformations to address distribution shift in both spatial and temporal dimensions. Our approach integrates graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks, enabling adaptive normalization that respects the underlying graph structure while maintaining reversibility. By combining Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks, our method captures and normalizes complex Spatio-Temporal relationships in a data-driven manner. The bidirectional nature of our framework allows models to learn in a normalized latent space and recover original distributional properties through inverse transformation, offering a robust and model-agnostic solution for forecasting on dynamic spatio-temporal systems.

AI.tldr: RRN is a novel reversible normalization framework that addresses complex spatio-temporal distribution shifts by integrating graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks, enabling adaptive normalization that respects graph structure while maintaining reversibility.
AI.motivation: Distribution shift severely degrades performance of deep forecasting models, especially in spatio-temporal domains. While temporal shifts can be mitigated with techniques like instance normalization, graph-based distribution shifts are more complex - involving both drift of individual node series and spatial heterogeneity across networks where different nodes exhibit distinct statistical properties.
AI.method: Proposes Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN), which performs spatially-aware invertible transformations to address both spatial and temporal distribution shifts. Integrates graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks, enabling adaptive normalization that respects the underlying graph structure while maintaining reversibility. Combines Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks to capture and normalize complex spatio-temporal relationships data-driven.
AI.result: The bidirectional framework allows models to learn in a normalized latent space and recover original distributional properties through inverse transformation, offering a robust and model-agnostic solution for forecasting on dynamic spatio-temporal systems.
AI.conclusion: RRN provides a novel approach to handling complex spatio-temporal distribution shifts by leveraging reversible transformations with graph-aware normalization, addressing both temporal drift and spatial heterogeneity challenges in forecasting models.

title: JumpLoRA: Sparse Adapters for Continual Learning in Large Language Models
summary: Adapter-based methods have become a cost-effective approach to continual learning (CL) for Large Language Models (LLMs), by sequentially learning a low-rank update matrix for each task. To mitigate catastrophic forgetting, state-of-the-art approaches impose constraints on new adapters with respect to the previous ones, by targeting either subspace or coordinate-wise interference. In this paper, we propose JumpLoRA, a novel framework to adaptively induce sparsity in the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) blocks through the use of JumpReLU gating. The method achieves dynamic parameter isolation, which helps prevent task interference. We demonstrate that our method is highly modular and compatible with LoRA-based CL approaches. Specifically, it significantly boosts the performance of IncLoRA and outperforms the leading state-of-the-art CL method, ELLA.

AI.tldr: JumpLoRA introduces adaptive sparsity in LoRA blocks using JumpReLU gating for dynamic parameter isolation to prevent task interference in continual learning for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current adapter-based continual learning methods for LLMs use low-rank updates but still suffer from catastrophic forgetting. State-of-the-art approaches impose constraints on new adapters, but the paper proposes a better approach to prevent task interference through dynamic parameter isolation.
AI.method: JumpLoRA framework adaptively induces sparsity in LoRA blocks through JumpReLU gating, achieving dynamic parameter isolation. The method is modular and compatible with existing LoRA-based continual learning approaches.
AI.result: The method significantly boosts performance of IncLoRA and outperforms the leading state-of-the-art CL method, ELLA.
AI.conclusion: JumpLoRA provides an effective framework for continual learning in LLMs by dynamically isolating parameters through adaptive sparsity, preventing task interference while maintaining compatibility with existing LoRA approaches.

title: Synthetic data in cryptocurrencies using generative models
summary: Data plays a fundamental role in consolidating markets, services, and products in the digital financial ecosystem. However, the use of real data, especially in the financial context, can lead to privacy risks and access restrictions, affecting institutions, research, and modeling processes. Although not all financial datasets present such limitations, this work proposes the use of deep learning techniques for generating synthetic data applied to cryptocurrency price time series. The approach is based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs), combining an LSTM-type recurrent generator and an MLP discriminator to produce statistically consistent synthetic data. The experiments consider different crypto-assets and demonstrate that the model is capable of reproducing relevant temporal patterns, preserving market trends and dynamics. The generation of synthetic series through GANs is an efficient alternative for simulating financial data, showing potential for applications such as market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, with lower computational cost compared to more complex generative approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Conditional GANs with LSTM generator and MLP discriminator to generate synthetic cryptocurrency time series data that preserves temporal patterns and market dynamics while addressing privacy and access limitations.
AI.motivation: Real financial data usage poses privacy risks and access restrictions that hinder institutions, research, and modeling processes. The paper aims to address these limitations by generating synthetic cryptocurrency price time series data.
AI.method: The approach uses Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs) with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) recurrent neural network as the generator and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) as the discriminator to produce statistically consistent synthetic data.
AI.result: Experiments with different crypto-assets demonstrate the model successfully reproduces relevant temporal patterns while preserving market trends and dynamics. The synthetic data generation shows lower computational cost compared to more complex generative approaches.
AI.conclusion: GAN-based synthetic data generation is an efficient alternative for simulating financial data with applications in market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, offering a practical solution to privacy and access challenges in financial data usage.

title: Neuro-Symbolic ODE Discovery with Latent Grammar Flow
summary:
Understanding natural and engineered systems often relies on symbolic formulations, such as differential equations, which provide interpretability and transferability beyond black-box models.
  We introduce Latent Grammar Flow (LGF), a neuro-symbolic generative framework for discovering ordinary differential equations from data. LGF embeds equations as grammar-based representations into a discrete latent space and forces semantically similar equations to be positioned closer together with a behavioural loss. Then, a discrete flow model guides the sampling process to recursively generate candidate equations that best fit the observed data. Domain knowledge and constraints, such as stability, can be either embedded into the rules or used as conditional predictors.

AI.tldr: LGF is a neuro-symbolic framework that discovers differential equations from data by embedding equations as grammar representations in discrete latent space, using behavioral loss and discrete flow models for guided sampling.
AI.motivation: Symbolic formulations like differential equations provide interpretability and transferability beyond black-box models for understanding natural and engineered systems, but discovering them from data remains challenging.
AI.method: LGF embeds equations as grammar-based representations into a discrete latent space, uses behavioral loss to position semantically similar equations closer together, and employs discrete flow models to recursively generate candidate equations that fit observed data.
AI.result: The framework enables discovery of ordinary differential equations from data while allowing incorporation of domain knowledge and constraints (like stability) either through grammar rules or conditional predictors.
AI.conclusion: LGF presents a neuro-symbolic generative approach that combines the interpretability of symbolic equations with data-driven discovery, offering a flexible framework for equation discovery that can incorporate domain-specific constraints.

title: (Weighted) Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor Search: Evaluation for WiFi Fingerprint-based Positioning
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, submitted to IPIN 2026
summary: Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search is an alternative to the widely used k Nearest Neighbors (kNN) search. Unlike kNN, FRNN determines a label or an estimate for a test sample based on all training samples within a predefined distance. While this approach is beneficial in certain scenarios, assuming a fixed maximum distance for all training samples can decrease the accuracy of the FRNN. Therefore, in this paper we propose the Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) and the Weighted ARNN (WARNN), which employ adaptive distances and in latter case weights. All three methods are compared to kNN and twelve of its variants for a regression problem, namely WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning, using 22 different datasets to provide a comprehensive analysis. While the performances of the tested FRNN and ARNN versions were amongst the worse, three of the four best methods in the test were WARNN versions, indicating that using weights together with adaptive distances achieves performance comparable or even better than kNN variants.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) and Weighted ARNN (WARNN) methods as improvements to Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search, demonstrating that weighted adaptive distance methods can outperform kNN variants in WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning.
AI.motivation: Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search uses a predefined distance for all training samples, which can decrease accuracy. The authors want to improve FRNN by making distances adaptive and incorporating weights to achieve better performance than standard kNN approaches.
AI.method: The authors propose two new methods: 1) Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) which uses adaptive distances instead of fixed radii, and 2) Weighted ARNN (WARNN) which incorporates weights along with adaptive distances. These methods are compared against standard kNN and 12 of its variants using 22 different datasets for WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning regression problems.
AI.result: While FRNN and basic ARNN versions performed poorly, three of the four best performing methods in the tests were WARNN versions. This demonstrates that combining weights with adaptive distances achieves performance comparable to or better than kNN variants for indoor positioning regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: Using weights together with adaptive distances (WARNN) significantly improves performance over fixed-radius approaches, making it a competitive alternative to kNN variants for regression problems like WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning.

title: Joint-Centric Dual Contrastive Alignment with Structure-Preserving and Information-Balanced Regularization
summary: We propose HILBERT (HIerarchical Long-sequence Balanced Embedding with Reciprocal contrastive Training), a cross-attentive multimodal framework for learning document-level audio-text representations from long, segmented sequences in low-resource data settings. HILBERT leverages frozen pre-trained speech and language encoders to extract segment-level features, which are aggregated via cross-modal attention and self-attentive pooling to form modality-specific document representations and a joint cross-attentive embedding. To align modalities while preserving modality-specific structure under severe audio-text dimensional imbalance, we introduce a reciprocal dual contrastive objective that simultaneously aligns audio-to-joint and text-to-joint representations, rather than directly contrasting audio and text alone. Two auxiliary regularizers further stabilize long-sequence fusion: a Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) loss that preserves structural consistency between each modality and the joint embedding, and a mutual information balancing loss that prevents dominance of a single modality by equalizing information flow from audio and text into the joint space. For downstream prediction, HILBERT employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier over concatenated audio, text, and joint representations to accommodate heterogeneous label regimes. Extensive evaluation across multiple audio-text backbone combinations demonstrates that HILBERT learns semantically meaningful long-sequence representations and achieves superior performance on highly imbalanced multi-class settings.

AI.tldr: HILBERT is a cross-attentive multimodal framework for document-level audio-text representation learning in low-resource settings, using frozen pre-trained encoders with hierarchical aggregation and reciprocal contrastive training to handle long, segmented sequences and severe dimensional imbalance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in learning document-level audio-text representations from long, segmented sequences in low-resource data settings, particularly dealing with severe audio-text dimensional imbalance and the need to preserve modality-specific structure while achieving cross-modal alignment.
AI.method: HILBERT uses frozen pre-trained speech and language encoders to extract segment-level features, aggregates them via cross-modal attention and self-attentive pooling to form modality-specific and joint embeddings. It introduces a reciprocal dual contrastive objective for modality alignment, plus two auxiliary regularizers: Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) loss for structural consistency and mutual information balancing loss to prevent single-modality dominance. For prediction, it employs a Mixture-of-Experts classifier over concatenated audio, text, and joint representations.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across multiple audio-text backbone combinations shows that HILBERT learns semantically meaningful long-sequence representations and achieves superior performance on highly imbalanced multi-class settings.
AI.conclusion: HILBERT effectively addresses the challenges of document-level audio-text representation learning in low-resource settings with severe dimensional imbalance by combining hierarchical aggregation, reciprocal contrastive training, and carefully designed regularizers to maintain modality-specific structure while achieving robust cross-modal alignment.

title: TwinTrack: Post-hoc Multi-Rater Calibration for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) segmentation on contrast-enhanced CT is inherently ambiguous: inter-rater disagreement among experts reflects genuine uncertainty rather than annotation noise. Standard deep learning approaches assume a single ground truth, producing probabilistic outputs that can be poorly calibrated and difficult to interpret under such ambiguity. We present TwinTrack, a framework that addresses this gap through post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to the empirical mean human response (MHR) -the fraction of expert annotators labeling a voxel as tumor. Calibrated probabilities are thus directly interpretable as the expected proportion of annotators assigning the tumor label, explicitly modeling inter-rater disagreement. The proposed post-hoc calibration procedure is simple and requires only a small multi-rater calibration set. It consistently improves calibration metrics over standard approaches when evaluated on the MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark.

AI.tldr: TwinTrack is a framework for pancreatic cancer segmentation that calibrates deep learning probabilities to match inter-expert disagreement rates, making outputs interpretable as expected expert consensus proportions.
AI.motivation: Standard deep learning segmentation approaches assume a single ground truth, but pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) segmentation on CT scans has genuine inter-rater disagreement among experts, not just annotation noise. Current methods produce poorly calibrated probabilities that are difficult to interpret under such ambiguity.
AI.method: TwinTrack uses post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to the empirical mean human response (MHR) - the fraction of expert annotators labeling a voxel as tumor. This simple calibration procedure requires only a small multi-rater calibration set and makes probabilities interpretable as expected proportion of annotators assigning tumor label.
AI.result: The framework consistently improves calibration metrics over standard approaches when evaluated on the MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark, demonstrating better alignment with expert disagreement patterns.
AI.conclusion: TwinTrack addresses the challenge of ambiguous medical image segmentation by explicitly modeling inter-rater disagreement through calibrated probabilities, providing more interpretable and reliable segmentation outputs that reflect genuine clinical uncertainty.

title: Beyond Distribution Sharpening: The Importance of Task Rewards
summary: Frontier models have demonstrated exceptional capabilities following the integration of task-reward-based reinforcement learning (RL) into their training pipelines, enabling systems to evolve from pure reasoning models into sophisticated agents. However, debate persists regarding whether RL genuinely instills new skills within a base model or merely sharpens its existing distribution to elicit latent capabilities. To address this dichotomy, we present an explicit comparison between distribution sharpening and task-reward-based learning, utilizing RL as a tool to implement both paradigms. Our analysis reveals the inherent limitations of distribution sharpening, demonstrating from first principles how and why the optima can be unfavorable and the approach fundamentally unstable. Furthermore, our experiments using Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 on math datasets confirm that sharpening yields limited gains, whereas incorporating task-based reward signal can greatly help achieve robust performance improvements and stable learning.

AI.tldr: RL with task rewards enables robust performance improvements in frontier models, while distribution sharpening alone yields limited gains and is fundamentally unstable.
AI.motivation: To address the debate about whether RL instills new skills or merely sharpens existing capabilities in base models, by comparing distribution sharpening versus task-reward-based learning.
AI.method: Comparative analysis using RL as a tool to implement both paradigms: distribution sharpening and task-reward-based learning. Experimental evaluation on math datasets using Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 models.
AI.result: Distribution sharpening yields limited performance gains and is fundamentally unstable with unfavorable optima. In contrast, incorporating task-based reward signals achieves robust performance improvements and stable learning across tested models.
AI.conclusion: Task-reward-based RL genuinely enhances model capabilities beyond mere distribution sharpening, providing stable and substantial performance improvements compared to the limited gains from sharpening existing latent abilities.

title: Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization via Adaptive \varepsilon-Constraints Decomposition
summary: Multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) provides a principled framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions with multiple objectives. However, existing MOBO methods often struggle with coverage, scalability with respect to the number of objectives, and integrating constraints and preferences. In this work, we propose \textit{STAGE-BO, Sequential Targeting Adaptive Gap-Filling $\varepsilon$-Constraint Bayesian Optimization}, that explicitly targets under-explored regions of the Pareto front. By analyzing the coverage of the approximate Pareto front, our method identifies the largest geometric gaps. These gaps are then used as constraints, which transforms the problem into a sequence of inequality-constrained subproblems, efficiently solved via constrained expected improvement acquisition. Our approach provides a uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation and naturally applies to constrained and preference-based settings. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate superior coverage and competitive hypervolume performance against state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: STAGE-BO is a new multi-objective Bayesian optimization method that identifies and targets gaps in Pareto front coverage using ε-constraints, providing uniform coverage without hypervolume computations.
AI.motivation: Existing MOBO methods struggle with coverage of Pareto front, scalability with many objectives, and integration of constraints and preferences. There's a need for methods that can provide better exploration of under-explored regions.
AI.method: STAGE-BO analyzes Pareto front coverage to identify largest geometric gaps, uses these as ε-constraints to transform into constrained subproblems, and solves them via constrained expected improvement acquisition. It sequences through different gap-filling targets adaptively.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show STAGE-BO achieves superior Pareto front coverage and competitive hypervolume performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed STAGE-BO method effectively addresses coverage issues in MOBO, naturally handles constraints and preferences, and provides uniform Pareto coverage without requiring hypervolume computations, making it a practical and efficient alternative to existing methods.

title: Evaluating quality in synthetic data generation for large tabular health datasets
summary: There is no consensus in the field of synthetic data on concise metrics for quality evaluations or benchmarks on large health datasets, such as historical epidemiological data. This study presents an evaluation of seven recent models from major machine learning families. The models were evaluated using four different datasets, each with a distinct scale. To ensure a fair comparison, we systematically tuned the hyperparameters of each model for each dataset. We propose a methodology for evaluating the fidelity of synthesized joint distributions, aligning metrics with visualization on a single plot. This method is applicable to any dataset and is complemented by a domain-specific analysis of the German Cancer Registries' epidemiological dataset. The analysis reveals the challenges models face in strictly adhering to the medical domain. We hope this approach will serve as a foundational framework for guiding the selection of synthesizers and remain accessible to all stakeholders involved in releasing synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates seven ML models for synthetic data generation on health datasets, proposes a unified evaluation methodology for joint distribution fidelity, and applies it to cancer registry data.
AI.motivation: There is no consensus on quality metrics or benchmarks for synthetic data generation, especially for large health datasets like historical epidemiological data.
AI.method: Evaluated seven recent models from major ML families across four datasets of different scales, systematically tuning hyperparameters for each. Proposed a methodology for evaluating synthesized joint distributions with aligned metrics and visualizations on a single plot.
AI.result: Analysis revealed challenges models face in adhering to medical domain constraints, particularly demonstrated through domain-specific analysis of German Cancer Registries' epidemiological dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach serves as a foundational framework for selecting synthetic data generators and remains accessible to all stakeholders involved in releasing synthetic datasets.

title: Impact of Nonlinear Power Amplifier on Massive MIMO: Machine Learning Prediction Under Realistic Radio Channel
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: M-MIMO is one of the crucial technologies for increasing spectral and energy efficiency of wireless networks. Most of the current works assume that M-MIMO arrays are equipped with a linear front end. However, ongoing efforts to make wireless networks more energy-efficient push the hardware to the limits, where its nonlinear behavior appears. This is especially a common problem for the multicarrier systems, e.g., OFDM used in 4G, 5G, and possibly also in 6G, which is characterized by a high Peak-to-Average Power Ratio. While the impact of a nonlinear Power Amplifier (PA) on an OFDM signal is well characterized, it is a relatively new topic for the M-MIMO OFDM systems. Most of the recent works either neglect nonlinear effects or utilize simplified models proper for Rayleigh or LoS radio channel models. In this paper, we first theoretically characterize the nonlinear distortion in the M-MIMO system under commonly used radio channel models. Then, utilizing 3D-Ray Tracing (3D-RT) software, we demonstrate that these models are not very accurate. Instead, we propose two models: a statistical one and an ML-based one using 3D-RT results. The proposed statistical model utilizes the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution to model Signal to Distortion Ratio (SDR) for victim users, receiving nonlinear distortion, e.g., as interference from neighboring cells. The proposed ML model aims to predict SDR for a scheduled user (receiving nonlinear distortion along with the desired signal), based on the spatial characteristics of the radio channel and the operation point of each PA feeding at the M-MIMO antenna array. The predicted SDR can then be used to perform PA-aware per-user power allocation. The results show about 12% median gain in user throughput achieved by the proposed ML-based power allocation scheme over the state-of-the-art, fixed operating point scheme.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes statistical and ML-based models to characterize and mitigate nonlinear distortion in M-MIMO OFDM systems caused by power amplifier nonlinearities, achieving 12% median throughput gain through ML-based power allocation.
AI.motivation: M-MIMO systems are crucial for wireless efficiency but current works assume linear front ends. Real-world hardware operates near nonlinear limits, especially problematic for OFDM with high PAPR. While PA nonlinearity is known for OFDM, it's underexplored for M-MIMO OFDM systems, with most works using oversimplified channel models.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical characterization of nonlinear distortion in M-MIMO under common radio channel models. 2) Validation using 3D-Ray Tracing software showing limitations of existing models. 3) Proposed statistical model using Generalized Extreme Value distribution to model SDR for victim users. 4) Proposed ML model to predict SDR for scheduled users based on spatial channel characteristics and PA operation points. 5) ML-based PA-aware per-user power allocation scheme.
AI.result: The proposed ML-based power allocation scheme achieves about 12% median gain in user throughput compared to state-of-the-art fixed operating point schemes. The 3D-Ray Tracing validation showed that conventional models are inaccurate for real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Modeling nonlinear distortion in M-MIMO OFDM systems requires more sophisticated approaches than conventional simplified models. The proposed statistical and ML-based models effectively characterize and mitigate PA nonlinearity effects, enabling substantial throughput improvements through intelligent power allocation that accounts for spatial channel characteristics and PA operating points.

title: Corner Reflector Array Jamming Discrimination Using Multi-Dimensional Micro-Motion Features with Frequency Agile Radar
summary: This paper introduces a robust discrimination method for distinguishing real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming with frequency-agile radar. The key idea is to exploit the multidimensional micro-motion signatures that separate rigid ships from non-rigid decoys. From Range-Velocity maps we derive two new hand-crafted descriptors-mean weighted residual (MWR) and complementary contrast factor (CCF) and fuse them with deep features learned by a lightweight CNN. An XGBoost classifier then gives the final decision. Extensive simulations show that the hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives, confirming the superiority of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A hybrid approach combining hand-crafted micro-motion descriptors with deep learning features for robust discrimination between real ships and corner-reflector-array jamming using frequency-agile radar.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of distinguishing real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming in frequency-agile radar systems, which is crucial for effective radar countermeasures and defense systems.
AI.method: 1) Exploit multidimensional micro-motion signatures to separate rigid ships from non-rigid decoys. 2) Derive two hand-crafted descriptors (MWR and CCF) from Range-Velocity maps. 3) Fuse these with deep features learned by a lightweight CNN. 4) Use XGBoost classifier for final decision.
AI.result: Extensive simulations show the hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives, confirming the superiority of the proposed approach over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The combination of carefully designed hand-crafted micro-motion features with deep learning features provides a robust and superior solution for ship target discrimination against sophisticated jamming techniques.

title: AEGIS: Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation for Knowledge-Preserving Vision-Language-Action Fine-Tuning
summary: Adapting pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) for robotic control requires injecting high-magnitude continuous gradients from a flow-matching action expert into a backbone trained exclusively with cross-entropy. This cross-modal gradient asymmetry - the spectral dimensionality mismatch between low-rank MSE regression gradients and the high-dimensional semantic manifold sculpted by CE pre-training, causes rapid, severe erosion of the VLM's visual-question-answering (VQA) capability. Industry-standard defences either sever the gradient pathway entirely via stop gradient, discarding the rich continuous supervision, or restrict parameter capacity through low-rank adapters (LoRA) that constrain the rank of updates but not their direction, and thus still overwrite the pre-trained manifold. We introduce AEGIS (Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation System): a buffer-free, layer-wise orthogonal gradient projection framework that enables direct continuous MSE learning while preserving the pre-trained VQA manifold - without any co-training data or replay buffer. AEGIS pre-computes a static Gaussian reference anchor from masked VQA forward passes across all transformer layers, then at each training step constructs a Wasserstein-2 transport penalty that generates an anchor restoration gradient. A sequential dual-backward decomposes the task and anchor gradients; for each transformer layer, AEGIS applies a single Gram-Schmidt orthogonal projection that bends the task gradient away from the destructive direction while preserving its constructive content. The projection sheds less than 1% of gradient energy on average, yet eliminates the cumulative activation drift that drives severe forgetting.

AI.tldr: AEGIS is a novel gradient projection framework that prevents catastrophic forgetting when adapting vision-language models for robotic control by isolating task gradients from the pre-trained VQA manifold without needing co-training data or replay buffers.
AI.motivation: When adapting pre-trained VLMs for robotic control using flow-matching action experts, there's a fundamental mismatch between low-rank MSE regression gradients and the high-dimensional semantic manifold from CE pre-training. This causes catastrophic forgetting of VQA capabilities. Existing solutions either discard continuous supervision (stop gradient) or still overwrite the manifold (LoRA).
AI.method: AEGIS computes a static Gaussian reference anchor from masked VQA forward passes across transformer layers, then constructs a Wasserstein-2 transport penalty for anchor restoration. It performs sequential dual-backward to decompose task and anchor gradients, then applies Gram-Schmidt orthogonal projection to each layer's task gradient, bending it away from destructive directions while preserving constructive content.
AI.result: The method sheds less than 1% of gradient energy on average while eliminating cumulative activation drift that causes severe forgetting. It enables direct continuous MSE learning while preserving pre-trained VQA capabilities without co-training data or replay buffers.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS provides an effective solution to the cross-modal gradient asymmetry problem in VLMs for robotics, enabling gradient-based adaptation without catastrophic forgetting through layer-wise orthogonal gradient isolation.

title: The Harder Path: Last Iterate Convergence for Uncoupled Learning in Zero-Sum Games with Bandit Feedback
comment: Accepted at the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025)
summary: We study the problem of learning in zero-sum matrix games with repeated play and bandit feedback. Specifically, we focus on developing uncoupled algorithms that guarantee, without communication between players, the convergence of the last-iterate to a Nash equilibrium. Although the non-bandit case has been studied extensively, this setting has only been explored recently, with a bound of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/8})$ on the exploitability gap. We show that, for uncoupled algorithms, guaranteeing convergence of the policy profiles to a Nash equilibrium is detrimental to the performance, with the best attainable rate being $Ω(T^{-1/4})$ in contrast to the usual $Ω(T^{-1/2})$ rate for convergence of the average iterates. We then propose two algorithms that achieve this optimal rate up to constant and logarithmic factors. The first algorithm leverages a straightforward trade-off between exploration and exploitation, while the second employs a regularization technique based on a two-step mirror descent approach.

AI.tldr: The paper studies zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback, showing uncoupled algorithms face fundamental limits (Ω(T^{-1/4})) for last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium, and proposes two optimal algorithms achieving this rate.
AI.motivation: Bandit feedback in zero-sum matrix games with repeated play is underexplored compared to full-information settings. Existing uncoupled algorithms with bandit feedback only achieve O(T^{-1/8}) exploitability gap. There's a need to understand fundamental limits and develop optimal algorithms for last-iterate convergence without communication between players.
AI.method: Two algorithms: 1) Simple trade-off between exploration and exploitation; 2) Regularization-based approach using two-step mirror descent. Both achieve optimal rate up to constant/logarithmic factors.
AI.result: Proved fundamental lower bound Ω(T^{-1/4}) for uncoupled algorithms' last-iterate convergence, contrasting with Ω(T^{-1/2}) for average iterate convergence. Proposed algorithms achieve this optimal rate up to constant/log factors.
AI.conclusion: For uncoupled algorithms in zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback, guaranteeing last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium imposes a fundamental performance limit (worse than average iterate convergence), but optimal algorithms can be designed to achieve this bound.

title: Sample Complexity Bounds for Stochastic Shortest Path with a Generative Model
comment: Accepted at the 32nd International Conference on Algorithmic Learning Theory (ALT 2021)
summary: We study the sample complexity of learning an $ε$-optimal policy in the Stochastic Shortest Path (SSP) problem. We first derive sample complexity bounds when the learner has access to a generative model. We show that there exists a worst-case SSP instance with $S$ states, $A$ actions, minimum cost $c_{\min}$, and maximum expected cost of the optimal policy over all states $B_{\star}$, where any algorithm requires at least $Ω(SAB_{\star}^3/(c_{\min}ε^2))$ samples to return an $ε$-optimal policy with high probability. Surprisingly, this implies that whenever $c_{\min} = 0$ an SSP problem may not be learnable, thus revealing that learning in SSPs is strictly harder than in the finite-horizon and discounted settings. We complement this lower bound with an algorithm that matches it, up to logarithmic factors, in the general case, and an algorithm that matches it up to logarithmic factors even when $c_{\min} = 0$, but only under the condition that the optimal policy has a bounded hitting time to the goal state.

AI.tldr: The paper studies sample complexity of learning optimal policies in Stochastic Shortest Path problems, establishing matching lower and upper bounds that reveal SSP learning is harder than finite-horizon or discounted settings.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental sample complexity of learning in Stochastic Shortest Path problems, particularly how it differs from finite-horizon and discounted reinforcement learning settings.
AI.method: Derives sample complexity lower bounds via worst-case instance analysis, then provides algorithms that match these bounds up to logarithmic factors. Two algorithms are presented: one for general case and one specialized for cases where optimal policy has bounded hitting time.
AI.result: Lower bound of Ω(SAB₊³/(c_minε²)) samples required for ε-optimal policy, showing SSP learning is strictly harder than finite-horizon/discounted settings. Provided matching algorithms (up to log factors) including one that works even when c_min=0 under bounded hitting time condition.
AI.conclusion: Reveals fundamental difficulty of SSP learning, establishes tight sample complexity bounds, and provides practical algorithms that achieve optimal sample efficiency under certain conditions.

title: Univariate Channel Fusion for Multivariate Time Series Classification
summary: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) plays a crucial role in various domains, including biomedical signal analysis and motion monitoring. However, existing approaches, particularly deep learning models, often require high computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time applications or deployment on low-cost hardware, such as IoT devices and wearable systems. In this paper, we propose the Univariate Channel Fusion (UCF) method to deal with MTSC efficiently. UCF transforms multivariate time series into a univariate representation through simple channel fusion strategies such as the mean, median, or dynamic time warping barycenter. This transformation enables the use of any classifier originally designed for univariate time series, providing a flexible and computationally lightweight alternative to complex models. We evaluate UCF in five case studies covering diverse application domains, including chemical monitoring, brain-computer interfaces, and human activity analysis. The results demonstrate that UCF often outperforms baseline methods and state-of-the-art algorithms tailored for MTSC, while achieving substantial gains in computational efficiency, being particularly effective in problems with high inter-channel correlation.

AI.tldr: UCF method transforms multivariate time series into univariate representation using simple channel fusion (mean, median, DTW barycenter), enabling use of any univariate classifier and achieving both accuracy and computational efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning approaches for multivariate time series classification require high computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time applications and deployment on low-cost hardware like IoT devices and wearable systems.
AI.method: Univariate Channel Fusion (UCF) method transforms multivariate time series into univariate representation through simple channel fusion strategies (mean, median, or dynamic time warping barycenter), allowing use of any classifier designed for univariate time series.
AI.result: UCF often outperforms baseline methods and state-of-the-art algorithms in five case studies covering chemical monitoring, brain-computer interfaces, and human activity analysis, while achieving substantial computational efficiency gains, especially effective for problems with high inter-channel correlation.
AI.conclusion: UCF provides a flexible and computationally lightweight alternative to complex models for multivariate time series classification, addressing the resource constraints of real-time applications and low-cost hardware while maintaining or improving classification performance.

title: Tabular foundation models for in-context prediction of molecular properties
summary: Accurate molecular property prediction is central to drug discovery, catalysis, and process design, yet real-world applications are often limited by small datasets. Molecular foundation models provide a promising direction by learning transferable molecular representations; however, they typically involve task-specific fine-tuning, require machine learning expertise, and often fail to outperform classical baselines. Tabular foundation models (TFMs) offer a fundamentally different paradigm: they perform predictions through in-context learning, enabling inference without task-specific training. Here, we evaluate TFMs in the low- to medium-data regime across both standardized pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets. We evaluate both frozen molecular foundation model representations, as well as classical descriptors and fingerprints. Across the benchmarks, the approach shows excellent predictive performance while reducing computational cost, compared to fine-tuning, with these advantages also transferring to practical engineering data settings. In particular, combining TFMs with CheMeleon embeddings yields up to 100\% win rates on 30 MoleculeACE tasks, while compact RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provide strong descriptor-based alternatives. Molecular representation emerges as a key determinant in TFM performance, with molecular foundation model embeddings and 2D descriptor sets both providing substantial gains over classic molecular fingerprints on many tasks. These results suggest that in-context learning with TFMs provides a highly accurate and cost-efficient alternative for property prediction in practical applications.

AI.tldr: TFMs with in-context learning outperform fine-tuned foundation models for molecular property prediction in low-data settings, offering better accuracy with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Real-world molecular property prediction is limited by small datasets, and while molecular foundation models show promise, they require task-specific fine-tuning, ML expertise, and often underperform classical baselines.
AI.method: Evaluated Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) using in-context learning on standardized pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets. Tested frozen molecular foundation model embeddings (like CheMeleon), classical descriptors (RDKit2d, Mordred), and traditional fingerprints.
AI.result: TFMs with CheMeleon embeddings achieved up to 100% win rates on 30 MoleculeACE tasks. RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provided strong alternatives. Molecular representation choice was critical, with foundation model embeddings and 2D descriptors outperforming classic fingerprints.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning with TFMs provides a highly accurate and computationally efficient alternative to fine-tuning for molecular property prediction in practical low-data applications.

title: Sketching the Readout of Large Language Models for Scalable Data Attribution and Valuation
summary: Data attribution and valuation are critical for understanding data-model synergy for Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing gradient-based methods suffer from scalability challenges on LLMs. Inspired by human cognition, where decision making relies on a focused readout of relevant memories rather than replaying all pathways, we introduce RISE (Readout Influence Sketching Estimator). Instead of computing and indexing gradients across the entire LLM, RISE focuses on influence hotspots at the output layer, where influence signals concentrate, and the gradient admits a decomposed outer-product form. This enables a dual-channel representation combining a lexical residual channel (RH) and a semantic projected-error channel (GH). Applying CountSketch projections to these channels achieves strong compression while maintaining accurate attribution. Across the OLMo (1B-32B) and Pythia (14M-6.9B) families, RISE reduces index storage by up to 112$\times$ compared to RapidIn and scales to 32B parameters LLM, where gradient-based baselines such as RapidIn and ZO-Inf become memory-infeasible. We evaluate RISE on two paradigms: (1) retrospective attribution, retrieving influential training examples for specific predictions, and (2) prospective valuation, scoring candidate data utility zero-shot. We validate RISE on three tasks: Howdy backdoor data detection, Finance-Medical domain separation, and Brain Rot high-quality data selection. In a closed-loop Brain Rot study, continued pretraining on RISE-selected data yields consistent downstream improvements. Overall, RISE provides a practical and scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern large language models.

AI.tldr: RISE introduces a scalable data attribution method for LLMs by focusing on output-layer influence hotspots using dual-channel compressed representations, achieving 112× storage reduction while enabling practical influence analysis on models up to 32B parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing gradient-based methods for data attribution and valuation suffer from scalability challenges on large language models, as computing and storing gradients across entire models becomes memory-infeasible for billion-parameter models.
AI.method: RISE focuses on influence hotspots at the output layer where signals concentrate, using a dual-channel representation (lexical residual channel and semantic projected-error channel) with CountSketch projections for compression while maintaining accurate attribution.
AI.result: RISE reduces index storage by up to 112× compared to RapidIn, scales to 32B parameter LLMs where gradient-based baselines fail, and demonstrates effectiveness on retrospective attribution, prospective valuation, and data selection tasks including backdoor detection, domain separation, and quality selection.
AI.conclusion: RISE provides a practical and scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern large language models, enabling applications that were previously infeasible due to computational constraints.

title: OT on the Map: Quantifying Domain Shifts in Geographic Space
summary: In computer vision and machine learning for geographic data, out-of-domain generalization is a pervasive challenge, arising from uneven global data coverage and distribution shifts across geographic regions. Though models are frequently trained in one region and deployed in another, there is no principled method for determining when this cross-region adaptation will be successful. A well-defined notion of distance between distributions can effectively quantify how different a new target domain is compared to the domains used for model training, which in turn could support model training and deployment decisions. In this paper, we propose a strategy for computing distances between geospatial domains that leverages geographic information with Optimal Transport methods (GeoSpOT). In our experiments, GeoSpOT distances emerge as effective predictors of cross-domain transfer difficulty. We further demonstrate that embeddings from pretrained location encoders provide information comparable to image/text embeddings, despite relying solely on longitude-latitude pairs as input. This allows users to get an approximation of out-of-domain performance for geospatial models, even when the exact downstream task is unknown, or no task-specific data is available. Building on these findings, we show that GeoSpOT distances can preemptively guide data selection and enable predictive tools to analyze regions where a model is likely to underperform.

AI.tldr: GeoSpOT uses Optimal Transport with geographic information to measure distances between geospatial domains and predict cross-region transfer difficulty, helping guide model deployment decisions without requiring task-specific data.
AI.motivation: Out-of-domain generalization is a major challenge in geospatial machine learning due to uneven global data coverage and distribution shifts across regions. There's no principled method to determine when cross-region model adaptation will succeed, making deployment decisions difficult.
AI.method: GeoSpOT (Geospatial Optimal Transport) leverages geographic information with Optimal Transport methods to compute distances between geospatial domains. It uses pretrained location encoders that take only longitude-latitude pairs as input to generate embeddings comparable to image/text embeddings.
AI.result: GeoSpOT distances effectively predict cross-domain transfer difficulty. Location encoder embeddings provide information comparable to image/text embeddings despite minimal input. The method allows approximation of out-of-domain performance even without task-specific data or known downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: GeoSpOT provides a principled way to quantify domain differences in geospatial contexts, enabling predictive analysis of model performance across regions. It can guide data selection and identify regions where models are likely to underperform, addressing the cross-region generalization challenge.

title: Enhancing AI and Dynamical Subseasonal Forecasts with Probabilistic Bias Correction
summary: Decision-makers rely on weather forecasts to plant crops, manage wildfires, allocate water and energy, and prepare for weather extremes. Today, such forecasts enjoy unprecedented accuracy out to two weeks thanks to steady advances in physics-based dynamical models and data-driven artificial intelligence (AI) models. However, model skill drops precipitously at subseasonal timescales (2 - 6 weeks ahead), due to compounding errors and persistent biases. To counter this degradation, we introduce probabilistic bias correction (PBC), a machine learning framework that substantially reduces systematic error by learning to correct historical probabilistic forecasts. When applied to the leading dynamical and AI models from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), PBC doubles the subseasonal skill of the AI Forecasting System and improves the skill of the operationally-debiased dynamical model for 91% of pressure, 92% of temperature, and 98% of precipitation targets. We designed PBC for operational deployment, and, in ECMWF's 2025 real-time forecasting competition, its global forecasts placed first for all weather variables and lead times, outperforming the dynamical models from six operational forecasting centers, an international dynamical multi-model ensemble, ECMWF's AI Forecasting System, and the forecasting systems of 34 teams worldwide. These probabilistic skill gains translate into more accurate prediction of extreme events and have the potential to improve agricultural planning, energy management, and disaster preparedness in vulnerable communities.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework called probabilistic bias correction (PBC) dramatically improves subseasonal weather forecast accuracy by correcting systematic errors in existing models, achieving top performance in global forecasting competitions.
AI.motivation: Current weather forecast models show excellent accuracy up to two weeks but suffer from significant skill degradation at subseasonal timescales (2-6 weeks) due to compounding errors and persistent biases, limiting their utility for critical applications like agriculture, wildfire management, and disaster preparedness.
AI.method: The researchers introduced probabilistic bias correction (PBC), a machine learning framework that learns to correct historical probabilistic forecasts to reduce systematic errors. PBC was applied to leading dynamical and AI models from ECMWF.
AI.result: PBC doubled the subseasonal skill of ECMWF's AI Forecasting System and improved the operationally-debiased dynamical model for 91% of pressure, 92% of temperature, and 98% of precipitation targets. In ECMWF's 2025 real-time forecasting competition, PBC's global forecasts placed first for all weather variables and lead times, outperforming all competing systems.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic bias correction represents a significant advance in subseasonal forecasting, with practical applications for agricultural planning, energy management, and disaster preparedness in vulnerable communities by providing more accurate prediction of extreme events.

title: Detecting and Suppressing Reward Hacking with Gradient Fingerprints
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically optimizes for outcome rewards without imposing constraints on intermediate reasoning. This leaves training susceptible to reward hacking, where models exploit loopholes (e.g., spurious patterns in training data) in the reward function to achieve high scores without solving the intended task. These reward-hacking behaviors are often implicit, as the intermediate chain-of-thought (CoT) may appear plausible on the surface, limiting the effectiveness of purely text-based monitoring. We propose Gradient Fingerprint (GRIFT), a method for detecting reward hacking using models' internal computations. Given a prompt and a model-generated CoT, GRIFT computes gradients of the CoT conditioned on the prompt and compresses them into a compact representation, which is then used to assess whether the CoT reflects reward hacking behavior. Across verifiable reasoning benchmarks spanning math, code, and logical reasoning, GRIFT substantially outperforms strong baselines, including CoT Monitor and TRACE, achieving over 25% relative improvement in detecting reward hacking behavior. Moreover, integrating GRIFT into the rejection fine-tuning pipeline for reasoning tasks reduces reward hacking and improves performance on the true task objective. Our results highlight a promising direction of leveraging gradient level representations for assessing the quality of CoT reasoning traces. Our code is available at: https://github.com/songtao-x/reward_hack.

AI.tldr: GRIFT is a method that uses gradient fingerprints of model's internal computations to detect reward hacking in chain-of-thought reasoning, outperforming existing baselines by over 25% and improving performance when integrated into rejection fine-tuning pipelines.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) optimizes for outcome rewards without constraining intermediate reasoning, making training susceptible to reward hacking where models exploit loopholes in reward functions to achieve high scores without solving the intended task. These behaviors are often implicit since intermediate CoT may appear plausible on the surface, limiting text-based monitoring effectiveness.
AI.method: GRIFT (Gradient Fingerprint) computes gradients of the chain-of-thought conditioned on the prompt and compresses them into a compact representation, which is then used to assess whether the CoT reflects reward hacking behavior. The method leverages models' internal computations rather than just text-based analysis.
AI.result: Across verifiable reasoning benchmarks in math, code, and logical reasoning, GRIFT substantially outperforms strong baselines (CoT Monitor and TRACE) with over 25% relative improvement in detecting reward hacking. Integrating GRIFT into rejection fine-tuning pipelines reduces reward hacking and improves performance on the true task objective.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight a promising direction of leveraging gradient-level representations for assessing the quality of chain-of-thought reasoning traces, offering a more effective approach to detecting reward hacking than text-based methods alone.

title: FL-MHSM: Spatially-adaptive Fusion and Ensemble Learning for Flood-Landslide Multi-Hazard Susceptibility Mapping at Regional Scale
summary: Existing multi-hazard susceptibility mapping (MHSM) studies often rely on spatially uniform models, treat hazards independently, and provide limited representation of cross-hazard dependence and uncertainty. To address these limitations, this study proposes a deep learning (DL) workflow for joint flood-landslide multi-hazard susceptibility mapping (FL-MHSM) that combines two-level spatial partitioning, probabilistic Early Fusion (EF), a tree-based Late Fusion (LF) baseline, and a soft-gating Mixture of Experts (MoE) model, with MoE serving as final predictive model. The proposed design preserves spatial heterogeneity through zonal partitions and enables data-parallel large-area prediction using overlapping lattice grids. In Kerala, EF remained competitive with LF, improving flood recall from 0.816 to 0.840 and reducing Brier score from 0.092 to 0.086, while MoE provided strongest performance for flood susceptibility, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.905, recall of 0.930, and F1-score of 0.722. In Nepal, EF similarly improved flood recall from 0.820 to 0.858 and reduced Brier score from 0.057 to 0.049 relative to LF, while MoE outperformed both EF and LF for landslide susceptibility, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.914, recall of 0.901, and F1-score of 0.559. GeoDetector analysis of MoE outputs further showed that dominant factors varied more across zones in Kerala, where susceptibility was shaped by different combinations of topographic, land-cover, and drainage-related controls, while Nepal showed a more consistent influence of topographic and glacier-related factors across zones. These findings show that EF and LF provide complementary predictive behavior, and that their spatially adaptive integration through MoE yields robust overall predictive performance for FL-MHSM while supporting interpretable characterization of multi-hazard susceptibility in spatially heterogeneous landscapes.

AI.tldr: A deep learning workflow for joint flood-landslide susceptibility mapping using spatial partitioning, Early Fusion, Late Fusion, and Mixture of Experts models, showing improved performance in heterogeneous landscapes.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-hazard susceptibility mapping studies have limitations: they rely on spatially uniform models, treat hazards independently, provide limited representation of cross-hazard dependence, and offer inadequate uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Deep learning workflow combining two-level spatial partitioning, probabilistic Early Fusion (EF), tree-based Late Fusion (LF) baseline, and soft-gating Mixture of Experts (MoE) model with MoE as final predictive model.
