Uses zonal partitions to preserve spatial heterogeneity and overlapping lattice grids for data-parallel large-area prediction.
AI.result: In Kerala: EF improved flood recall from 0.816 to 0.840 and reduced Brier score from 0.092 to 0.086. MoE achieved best flood performance: AUC-ROC 0.905, recall 0.930, F1-score 0.722. In Nepal: EF improved flood recall from 0.820 to 0.858 and reduced Brier score from 0.057 to 0.049. MoE outperformed for landslide susceptibility: AUC-ROC 0.914, recall 0.901, F1-score 0.559. GeoDetector analysis revealed zone-dependent factor variations.
AI.conclusion: EF and LF provide complementary predictive behavior, and their spatially adaptive integration through MoE yields robust overall predictive performance for flood-landslide multi-hazard susceptibility mapping while supporting interpretable characterization of multi-hazard susceptibility in spatially heterogeneous landscapes.

title: Evaluating the Progression of Large Language Model Capabilities for Small-Molecule Drug Design
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have the potential to accelerate small molecule drug design due to their ability to reason about information from diverse sources and formats. However, their practical utility remains unclear due to the lack of benchmarks that reflect real-world scenarios. In this work, we introduce a suite of chemically-grounded tasks spanning molecular property prediction, molecular representation transformations, and molecular design. Importantly, we formulate these tasks as reinforcement learning (RL) environments, enabling a unified approach for evaluation and post-training. Across three model families, we find that frontier models are increasingly proficient at chemical tasks, but that there is significant room for improvement, especially in experimental settings with low data. Critically, we show that RL-based post-training can substantially improve performance. A smaller model post-trained on our environments becomes competitive with state-of-the-art frontier models, despite a significantly weaker base model. This suggests a practical route toward employing LLMs in drug discovery; by combining carefully-designed evaluation tasks with targeted post-training, we can both elucidate and close critical capability gaps.

AI.tldr: LLMs show promise for drug design but need better benchmarks. New chemically-grounded RL environment suite evaluates models on molecular tasks. RL post-training significantly boosts smaller models to compete with frontier models.
AI.motivation: LLMs have potential to accelerate small molecule drug design due to multimodal reasoning capabilities, but practical utility remains unclear due to lack of benchmarks reflecting real-world drug discovery scenarios.
AI.method: Introduces a suite of chemically-grounded tasks (molecular property prediction, representation transformations, molecular design) formulated as RL environments for unified evaluation and post-training. Evaluates three model families and uses RL-based post-training to improve performance.
AI.result: Frontier models show increasing proficiency at chemical tasks but significant room for improvement, especially in low-data experimental settings. RL-based post-training substantially improves performance - a smaller post-trained model becomes competitive with state-of-the-art frontier models despite weaker base capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Carefully-designed evaluation tasks combined with targeted RL post-training can both elucidate and close critical capability gaps, providing a practical route toward employing LLMs in real-world drug discovery applications.

title: Geometric regularization of autoencoders via observed stochastic dynamics
summary: Stochastic dynamical systems with slow or metastable behavior evolve, on long time scales, on an unknown low-dimensional manifold in high-dimensional ambient space. Building a reduced simulator from short-burst ambient ensembles is a long-standing problem: local-chart methods like ATLAS suffer from exponential landmark scaling and per-step reprojection, while autoencoder alternatives leave tangent-bundle geometry poorly constrained, and the errors propagate into the learned drift and diffusion. We observe that the ambient covariance~$Λ$ already encodes coordinate-invariant tangent-space information, its range spanning the tangent bundle. Using this, we construct a tangent-bundle penalty and an inverse-consistency penalty for a three-stage pipeline (chart learning, latent drift, latent diffusion) that learns a single nonlinear chart and the latent SDE. The penalties induce a function-space metric, the $ρ$-metric, strictly weaker than the Sobolev $H^1$ norm yet achieving the same chart-quality generalization rate up to logarithmic factors. For the drift, we derive an encoder-pullback target via Itô's formula on the learned encoder and prove a bias decomposition showing the standard decoder-side formula carries systematic error for any imperfect chart. Under a $W^{2,\infty}$ chart-convergence assumption, chart-level error propagates controllably to weak convergence of the ambient dynamics and to convergence of radial mean first-passage times. Experiments on four surfaces embedded in up to $201$ ambient dimensions reduce radial MFPT error by $50$--$70\%$ under rotation dynamics and achieve the lowest inter-well MFPT error on most surface--transition pairs under metastable Müller--Brown Langevin dynamics, while reducing end-to-end ambient coefficient errors by up to an order of magnitude relative to an unregularized autoencoder.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a novel method for learning reduced simulators of stochastic dynamical systems with slow/metastable behavior by leveraging ambient covariance structure to constrain tangent-bundle geometry, achieving significant improvements in mean first-passage time accuracy and ambient coefficient errors.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for building reduced simulators from short-burst ambient ensembles have limitations: local-chart methods (like ATLAS) suffer from exponential landmark scaling and per-step reprojection, while autoencoder alternatives leave tangent-bundle geometry poorly constrained, causing errors to propagate into learned drift and diffusion terms.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline (chart learning, latent drift, latent diffusion) using a tangent-bundle penalty and inverse-consistency penalty. Key innovations: 1) Observes that ambient covariance Λ encodes coordinate-invariant tangent-space information, 2) Constructs penalties that induce a ρ-metric (strictly weaker than Sobolev H¹ norm), 3) Derives encoder-pullback target via Itô's formula, 4) Proves bias decomposition showing decoder-side formulas carry systematic error for imperfect charts.
AI.result: Achieves same chart-quality generalization rate as Sobolev H¹ norm up to logarithmic factors. Under W^{2,∞} chart-convergence assumption, chart-level error propagates controllably to weak convergence of ambient dynamics and radial mean first-passage times. Experiments: 50-70% reduction in radial MFPT error under rotation dynamics; lowest inter-well MFPT error on most surface-transition pairs under metastable Müller-Brown Langevin dynamics; up to order-of-magnitude reduction in end-to-end ambient coefficient errors compared to unregularized autoencoder.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively leverages ambient covariance structure to constrain tangent-bundle geometry in learned simulators, addressing fundamental limitations of existing approaches and achieving substantial improvements in accuracy for modeling slow/metastable stochastic dynamical systems in high-dimensional spaces.
